2.7: A direct revelation: 'You are my Son; today I have begotten you.' The royal Psalm 2 reaches its Christological fullness in the resurrection. Paul at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:33): God raised Jesus, 'as it is written in the second Psalm, You are my Son, today I have begotten you.' The 'today' of divine fatherhood is not the eternal generation alone but the specific day of the resurrection-enthronement, when the Son's identity is publicly declared. Every application of Ps 2:7 in the NT (baptism, transfiguration, resurrection) marks a moment when the Father publicly declares the Son's identity for the community to hear.
2.1: A direct fulfillment: 'why do the nations rage, and the peoples plot in vain?' Acts 4:25-26 quotes this verse explicitly as fulfilled in the conspiracy against Jesus: the apostles pray, 'Sovereign Lord... you spoke by the Holy Spirit through the mouth of our father David: why did the Gentiles rage, and the peoples plot in vain?' The nations and peoples of Ps 2 are identified as Herod, Pontius Pilate, the Gentiles, and the people of Israel — all gathered against Jesus (Acts 4:27). The rage of the nations is the crucifixion.
2.2: A direct fulfillment: 'the kings of the earth set themselves, and the rulers take counsel together, against the LORD and against his Anointed' — meshicho (his Anointed, his Messiah). The Davidic king of Ps 2 is the Anointed One by title and function; the NT reads this title as referring ultimately to Jesus (Acts 4:26: 'against the Lord and against his Christ'). The coalition that crucified Jesus — Roman power, Herodian client king, Jewish religious leadership — is the fulfillment of the multi-party conspiracy Ps 2 describes.
2.3: A revelation of God: 'let us burst their bonds apart and cast away their cords from us' — the rulers' ambition to free themselves from YHWH's authority. The crucifixion was humanity's definitive attempt to throw off divine governance by killing the divine Son. The claim 'we have no king but Caesar' (John 19:15) is the explicit assertion of this rejection. Yet the attempt to break the cords of covenant becomes the very means by which Christ binds those who trust him (Matt 11:29-30: 'my yoke is easy').
2.4: A revelation of God: 'he who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord holds them in derision.' The divine laughter at the conspiracy of the powerful is not cruelty but the perception of futility: the plot to overthrow the Creator's Son cannot succeed. The resurrection vindicates the divine laughter — the cross that the conspirators thought final was the precise instrument of their own defeat (1 Cor 2:8: 'if they had understood, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory').
2.5: A revelation of God: 'then he will speak to them in his wrath and terrify them in his fury.' The divine speech in judgment contrasts with the nations' conspiring speech. The final judgment that Ps 2 sets in the future is what Christ's return inaugurates (Rev 19:11-16): the one who was judged by the nations comes as judge. The wrath that Ps 2 announces is what Christ absorbs at the cross (Rom 5:9; 1 Thess 1:10) so that those in him are not subject to it.
2.6: A direct connection: 'as for me, I have set my King on Zion, my holy hill.' The divine installation of the Davidic king on Zion is what the NT interprets as Christ's resurrection and ascension. Acts 2:36 (Peter at Pentecost): 'God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified.' The installation of YHWH's King on the holy hill is not Solomon's coronation but the resurrection enthronement of Jesus at the Father's right hand (Ps 110:1; Acts 2:33-34).
2.8: A direct connection: 'ask of me, and I will make the nations your heritage, and the ends of the earth your possession.' The Father's promise to the Son in Ps 2:8 is what Jesus claims in the Great Commission (Matt 28:18-20: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go therefore and make disciples of all nations'). The nations as the Son's inheritance is the ground of the universal mission: the Father has already given them; the mission gathers what belongs to the Son.
2.9: A direct connection: 'you shall break them with a rod of iron and dash them in pieces like a potter's vessel.' Rev 2:27, 12:5, and 19:15 all apply this verse to Christ. In Rev 19:15 the rider on the white horse (Christ) 'will rule the nations with a rod of iron.' The iron scepter is both the authority of the enthroned Christ and the instrument of eschatological judgment on all who refuse the Son.
2.10: A theme: 'now therefore, O kings, be wise; be warned, O rulers of the earth.' The summons to the kings anticipates the universal call of the gospel: all rulers, all power-holders, are called to submit to the Son. The 'wisdom' (haskilah) that kings need is the wisdom that acknowledges Christ's lordship — the fear of the Lord that Ps 1:6 and Ps 111:10 describe as wisdom's beginning.
2.11: A theme: 'serve the LORD with fear, and rejoice with trembling.' The paradox of trembling-joy — fear and gladness combined — is the posture that Christ's cross and resurrection create in those who receive him. The NT calls it 'reverent fear' (Phil 2:12: 'work out your salvation with fear and trembling') precisely because the grace that saves is also the holiness that terrifies. The resurrection-joy that comes with acknowledging the Son includes the trembling of those who understand what was at stake.
2.12: A direct connection: 'kiss the Son, lest he be angry, and you perish in the way' — 'blessed are all who take refuge in him.' The 'kiss' as covenant submission and loyalty: in the ANE, kissing the feet of the conquering king was the act of submission. The warning and the beatitude together frame the gospel's structure: the Son is both the one who judges and the one in whom refuge is found. The last word of Ps 2 (ashre, blessed) echoes the first word of Ps 1 (ashre, blessed): the entire framework of the blessed life is defined by relation to the Son.

22.1: A fulfillment: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The Passion Psalm that Jesus cited from the cross is both a historical lament (David in extremity) and a Christological prophecy (the Son's abandonment in the atonement). The cry 'why have you forsaken me' is not a failure of faith but a genuine experience of divine absence — the moment when Christ bore the full weight of human sin-under-judgment. The Psalm's movement (dereliction → trust → vindication → universal proclamation) is the death-and-resurrection narrative in miniature. Jesus did not die in despair; the Psalm he cited ends in the world's worship of YHWH through the one who suffered.
22.2: A direct connection: 'O my God, I cry by day, but you do not answer, and by night, but I find no rest.' The sustained divine silence that follows the cry of dereliction (v1, already present) is what Jesus experienced for the full duration of the crucifixion — from the sixth hour to the ninth (Matt 27:45-46). The 'by day' and 'by night' suggest a duration that exceeds a moment: the forsakenness was not instantaneous but prolonged. The one who had never known divine silence (John 11:41-42: 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me') entered it fully at Golgotha.
22.3: A revelation of God: 'yet you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.' The suffering psalmist affirms the divine holiness even in the midst of forsakenness. The theological precision here is profound: it is precisely because YHWH is holy that the forsakenness occurs. 'He made him to be sin who knew no sin' (2 Cor 5:21) — the holy God cannot face sin without judgment; Christ bearing the world's sin faced the full force of that holiness. The holiness that caused the forsakenness is the same holiness that vindicates the resurrection.
22.4: A revelation of God: 'in you our fathers trusted; they trusted, and you delivered them.' The contrast that makes the psalm's suffering so acute: ancestors who trusted were delivered; the Son who trusts is not (yet) delivered. The patriarchs and Israel through the wilderness — all trusted and were saved. Jesus on the cross is the one for whom the pattern is temporarily reversed, precisely to secure the deliverance of all who come after. The deliverances of the fathers were types and pledges of the one deliverance that requires the non-deliverance of the Son.
22.5: A revelation of God: 'to you they cried and were saved; in you they trusted and were not put to shame.' The covenantal expectation: crying → salvation; trusting → not put to shame. Jesus fulfills the negative of this pattern temporarily at the cross (the trusting one is shamed publicly) in order to fulfill it absolutely in the resurrection. The shame of the cross becomes the ground for 'whoever believes in him will not be put to shame' (Rom 9:33; 10:11, quoting Isa 28:16).
22.6: A direct connection: 'but I am a worm and not a man, scorned by mankind and despised by the people.' The self-description of ultimate humiliation — 'a worm' (tolaat) — anticipates Phil 2:7-8: Christ 'emptied himself, taking the form of a servant... humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross.' The scorned and despised one of Ps 22:6 is identified in Isa 53:3 ('despised and rejected by men') as the Suffering Servant — and in the NT as Jesus crucified (Mark 15:31-32: the chief priests and scribes 'mocked him').
22.7: A direct connection: 'all who see me mock me; they make mouths at me; they wag their heads.' Matt 27:39 cites this verse as fulfilled at the crucifixion: 'those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads and saying...' The physical gesture of head-wagging is not coincidental — the Gospel narrator recognizes it as the fulfillment of the specific gesture Ps 22:7 describes. The entire crucifixion scene in Matthew is narrated through the lens of Ps 22.
22.8: A direct connection: 'He trusts in the LORD; let him deliver him; let him rescue him, for he delights in him!' Matt 27:43 quotes this verse verbatim: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him. For he said, I am the Son of God.' The mockers at the cross unwittingly speak the script of Ps 22:8, enacting the prophecy while intending only to taunt. The citation proves the fulfillment is not coincidental — the psalm was composed for this moment.
22.9: A direct connection: 'yet you are he who took me from the womb; you made me trust you at my mother's breasts.' The birth-trust established by YHWH from the beginning. Applied to Christ: the incarnation itself is the divine act of 'taking from the womb' — Luke 2:7, 11-12: 'she gave birth to her firstborn son... today in the city of David a Savior has been born to you, who is Christ the Lord.' The infant Jesus is the one YHWH brought from the womb for the specific mission of Ps 22.
22.10: A direct connection: 'on you was I cast from my birth, and from my mother's womb you have been my God.' The divine claim on Christ from his very entrance into human life. Luke 1:35: 'the Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the child to be born will be called holy — the Son of God.' The total God-belonging of Ps 22:10 is expressed in the annunciation: the one born is God's Son, cast upon God from the womb by divine design.
22.11: A shadow: 'be not far from me, for trouble is near, and there is none to help.' The petition for divine nearness in the moment of absolute isolation. At Gethsemane: 'Could you not watch with me one hour?' (Matt 26:40) — the disciples failed to stay near. At the cross: 'they all left him and fled' (Matt 26:56). The aloneness of Ps 22:11 — no human help — was the condition of the crucifixion. The 'none to help' is precisely the situation that required Christ to be the only helper: he who had no human help becomes the helper of all.
22.12: A shadow: 'many bulls encompass me; strong bulls of Bashan surround me.' Bashan — the fertile highland region whose bulls were the largest and most powerful in the ancient world (Amos 4:1: 'cows of Bashan'). The powerful enemies surrounding the psalmist become, in Christ's passion, the combined forces of religious, political, and spiritual opposition — the Sanhedrin, the Roman soldiers, and the demonic powers all converging on Golgotha.
22.13: A shadow: 'they open wide their mouths at me, like a ravening and roaring lion.' The lion metaphor for the enemies who seek to devour — Peter uses this image for the devil himself (1 Pet 5:8: 'your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour'). At the cross, the 'roaring lion' was unleashed against the Son of God. The resurrection is Christ's defeat of the lion: Heb 2:14 ('through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil').
22.14: A direct connection: 'I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint; my heart is like wax; it is melted within my breast.' The physical description of crucifixion: dehydration ('poured out like water'), joint dislocation from the position on the cross, and cardiac trauma ('heart like wax, melted'). Modern medical analysis of crucifixion confirms the cardiac rupture that John 19:34 may describe: 'one of the soldiers pierced his side with a spear, and at once there came out blood and water' — the water from the pericardial sac consistent with cardiac rupture from the Ps 22:14 description.
22.15: A direct connection: 'my strength is dried up like a potsherd; and my tongue sticks to my jaws; and you lay me in the dust of death.' The extreme thirst ('my tongue sticks to my jaws') directly anticipates John 19:28: 'After this, Jesus, knowing that all was now finished, said (to fulfill the Scripture), I thirst.' The word 'to fulfill the Scripture' in John 19:28 most likely refers to Ps 22:15 (or Ps 69:21: 'for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink'). The 'dust of death' is the grave into which YHWH 'lays' him — the burial that precedes the resurrection.
22.16: A direct connection: 'for dogs encompass me; a company of evildoers encircles me; they have pierced my hands and my feet.' 'Pierced my hands and my feet' — the crucifixion's specific physical detail, written a millennium before crucifixion was invented as a form of execution. John 20:25,27: Thomas demands to see 'the mark of the nails' in Jesus's hands. John 19:37 quotes Zech 12:10 ('they will look on him whom they have pierced') but the primary fulfillment of 'pierced hands and feet' is Ps 22:16. The early church read Ps 22:16 as predictive specifically of the nailing to the cross.
22.17: A direct connection: 'I can count all my bones — they stare and gloat over me.' 'I can count all my bones' — the emaciation or exposure of the body on the cross, where every rib and joint is visible. The 'staring and gloating' of the crowd at the crucifixion: 'the people stood by, watching' (Luke 23:35) while the rulers scoffed. The public spectacle of the cross — designed by Rome for maximum humiliation and visibility — fulfills the 'stare and gloat' of Ps 22:17.
22.18: A direct connection: 'they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' John 19:23-24 cites this verse explicitly as fulfilled: 'the soldiers divided his garments among the four of them. They also took his tunic, but it was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom, so they said to one another, Let us not tear it, but cast lots for it to see whose it shall be. This was to fulfill the Scripture which says, They divided my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' One of the most precisely documented fulfillments in all the Gospels.
22.19: A shadow: 'but you, O LORD, do not be far off! O you my help, come quickly to my aid!' The renewed petition from the depths — the turn from lament to petition. The resurrection is the answer to this cry: YHWH did not stay 'far off' permanently. The speed of the divine response ('come quickly') was three days — not instantaneous, but not forever. The confidence beneath the petition is that YHWH will act; the psalm does not end in forsakenness.
22.20: A shadow: 'deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of the dog!' The 'sword' and the 'dog' — the instruments of violent death. Christ's soul was delivered from the sword through the resurrection: the 'power of death' (Heb 2:14) over which the devil presided was broken when God raised him. The 'precious life' (yechidati, my only one) corresponds to the 'only-begotten Son' (monogenes, John 3:16) — the uniquely precious one whose life was laid down and restored.
22.21: A shadow: 'save me from the mouth of the lion! You have rescued me from the horns of the wild oxen!' The answer to the petition arrives — 'you have rescued me' shifts the psalm to confident retrospect. This is the pivot of Ps 22: from the depths of forsakenness to the assurance of rescue. Applied to Christ: the resurrection is the moment of 'you have rescued me.' The lion's mouth and the wild oxen's horns (the forces of death and evil unleashed at the cross) are defeated by the divine rescue of the third day.
22.22: A direct connection: 'I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you.' Heb 2:12 quotes this verse explicitly as words spoken by the risen Christ: 'he is not ashamed to call them brothers, saying, I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise.' The post-resurrection community is the congregation where the risen Christ praises the Father through his people. The 'congregation' of Ps 22:22 becomes the church — those who hear the name proclaimed by the risen one.
22.23: A theme: 'you who fear the LORD, praise him! All you offspring of Jacob, glorify him, and stand in awe of him, all you offspring of Israel!' The summons to the covenant community following the rescue — the resurrection community called to praise. The 'offspring of Jacob' and 'offspring of Israel' who praise the rescued one become, in the NT's universal reading, all who belong to the true Israel — those grafted in through faith in Christ (Rom 11:17-24; Gal 3:29: 'if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's offspring').
22.24: A revelation of God: 'for he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him but has listened to his cry for help.' The theological climax of Ps 22: YHWH did not ultimately despise the suffering or hide his face permanently. The resurrection is the proof that the 'hiding of the face' at Golgotha was temporary — the Father heard the cry, and the answer was the empty tomb. The one who cried 'why have you forsaken me?' was heard, and the resurrection was the answer.
22.25: A theme: 'from you comes my praise in the great assembly; my vows I will perform before those who fear him.' The thanksgiving vow performed in the assembly — the todah (thanksgiving sacrifice) following deliverance. Christ's resurrection appearances in the midst of his disciples are the beginning of the 'great assembly' praise. The Eucharist (the Christian todah) is the ongoing performance of the vow: 'do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19) — the risen one's thanksgiving meal continued in his body.
22.26: A theme: 'the afflicted shall eat and be satisfied; those who seek him shall praise the LORD! May your hearts live forever!' 'The afflicted shall eat and be satisfied' — the covenant meal after the affliction: anticipating the resurrection appearances where Jesus ate with his disciples (Luke 24:30-31,41-43; John 21:9-13). The eschatological banquet that the afflicted share after the rescue of Ps 22 is the gospel feast, and ultimately the Marriage Supper of the Lamb (Rev 19:9).
22.27: A theme: 'all the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to the LORD, and all the families of the nations shall worship before you.' The universal missionary vision arising from the individual's rescue: the nations turning to YHWH because of what happened to the anointed sufferer. Paul's entire missionary theology is grounded here: the crucified and risen Christ is the reason 'all the families of the nations' have reason to worship. The Great Commission (Matt 28:18-20) is the activation of this vision.
22.28: A revelation of God: 'for kingship belongs to the LORD, and he rules over the nations.' The universal kingship of YHWH — declared as the ground of the nations' turning (v27). In the NT, this kingship is exercised through the risen Christ: 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matt 28:18). The nations turn to YHWH (v27) because YHWH rules through his Son — the kingship of Ps 22:28 is the inheritance of Ps 2:8.
22.29: A theme: 'all the prosperous of the earth eat and worship; before him shall bow all who go down to the dust, even the one who could not keep himself alive.' 'All who go down to the dust' — the dead worship YHWH. This is among the earliest OT hints of resurrection worship: even those who have died bow before YHWH. Phil 2:10-11 ('at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth') makes this universal: the dead as well as the living bow before the risen Christ.
22.30: A theme: 'posterity shall serve him; it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation.' The intergenerational transmission of the rescue-story — 'it shall be told.' The church's proclamation of the gospel (evangelism) is precisely this: telling the coming generation what YHWH did for his anointed one. The Great Commission establishes the mechanism: 'teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you' (Matt 28:20). The story of Ps 22 is the story that 'shall be told' to every generation.
22.31: A direct connection: 'they shall come and proclaim his righteousness to a people yet to be born, that he has done it.' 'That he has done it' — ki asah — corresponds to the great 'It is finished' (tetelestai, John 19:30): the Greek perfect tense of completed-with-ongoing-results. The proclamation of Ps 22:31 ('he has done it!') is the gospel: the work of rescue is complete. The people 'yet to be born' who will hear this proclamation are the Gentile nations — those who come to faith in every generation since the first Easter.

110.1: A direct revelation: 'The LORD says to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool.' The most foundational Christological oracle in the OT: the risen and ascended Christ is enthroned at the Father's right hand (the supreme position of divine authority) awaiting the final subjection of all enemies, including death (1 Cor 15:25-26). The present era is the era of Christ's enthronement: he reigns now, his enemies are being progressively placed under his feet, and the ultimate subjection is certain. Ps 110:1 frames the entire NT's understanding of the ascension and the present Christological situation of the cosmos.
110.4: A fulfillment: 'You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.' The divine oath of the eternal priesthood is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection, which established him as the priest who never dies. Unlike the Levitical priests who needed to offer sacrifice repeatedly and who were replaced by death, Christ offered himself once and lives forever in the power of an indestructible life (Heb 7:16, 24-25). The Melchizedekian priesthood — prior to Aaron, not from the tribe of Levi, without recorded end — is the OT's own signal that the Levitical system was provisional. Christ's eternal intercession ('he always lives to make intercession for them', Heb 7:25) is the fulfillment of Ps 110:4's oath.
110.2: direct The LORD sends forth your mighty scepter from Zion. Rule in the midst of your enemies! The ruling scepter sent from Zion — Christ's current reign from his heavenly enthronement. Acts 2:33-36: God has made Jesus both Lord and Christ, seated at his right hand. All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matt 28:18). He rules now, in the midst of enemies, through the church's proclamation and the Spirit's power — the mission of Matt 28:19 is the exercise of this scepter.
110.3: shadow Your people will offer themselves freely on the day of your power, in holy garments; from the womb of the morning, the dew of your youth will be like dew. Freely offered, holy-garmented people on the day of power — the church gathered by the Spirit at Pentecost. God is at work in you, both to will and to work for his good pleasure (Phil 2:13). Holy garments: clothed with Christ (Gal 3:27). The dew of youth: the inexhaustible vitality of Christ's eternal priesthood, the endless spring of the new covenant people.
110.5: shadow The Lord is at your right hand; he will shatter kings on the day of his wrath. Shattering kings on the day of wrath — Rev 19:11-16: the rider on the white horse, with a name written King of kings and Lord of lords, strikes down the nations. The day of Christ's wrath is the day of his return; the kings who resisted his rule face the scepter that shatters (Ps 2:9: you shall break them with a rod of iron, and dash them in pieces like a potter's vessel).
110.6: shadow He will execute judgment among the nations, filling them with corpses; he will shatter chiefs over the wide earth. Judgment executed among the nations — the final judgment of Rev 20:11-15 and Rev 19:17-21. Matt 25:31-46: all nations gathered before the Son of Man; he separates the sheep from the goats, executing judgment across the wide earth. The one who came first in humility comes again in the fullness of this authority.
110.7: shadow He will drink from the brook by the way; therefore he will lift up his head. Drinking from the brook and lifting up the head — the humiliation and exaltation of Christ. The warrior-king pauses to drink (condescension into mortality); then lifts his head in victory. Christ took the cup of suffering (Matt 26:42: let this cup not pass from me), drank it fully on the cross, and was exalted: Therefore God has highly exalted him (Phil 2:9). Heb 12:2: who for the joy set before him endured the cross... and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God.

1.1: A theme: the blessed man who refuses the counsel of the wicked, does not stand in the way of sinners, and does not sit in the seat of scoffers describes a life that no fallen human lives perfectly — yet the opening beatitude points toward the one who did. Jesus alone walked through the world without ever taking counsel from the wicked or sitting among scoffers. Where Ps 1 sets the standard, Jesus embodies it: the perfectly Torah-formed human whose delight is unbroken (Matt 5:17: 'I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it').
1.2: A theme: 'his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night' — the portrait of the perfect covenant person who never ceases from Torah-meditation. Jesus is the one who went into the wilderness, enduring each temptation by citing Scripture (Matt 4:1-11); who debated the Pharisees from within the law's depth rather than against it; who declared that not one jot or tittle of the Torah would pass away until all was fulfilled (Matt 5:18). The meditative life of Ps 1 reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who himself is the Word that the Torah mediates.
1.3: A shadow: 'he is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season.' The tree planted by water as the image of the covenant-loyal person anticipates the NT's vine imagery: Jesus as the true vine (John 15:1-8) who bears fruit and enables others to bear fruit only through abiding connection. The stability of the planted tree — unmoved by drought — foreshadows the one whose root system in the Father's love was unshaken by crucifixion itself.
1.4: A revelation of God: 'the wicked are not so, but are like chaff that the wind drives away' — the instability of all that is not rooted in YHWH. This contrast establishes the eschatological principle that governs the entire Psalter: what is grounded in the covenant endures; what is not is dispersed. The NT develops this as the judgment of the Son of Man who separates wheat from chaff (Matt 3:12; 13:30) — the eschatological harvest Ps 1 announces.
1.5: A theme: 'therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous.' The judgment-separation that Ps 1 describes as future reality is what Christ's coming makes both urgent and certain. He is the one appointed to judge the living and the dead (Acts 17:31); the 'standing in the congregation of the righteous' is granted only to those who are clothed in his righteousness (Rom 3:22; Phil 3:9). The psalm's judgment anticipates the criterion: relation to Christ himself.
1.6: A revelation of God: 'for the LORD knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.' The divine knowing (yodea) — the intimate covenant knowledge — is what secures the righteous. Jesus develops this as the shepherd who 'knows' his sheep (John 10:14-15: 'I know my own and my own know me') and whose knowledge is mutual. Those the Father has given to the Son will not perish (John 10:28-29): the covenant knowing of Ps 1:6 finds its full expression in the Son's reciprocal knowing of the Father and of those who belong to him.

3.1: A theme: the superscription connects Ps 3 to David fleeing Absalom — a son's rebellion driving out the father. This historical situation becomes a type of the deeper exile Christ entered: the one who came to his own and was not received (John 1:11), who was handed over by those he came to save. The many enemies of Ps 3 who say 'there is no salvation for him in God' (v2) anticipate the mockers at the cross ('He trusts in God; let God deliver him now,' Matt 27:43).
3.2: A theme: 'many are saying of my soul, there is no salvation for him in God.' The mocking of the sufferer's trust in YHWH is the exact taunting at the crucifixion (Matt 27:43: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him'). The psalm provides the script that the mockers at Golgotha follow unknowingly — and which the resurrection answers definitively. There was salvation for him in God, precisely because he bore the weight of those who had none.
3.3: A revelation of God: 'but you, O LORD, are a shield about me, my glory, and the lifter of my head.' The divine protection that David confesses in extremity is what Christ claimed in the Garden of Gethsemane: 'Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will' (Matt 26:39). The Father who is the shield of his anointed did not ultimately abandon the Son — the resurrection is the lifting of the head after the crucifixion's shame.
3.4: A revelation of God: 'I cried aloud to the LORD, and he answered me from his holy hill.' The confident prayer answered from Zion — the divine responsiveness to the royal sufferer. The hill of Zion from which YHWH answers anticipates the resurrection-proclamation from Jerusalem: YHWH answered the crucified Son from the 'holy hill' by raising him on the third day (Acts 2:24).
3.5: A shadow: 'I lay down and slept; I woke again, for the LORD sustained me.' The sleep-and-waking of the endangered person becomes a type of death and resurrection when applied to Christ. Jesus quoted similar language of trust in the Father — and slept peacefully in the storm-tossed boat (Mark 4:38) as one who trusted the Father's sustaining power over death itself. The resurrection is the ultimate waking that YHWH's sustaining makes possible.
3.6: A revelation of God: 'I will not be afraid of many thousands of people who have set themselves against me all around.' The fearlessness of the anointed sufferer surrounded by enemies — Christ in Gethsemane, at the arrest, at the trial, on the cross — surrounded by 'many thousands' who sought his destruction, and yet trusting absolutely in the Father's rescue. The steadiness that Ps 3:6 describes is what the Gospel narratives portray as Jesus's unshaken calm before Pilate and on the cross.
3.7: A theme: 'arise, O LORD! Save me, O my God! For you strike all my enemies on the cheek; you break the teeth of the wicked.' The petition for divine arising echoes the resurrection-prayer: 'arise' is the language of YHWH's redemptive action throughout the Psalter and prophets. The striking of enemies on the cheek is ironic when applied to Christ — he was the one struck on the cheek (John 18:22; Matt 5:39: 'if anyone slaps you on the right cheek, turn to him the other also'). The one who absorbed the blow becomes, in resurrection, the one before whom all enemies are struck down.
3.8: A theme: 'salvation belongs to the LORD; your blessing be on your people!' The psalm's closing declaration — salvation (yeshu'ah) is YHWH's possession and gift — is the theological affirmation that the name Jesus embodies (Matt 1:21: 'you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins'). Salvation belongs to YHWH — and YHWH has given it a human name and face in the Son.

4.1: A revelation of God: 'answer me when I call, O God of my righteousness! You have given me relief when I was in distress. Be gracious to me and hear my prayer!' The evening cry to God — the God of the psalmist's righteousness (Elohei tzidqi) — reveals the divine character as the one who gives relief in distress. Christ, the perfectly righteous one, makes this cry his own in Gethsemane and on the cross ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' Ps 22:1) and by entering that distress opens the relief to those who have no righteousness of their own.
4.2: A theme: 'O men, how long shall my honor be turned into shame? How long will you love vain words and seek after lies?' The psalmist's honor turned to shame — the reversal that the righteous experience in a world that honors vanity. Christ's honor was supremely turned to shame at the crucifixion (the purple robe of mockery, the crown of thorns, the placard) — and the resurrection reversed the reversal: 'God has highly exalted him' (Phil 2:9).
4.3: A theme: 'know that the LORD has set apart the godly for himself; the LORD hears when I call to him.' The divine setting-apart of the chasid (godly/loyal one) prefigures Christ's unique setting-apart: the holy one of God whom demons recognized (Mark 1:24), anointed and set apart for the specific mission of bearing the world's sin. The Father's hearing of the Son's prayers (John 11:41-42: 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me') is the covenant assurance of Ps 4:3 realized perfectly.
4.4: A revelation of God: 'be angry, and do not sin; ponder in your own hearts on your beds, and be silent.' Paul quotes this verse in Eph 4:26 as guidance for Christian anger in the community. The revelation here is of a God who acknowledges righteous anger while demanding its discipline — a character fully expressed in Christ who 'drove out those who sold and those who bought' (Mark 11:15) in righteous anger while never sinning. Anger and holiness coexist in Christ as in no other.
4.5: A theme: 'offer right sacrifices, and put your trust in the LORD.' The right sacrifices (zivchei tzedek) — the covenant worship that is both externally correct and internally aligned with trust. Christ both fulfills the sacrificial system (Heb 10:5-10: 'a body you have prepared for me') and abolishes the need for further offering by being himself the right sacrifice. The trust (bitchu) in YHWH is simultaneously the posture Ps 4 calls for and what the sacrifice of Christ enables — access to trust the Father.
4.6: A theme: 'there are many who say, Who will show us some good? Lift up the light of your face upon us, O LORD!' The longing for the face of YHWH to shine — the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26) — is the universal human longing for divine favor. Christ is the answer to this prayer: 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' (2 Cor 4:6). The divine face the world longed to see became visible in the incarnation.
4.7: A revelation of God: 'you have put more joy in my heart than they have when their grain and wine abound.' The covenant joy that surpasses material prosperity — the joy that Christ imparts is specifically this kind: 'that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full' (John 15:11). The abundance of grain and wine (the covenant blessings of earth) yields to the joy of divine presence. The Last Supper itself holds the tension: earthly bread and wine become the sign of the greater covenant joy.
4.8: A theme: 'in peace I will both lie down and sleep; for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety.' The peace that enables sleep — Christ who 'slept' in the boat (Mark 4:38) with absolute peace amid the storm is the model of this trust. The deeper application: the peace that Christ gives is 'not as the world gives' (John 14:27) — it holds even in the face of crucifixion. And his lying down in death became the sleep from which YHWH alone could raise him, making him dwell in ultimate safety.

5.1: A revelation of God: 'give ear to my words, O LORD; consider my groaning.' The morning prayer of the sufferer — the one who cries out at dawn and waits. Christ's prayers in the Gospels are consistently depicted as early and earnest (Mark 1:35: 'rising very early in the morning, while it was still dark, he departed and went to a desolate place, and there he prayed'). The groaning (haggigi) of Ps 5 anticipates the Spirit's groaning in intercession (Rom 8:26) that the Son's work makes possible.
5.2: A revelation of God: 'give attention to the sound of my cry, my King and my God, for to you do I pray.' The address 'my King and my God' — the double royal-divine title — is the posture of covenant prayer. Thomas uses the same double title in his resurrection confession: 'My Lord and my God!' (John 20:28). The prayer of Ps 5:2 addressed to the King and God becomes, in the NT, the worshipful confession addressed to the risen Christ himself.
5.3: A revelation of God: 'O LORD, in the morning you hear my voice; in the morning I prepare a sacrifice for you and watch.' The morning orientation of prayer — the daily sacrifice accompanied by prayer. The morning resurrection: 'early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark' (John 20:1) the women came to the tomb. The morning of the resurrection is the ultimate morning when YHWH heard and answered — the divine hearing of the Son's morning cry vindicated by raising him.
5.4: A revelation of God: 'for you are not a God who delights in wickedness; evil may not dwell with you.' The divine holiness that cannot coexist with evil is the theological background for the atonement. Christ, who 'knew no sin' (2 Cor 5:21), was 'made sin' precisely because the holy God cannot receive sinners as they are. The cross is the event where the divine non-delight in wickedness and the divine love for sinners are simultaneously honored — by transferring the wickedness to the Holy One.
5.5: A revelation of God: 'the boastful shall not stand before your eyes; you hate all evildoers.' The radical divine opposition to evil — 'you hate all evildoers.' This is not incompatible with divine love for sinners: YHWH hates the sin and, in Christ, judges it fully so that the sinner may be freed from it. The cross is where YHWH's hatred of evil and his love for those in evil's grip are both fully expressed — the evil condemned in the flesh (Rom 8:3) so that the person may go free.
5.6: A revelation of God: 'you destroy those who speak lies; the LORD abhors the bloodthirsty and deceitful man.' The divine judgment on the liar and the violent one — what Ps 5:6 announces as YHWH's character becomes, in the NT, the criterion of judgment. The 'blood' language anticipates the blood that speaks better things than Abel's (Heb 12:24) — Christ's blood that speaks not for judgment but for forgiveness.
5.7: A theme: 'but I, through the abundance of your steadfast love, will enter your house.' The ground of access: not merit but chesed (steadfast love). This is the gospel principle before the gospel is announced: the worshiper approaches not on the basis of his own standing but on the basis of YHWH's chesed. Eph 2:8 ('by grace you have been saved through faith') and Heb 10:19-22 (approaching the throne through the blood of Jesus) are the NT fulfillment of this principle.
5.8: A theme: 'lead me, O LORD, in your righteousness because of my enemies; make your way straight before me.' The prayer for divine leading in righteousness — 'your righteousness' (tzidqatecha) — not the worshiper's own. Paul's entire argument in Romans (1:17; 3:21-22) is that the righteousness of God, disclosed in the gospel, is now the ground of the believer's standing: the prayer of Ps 5:8 answered in Christ.
5.9: A direct connection: Paul quotes v9 in Rom 3:13 — 'their throat is an open grave' — as part of the indictment of universal human sinfulness. In Paul's argument, the psalm's description of the wicked is applied to all humanity, Jew and Gentile alike, to show that all stand under condemnation and all need the righteousness of God through faith in Christ (Rom 3:19-22). The wicked man of Ps 5 becomes, in Paul's reading, every man — showing the radical necessity of Christ's righteousness.
5.10: A theme: 'make them bear their guilt, O God; let them fall by their own counsels.' The imprecatory prayer — that the wicked's own schemes return on their heads. The cross is the supreme instance of this irony: those who plotted against the Son of God ensured through their plotting the very salvation they were trying to prevent. The 'counsels' that destroyed Jesus were the precise instrument by which YHWH defeated sin and death (Acts 2:23: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God').
5.11: A theme: 'but let all who take refuge in you rejoice; let them ever sing for joy, and spread your protection over them, that those who love your name may exult in you.' The joy of those who take refuge in YHWH — this is the community of Christ: those who have taken refuge specifically in the Son, in whom all the promises of YHWH are 'Yes and Amen' (2 Cor 1:20). The name they love is now embodied in Jesus: 'the name that is above every name' (Phil 2:9).
5.12: A theme: 'for you bless the righteous, O LORD; you cover him with favor as with a shield.' The divine blessing and covering of the righteous — the righteousness that qualifies for blessing is now 'not my own righteousness, which comes from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God that depends on faith' (Phil 3:9). The shield of favor that Ps 5 promises is extended to those who are 'in Christ' — the righteous one who covers them with his own standing.

6.1: A shadow: Ps 6 is the first of the seven penitential psalms (6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143). It opens with the cry not to be rebuked in divine anger — the fear of divine wrath that the covenant people rightly felt under the law's condemnation. Christ absorbs this rebuking: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Gal 3:13). The rebuking anger that Ps 6 fears is the anger Christ bore, so that those who trust him receive instead the blessing.
6.2: A shadow: 'be gracious to me, O LORD, for I am languishing; heal me, O LORD, for my bones are troubled.' The embodied suffering of the penitent — bones troubled, soul terrified. Christ entered this embodied vulnerability fully: in Gethsemane, 'his sweat became like great drops of blood falling down to the ground' (Luke 22:44); on the cross, the Johannine account notes that not a bone was broken (John 19:36, fulfilling Ps 34:20) — yet he bore the full spiritual anguish of the troubled soul.
6.3: A shadow: 'my soul also is greatly troubled. But you, O LORD — how long?' The 'how long?' of the penitent's cry — the central question of lament. At Golgotha, 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Ps 22:1) is the ultimate 'how long?' The silence of God that the penitent experiences is what Christ entered to its absolute depth, so that the believer who prays 'how long?' does so as one who knows that YHWH has already answered in the resurrection.
6.4: A shadow: 'turn, O LORD, deliver my soul; save me for the sake of your steadfast love.' The petition grounded in chesed — save not because of my merit but because of your covenant love. This is the ground of all atonement: YHWH saves 'for the sake of your steadfast love' (lema'an chasdecha). Christ's atoning work is itself the supreme expression of divine chesed (John 3:16: 'for God so loved the world that he gave...') — the covenant-love given its fullest form in the cross.
6.5: A shadow: 'for in death there is no remembrance of you; in Sheol who will give you praise?' The OT concern that death silences praise. Christ's descent into death (1 Pet 3:18-19; Eph 4:9) and his resurrection out of it is the answer to this fear: he went where praise was impossible and returned, making the resurrection the foundation of eternal praise. Heb 2:12 quotes Ps 22:22 ('I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise') as what the risen Christ does — praise has been restored from the dead.
6.6: A shadow: 'I am weary with my moaning; every night I flood my bed with tears; I drench my couch with my weeping.' The depths of the penitent's grief — the physical expression of spiritual distress. Jesus wept (John 11:35) — the Son of God entering the full range of human grief. Heb 5:7 describes Jesus in Gethsemane: 'he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death.' The weeping of Ps 6 finds its fullest expression and its final answer in Christ's tears and resurrection.
6.7: A shadow: 'my eye wastes away because of grief; it grows weak because of all my foes.' The physical dimming through grief — the body bearing the weight of spiritual distress. The 'foes' who cause this grief are, in Christ's case, the systemic powers of sin, death, and the devil that he entered combat with. His 'weakness' at the cross (2 Cor 13:4: 'he was crucified in weakness') is the entry point for divine power: 'he was raised by the power of God.'
6.8: A theme: 'depart from me, all you workers of evil, for the LORD has heard the sound of my weeping.' The confident reversal — the psalmist's assurance that YHWH has heard. Jesus uses the language of this verse ('depart from me') in Matt 7:23, addressed to those who called him Lord but did not know him, and in Matt 25:41, addressed to those who failed to serve him in the poor. The 'depart from me' of Ps 6:8 becomes the eschatological judgment-word of the Son.
6.9: A theme: 'the LORD has heard my plea; the LORD accepts my prayer.' The confident assurance of answered prayer — what the penitent gains from entering the suffering of Ps 6. Christ's prayers were always heard by the Father (John 11:42). And through Christ, the prayers of those who come to God 'through him' are accepted (Heb 7:25: 'he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them').
6.10: A theme: 'all my enemies shall be ashamed and greatly troubled; they shall turn back and be put to shame in a moment.' The final reversal — the shame that the penitent feared (v1: fear of divine rebuke) is redirected to the enemies. The resurrection is the moment when the shame is redirected: those who killed the Son are confronted with the one they crucified (Acts 2:36: 'let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified'). The 'in a moment' of the reversal anticipates the sudden transformation of the third day.

7.1: A shadow: 'O LORD my God, in you do I take refuge; save me from all my pursuers and deliver me.' The fugitive's cry for divine protection. The superscription connects to Cush the Benjaminite — a false accusation episode in David's life. As a shadow: Jesus alone was pursued by accusers whose charges were entirely false (Matt 26:59-60: 'the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus'). The innocent fugitive who takes refuge in YHWH is the template Christ inhabits at his trial.
7.2: A shadow: 'lest like a lion they tear my soul apart, rending it in pieces, with none to deliver.' The lion tearing as the image of enemy destruction. Peter uses the same image for the devil: 'your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (1 Pet 5:8). At the cross, the lion was unleashed — and the one torn was Christ, so that those who take refuge in him are not devoured.
7.3: A shadow: 'O LORD my God, if I have done this, if there is wrong in my hands' — the protestation of innocence. Jesus alone could make this claim before YHWH with absolute truthfulness. Pilate's declaration 'I find no guilt in him' (John 18:38) is the judicial echo of the psalmist's claim. The one who bore the world's guilt was the one who had committed none of his own — the substitution requires this precise innocence.
7.4: A shadow: 'if I have repaid my friend with evil or plundered my enemy without cause...' — the negative covenant oath. Christ's covenant faithfulness was total: he never repaid evil with evil (1 Pet 2:23: 'when he was reviled, he did not revile in return') and never plundered unjustly. The perfection of his human covenant-keeping is what makes him the acceptable substitute for those who have broken every covenant term.
7.5: A shadow: 'let the enemy pursue my soul and overtake it, and let him trample my life to the ground and lay my glory in the dust.' The ultimate curse-formula — what the psalmist says should happen if he is guilty. Christ voluntarily entered this curse for those who were guilty: 'he made him to be sin who knew no sin' (2 Cor 5:21). The glory laid in the dust is the incarnation and crucifixion; the resurrection is the divine vindication that proves the guilt was transferred, not inherent.
7.6: A revelation of God: 'arise, O LORD, in your anger; lift yourself up against the fury of my enemies; awake for me; you have appointed a judgment.' The petition for divine arising to judge — the 'arise, O LORD' cry echoes the Sinai prayer (Num 10:35). The divine judgment that Ps 7 calls for is what Christ's return inaugurates: the appointed judge (Acts 17:31) rises to render the verdict that history has been waiting for.
7.7: A revelation of God: 'let the assembly of the peoples be gathered about you; over it return on high.' The universal assembly gathered around YHWH — the eschatological vision: all peoples assembled before the divine throne. Rev 20:11-12 depicts this: 'a great white throne and him who was seated on it... and I saw the dead, great and small, standing before the throne.' The Judge of Ps 7 is the Christ of Rev 20.
7.8: A theme: 'the LORD judges the peoples; judge me, O LORD, according to my righteousness and according to the integrity that is in me.' The appeal to divine justice — which is also the appeal to divine mercy for those who have no righteousness of their own. Paul's argument in Romans is precisely that no one can say 'judge me according to my righteousness' — except the one who has Christ's righteousness imputed to them. The prayer of Ps 7:8 becomes safe only for those in whom Christ's integrity stands.
7.9: A revelation of God: 'oh, let the evil of the wicked come to an end, and may you establish the righteous — you who test minds and hearts, O righteous God!' The searching of minds and hearts by the righteous God — the divine omniscience applied to moral judgment. 'The heart is deceitful above all things... who can understand it? I the LORD search the heart and test the mind' (Jer 17:9-10). Jesus, who 'knew what was in man' (John 2:25), exercises this divine prerogative as the Son who knows the Father.
7.10: A theme: 'my shield is with God, who saves the upright in heart.' The divine shield again (cf. Gen 15:1; Ps 3:3) — YHWH as protector of the upright. Christ is both the shield and the one who makes the upright: 'the upright in heart' are those whose hearts have been transformed by the Spirit (Ezek 36:26; Ps 51:10) so that they may stand in the judgment Ps 7 describes.
7.11: A revelation of God: 'God is a righteous judge, and a God who feels indignation every day.' The perpetual divine indignation at injustice — YHWH does not grow accustomed to sin. This perpetual moral attentiveness is what makes the atonement necessary: every sin is registered by the one who 'feels indignation every day.' The cross is where this daily indignation is absorbed in full, so that those in Christ stand before the righteous judge without condemnation (Rom 8:1).
7.12: A revelation of God: 'if a man does not repent, God will whet his sword; he has bent and readied his bow.' The divine warning: the sword and bow of divine judgment are prepared for the unrepentant. The urgency of repentance — what the NT calls 'today' (Heb 3:7,13,15: 'today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts') is the urgency of Ps 7:12. The prepared sword is not yet swung; the repentance that turns it is available now through Christ.
7.13: A revelation of God: 'he has prepared for him his deadly weapons, making his arrows fiery shafts.' The divine warrior's arsenal — the weapons of the holy war are prepared specifically for the unrepentant. Yet Christ takes the fire: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Gal 3:13) — the fiery shaft of divine judgment struck the Son so that it need not strike those who take refuge in him.
7.14: A theme: 'behold, the wicked man conceives evil and is pregnant with mischief and gives birth to lies.' The wicked person's internal trajectory — evil conceived, born as mischief, delivered as lies. James 1:15 describes sin's similar trajectory: 'desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.' Christ enters the world precisely where this trajectory has led: a world full of the death that sin-conceived-in-mischief produces.
7.15: A shadow: 'he makes a pit, digging it out, and falls into the hole he has made.' The self-trapping principle of evil — the pit-digger falls into his own pit (Prov 26:27; Eccl 10:8). Applied to the crucifixion: those who plotted to destroy Jesus through death were unwittingly using the instrument of their own defeat. The resurrection is the moment when the enemy falls into the pit: death is defeated by death (Heb 2:14: 'through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil').
7.16: A shadow: 'his mischief returns upon his own head, and on his own skull his violence descends.' The law of return: violence against the innocent returns on the violent. The cross applies this: YHWH allowed the violence of crucifixion to fall on the innocent Son — and it returned on all who hold the power of sin and death through the resurrection. Satan's greatest weapon (death) became his own defeat.
7.17: A revelation of God: 'I will give to the LORD the thanks due to his righteousness, and I will sing praise to the name of the LORD, the Most High.' The psalm ends in doxology — as most penitential-vindication psalms do. The pattern (crisis → lament → confidence → praise) is the structure of the gospel itself: the crisis of human sin → the lament of the cross → the confidence of the resurrection → the eternal praise of those who are vindicated in Christ. The 'name of the LORD, the Most High' is the name now borne by Jesus: 'the name that is above every name' (Phil 2:9).

8.1: A direct connection: 'O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth! You have set your glory above the heavens.' Ps 8 is the psalm the NT most explicitly applies to Christ. The majestic name 'in all the earth' anticipates the name 'above every name' (Phil 2:9-10). The glory set above the heavens is what the Son shared with the Father before the world existed (John 17:5: 'Father, glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed') and what the incarnation temporarily veiled.
8.2: A direct connection: Jesus quotes this verse in Matt 21:16 in response to the Pharisees' objection to the children crying 'Hosanna!' in the temple. 'Have you never read, Out of the mouth of infants and nursing babies you have prepared praise?' The children's Hosannas in the temple are the fulfillment of Ps 8:2 — the praise of the weak silences the enemy. Christ's claim that the children's praise fulfills Ps 8 means he identifies himself as the one whose name elicits this praise.
8.3: A revelation of God: 'when I look at your heavens, the work of your fingers, the moon and the stars, which you have set in place' — the created order as the work of divine fingers. The creator who set the stars in place (Ps 147:4: 'he determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of them their names') became flesh in the incarnation. John 1:3: 'all things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made.' The creator entered his creation as the creature the next verses describe.
8.4: A direct connection: Heb 2:6-9 quotes this verse as applying to Christ: 'it has been testified somewhere, What is man, that you are mindful of him, or the son of man, that you care for him?' The 'son of man' (ben adam) of Ps 8:4 is applied in Hebrews to Jesus, who 'for a little while was made lower than the angels' (= the incarnation and humiliation of Ps 8:5a), then 'crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death' (= the resurrection and exaltation of Ps 8:5b). Ps 8:4 is the question the incarnation answers.
8.5: A direct connection: Heb 2:7,9 applies both halves of this verse to Christ. 'You made him for a little while lower than the angels' = the incarnation, in which the Son who is 'far superior to angels' (Heb 1:4) nevertheless 'was made lower than the angels' through taking on flesh and mortality. 'You have crowned him with glory and honor' = the resurrection and ascension: 'we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death' (Heb 2:9).
8.6: A direct connection: Paul quotes 'you have put all things under his feet' in 1 Cor 15:27 and the same language appears in Eph 1:22 ('he put all things under his feet and gave him as head over all things to the church') and Heb 2:8 ('leaving nothing outside his control'). The subjection of 'all things' under the son of man is fully realized in Christ's resurrection and reign. 1 Cor 15:24-28 clarifies: the subjection is not yet fully visible — Christ reigns now and will reign until all enemies including death itself are put under his feet.
8.7: A theme: 'all sheep and oxen, and also the beasts of the field' — the animals under human (and thus Christ's) dominion. The dominion mandate of Gen 1:28 ('have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing') is what Ps 8 celebrates and what Christ's resurrection-kingship restores. The groaning creation (Rom 8:19-22) awaits the full revelation of the children of God — when the dominion of Ps 8 is restored through the one who became the son of man to reclaim it.
8.8: A theme: 'the birds of the heavens, and the fish of the sea, whatever passes along the paths of the seas.' The completeness of the creation-dominion — birds, fish, sea-paths. Christ's mastery over creation anticipates the restored dominion: he commands fish (Luke 5:4-7; John 21:6), calms seas (Mark 4:39), walks on water (Matt 14:25). These are not magic tricks but demonstrations that the son of man of Ps 8 has come — the one in whom the creation-dominion of Adam is restored and surpassed.
8.9: A direct connection: 'O LORD, our Lord, how majestic is your name in all the earth!' The psalm closes as it opened — with the majestic name. In the NT's reading of Ps 8, this final doxology is also the first: the name that is majestic 'in all the earth' is the name of Jesus, before whom 'every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father' (Phil 2:10-11). The earthly majestic name of Ps 8 becomes the cosmic-universal name of the crucified and risen Son.

9.1: The vow to praise with the whole heart anticipates the perfect worshiper. Every human act of thanksgiving is shadowed by a heart that is divided — part devoted, part distracted. Christ alone gave thanks to the Father with a perfectly undivided heart (John 11:41-42; Matt 11:25-26; John 17:4). His praise was the first perfectly whole offering of human devotion, and his people are drawn into it through union with him (Eph 5:19-20; Col 3:17).
9.2: "I will be glad and rejoice in you; I will sing the praises of your name, O Most High" — the joy in YHWH that marks the redeemed heart. Jesus expressed this joy most fully in John 15:11: "I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and that your joy may be complete." The Messianic community inherits the joy of the anointed Son — his rejoicing in the Father becomes the pattern for theirs.
9.3: The enemies turning back and stumbling before YHWH anticipates the defeat of all opposition to Christ. At the empty tomb, the guards "trembled and became like dead men" (Matt 28:4). At the name of Jesus, every knee will bow and every tongue confess (Phil 2:10-11). The scattering of enemies — which the psalmist prays — is accomplished definitively at the resurrection.
9.4: "For you have upheld my right and my cause, sitting enthroned as the righteous judge" — Christ is the one whose cause was perfectly upheld by the Father in the resurrection. The raising of Jesus was the Father's verdict: the cross was injustice; the empty tomb is the divine acquittal of the righteous sufferer (Rom 4:25 — "he was raised for our justification"). The Father who sits as righteous judge vindicated his Son, and in his Son vindicates all who are united to him.
9.5: The blotting out of the names of the wicked from the divine record echoes the book-of-life theology (Rev 3:5; 20:15). Christ is both the Lamb in whose book the names of the redeemed are written (Rev 13:8; 21:27) and the judge before whom the books are opened (Rev 20:12). That he "blots out their name forever" points toward the final judgment where every claim to self-justification apart from Christ is permanently erased.
9.6: The complete ruin of the enemy — cities destroyed, memory blotted out — is the language of final judgment. The NT applies this eschatological pattern to the defeat of Satan and the powers (1 John 3:8; Heb 2:14; Rev 20:10). Christ's cross was the decisive blow against the enemy kingdom; the final judgment extends that defeat to its ultimate completion.
9.7: "But the LORD reigns forever; he has established his throne for judgment" — Christ the enthroned King-Judge. Jesus declared "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me" (Matt 28:18) after the resurrection. The Father committed all judgment to the Son (John 5:22: "the Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son"). The eternal throne of YHWH is the throne on which the risen Christ is seated (Rev 3:21; 22:1).
9.8: "He rules the world in righteousness and judges the peoples with equity" — Christ's judicial reign is characterized by perfect righteousness and equity. Acts 17:31: "God has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The resurrection-as-credential for universal judgment: the world will be judged by the one who was himself judged unjustly and vindicated by God.
9.9: "The LORD is a refuge for the oppressed, a stronghold in times of trouble" — Christ as the ultimate refuge. Heb 6:18: "we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us" — the imagery of fleeing to a city of refuge (Num 35) applied to taking hold of Christ. Jesus himself is the "city of refuge" — those who flee to him for sanctuary are safe from the avenger of blood, which is the law's condemnation (Rom 8:1: "no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus").
9.10: "Those who know your name trust in you, for you, LORD, have never forsaken those who seek you" — the promise of divine faithfulness to those who seek. Christ embodies this promise — he is the one through whom YHWH is known by name (John 17:6: "I have revealed you to those whom you gave me out of the world"; John 17:26: "I have made you known to them"). To know the Father's name through the Son is the foundation of unshakeable trust.
9.11: "Sing the praises of the LORD, enthroned in Zion; proclaim among the nations what he has done" — the missionary dimension of Messianic praise. The proclamation "among the nations" anticipates the Great Commission (Matt 28:19-20) and the Pentecost mission (Acts 2:5-11). Christ is "enthroned in Zion" — the heavenly Zion (Heb 12:22-24) from which the gospel goes out to all nations. The risen Lord's acts are the content of the proclamation.
9.12: "For he who avenges blood remembers; he does not ignore the cries of the afflicted" — Christ is the avenger of blood for his people (Rev 6:10-11 — the martyrs cry "how long?" and are told to wait until the full number is complete; Rev 19:2 — he has avenged the blood of his servants). The divine memory of every cry of the afflicted reaches its fullness in Christ who both suffered as the afflicted one and will avenge all the afflicted at his return.
9.13: "LORD, see how my enemies persecute me! Have mercy and lift me up from the gates of death" — the prayer for rescue from death's threshold. Christ himself descended to the uttermost depth — "the gates of death" (Matt 12:40; Acts 2:24 — "it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him"). The prayer for rescue from the gates of death was answered in the resurrection: the gate opened from the inside, and the one who prayed was lifted up to life.
9.14: "...that I may declare your praises in the gates of Daughter Zion, and there rejoice in your salvation" — Heb 2:12 quotes Ps 22:22: "I will declare your name to my brothers and sisters; in the assembly I will sing your praises" — the risen Christ praising the Father in the midst of the church. The movement of v. 14 anticipates this: the prayer to be lifted from death's gates leads directly to proclamation in Zion's gates. Rescue becomes testimony; testimony becomes communal praise.
9.15: "The nations have fallen into the pit they have dug; their feet are caught in the net they have hidden" — the self-destructive principle of sin, realized most fully at the cross. Those who plotted against Christ — Judas, the chief priests, Pilate — were each caught in their own nets: Judas by guilt and self-destruction; the chief priests by the fall of the temple they sought to protect (70 CE); Rome by its own imperial overreach. The cross that was intended to destroy Christ became the instrument of salvation; the stone the builders rejected became the cornerstone (Ps 118:22; Acts 4:11).
9.16: "The LORD is known by his acts of justice; the wicked are ensnared by the work of their hands" — the divine revelation through historical action climaxes in the cross and resurrection. God's justice — his righteous judgment of sin — was manifested at Golgotha (Rom 3:25-26: "God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... to demonstrate his justice... so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus"). The work of the wicked hands that crucified Christ became the very act of redemption.
9.17: "The wicked go down to the realm of the dead, all the nations that forget God" — the ultimate descent into death contrasts with Christ's descent-and-ascent. Christ went down to the realm of the dead (Acts 2:31; 1 Pet 3:19) not as one judged but as the conqueror who holds "the keys of death and Hades" (Rev 1:18). The nations that forget God face this descent without rescue; the nations that remember him through Christ face death as a passage, not a destination (1 Cor 15:54-57).
9.18: "But God will never forget the needy; the hope of the afflicted will never perish" — the beatitude theology of Christ. The Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12) are the direct continuation of this promise: "blessed are the poor in spirit," "blessed are the meek," "blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness." Christ announced himself as the fulfillment of this promise to the afflicted (Luke 4:18 — "the Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor"). The needy are never forgotten because the Son of God himself became needy (2 Cor 8:9).
9.19: "Arise, LORD, do not let mortals triumph; let the nations be judged in your presence" — the prayer for divine intervention answered definitively in the resurrection. "Arise (qûmāh)" is the prayer-equivalent of the Num 10:35 battle cry ("Rise up, LORD!") and the language of resurrection (qûm = to rise, stand up). The resurrection is God's definitive "arising" to judge the nations — the final demonstration that mortals do not have the final word (Acts 17:31).
9.20: "Strike them with terror, LORD; let the nations know they are only mortal" — the goal of divine judgment: that all human pride before God be ended. Paul echoes this in Rom 3:19-20: the purpose of the law is that "every mouth may be silenced and the whole world held accountable to God." Christ as judge accomplishes what the law declared: the finitude and fallibility of every human claim to self-sufficiency. Before the risen Christ, all mortal pretension collapses.

10.1: "Why, LORD, do you stand far off? Why do you hide yourself in times of trouble?" — the cry of divine absence that reaches its culmination in Christ's cross. Ps 22:1 is the canonical expression: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" — which Jesus cried in Matt 27:46 and Mark 15:34. Ps 10:1 belongs to the same family of desolation prayers. Christ inhabited the depth of this question — not merely citing it but living it, experiencing the actual absence of divine consolation so that the abandonment of sin could be fully borne and exhausted.
10.2: The wicked pursuing the poor in arrogance describes the spiritual dynamic that opposed Christ. His entire ministry was characterized by the powerful targeting the powerless — and targeting him for his alignment with them. The religious establishment pursued Jesus for associating with tax collectors and sinners (Luke 5:30), and ultimately pursued him to the cross (John 11:47-53). In being hunted by the arrogant, Christ identified himself with every oppressed person throughout history.
10.3: "The wicked man boasts about the cravings of his heart; he blesses the greedy and reviles the LORD" — the idolatry of self-exaltation. Paul's analysis in Rom 1:21-23 identifies this as the root human sin: "they neither glorified him as God nor gave thanks to him... their thinking became futile." Christ exposed this pattern most directly in his confrontations with the Pharisees (Matt 23) and in the parables of self-exaltation (Luke 18:9-14 — the Pharisee who prayed about his own virtues).
10.4: "In his pride the wicked man does not seek him; in all his thoughts there is no room for God" — the practical atheism of self-sufficiency. Jesus diagnosed this in the parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21): the man whose plans had no room for God. The incarnation is God's response to a world with no room for him — he entered it anyway, born into a borrowed stable (Luke 2:7). The rejection of Christ is the definitive form of this verse: in the climactic moment of divine presence, there was "no room."
10.5: "His ways are always prosperous; your laws are rejected by him; he sneers at all his enemies" — the apparent prosperity of the wicked that deceives. Jesus addressed this in the parable of the rich man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19-31): visible prosperity and suffering are not reliable indicators of divine favor. The cross is the ultimate inversion: the one who appeared cursed (Gal 3:13 — "cursed is everyone who is hung on a pole") was in fact carrying the blessing of the world.
10.6: "He says to himself, 'Nothing will ever shake me.' He swears, 'No one will ever do me harm'" — the false security of self-confidence. Christ warned against this repeatedly: "But God said to him, 'You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you'" (Luke 12:20). The one who built on rock (Christ's words) would stand; the one who built on sand (self-sufficiency) would fall (Matt 7:24-27). The whole security of the believer is in Christ, not self.
10.7: "His mouth is full of lies and threats; trouble and evil are under his tongue" — James 3:6-8 develops the theological significance of the corrupt tongue: "the tongue is a fire, a world of evil... it corrupts the whole body." Christ diagnosed the tongue's evil as a symptom of a corrupt heart (Matt 15:18: "the things that come out of a person's mouth come from the heart"). Paul quotes a cluster of passages including Ps 10:7 in Rom 3:14 as part of his diagnosis of universal human corruption requiring Christ's righteousness.
10.8: The wicked lying in wait to murder the innocent — this pattern culminated in the cross. Ps 10:8-10 pictures an ambush: hidden waiting, then sudden attack on the unsuspecting. The betrayal of Jesus by Judas (Matt 26:14-16, 47-50) and the night-time arrest in Gethsemane follow exactly this pattern — the chief priests and teachers waited for "an opportunity" (Luke 22:6), then struck in the night. Christ walked into the ambush knowingly, laying down his life rather than having it taken (John 10:18).
10.9: "He lies in wait like a lion in cover; he lies in wait to catch the helpless" — the lion-imagery for the enemy connects to 1 Pet 5:8 ("your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour") and Rev 5:5 (Christ as "the Lion of the tribe of Judah" who has triumphed). The evil lion who hunts the helpless is overcome by the Lion who gave himself to be hunted, so that through his willing surrender he defeated the predator (Heb 2:14-15: "by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death").
10.10: "His victims are crushed, they collapse; they fall under his strength" — the condition of those crushed by sin and its consequences, which Christ specifically came to address. Luke 4:18 (Isa 61:1): "He has sent me to proclaim freedom for the prisoners and recovery of sight for the blind, to set the oppressed free." Christ's entire ministry was oriented toward those who had been crushed by the enemy — the demon-possessed, the disease-ridden, the social outcasts, the spiritually broken.
10.11: "He says to himself, 'God will never notice; he covers his face and never sees'" — the denial of divine omniscience that sin requires. Christ exposed this illusion consistently: "Nothing is hidden that will not be disclosed" (Luke 8:17); "your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you" (Matt 6:4, 6, 18). The cross made visible what was hidden — it publicly displayed "the rulers and authorities" (Col 2:15) and their character, "triumphing over them by the cross." What sin tries to conceal, the cross unmasks.
10.12: "Arise, LORD! Lift up your hand, O God. Do not forget the helpless" — the resurrection prayer at the center of the psalm. As in Ps 9:19-20, the petition for divine arising reaches its answer in the resurrection of Christ. God "lifted up his hand" against sin and death in raising Christ from the dead (Eph 1:19-20: "the incomparably great power... which he exerted when he raised Christ from the dead"). The helpless — those who cannot save themselves — are the primary beneficiaries of this arising.
10.13: "Why does the wicked man revile God? Why does he say to himself, 'He won't call me to account'?" — the fundamental failure of accountability before God that the cross addresses. Christ's death was the definitive calling-to-account: all sin was held accountable at Calvary (2 Cor 5:21 — "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us"). The resurrection is the announcement that the account has been settled and the one who bore accountability for sin has been vindicated.
10.14: "But you, God, see the trouble of the afflicted; you consider their grief and take it in hand" — YHWH as the one who takes in hand the cause of the afflicted. The incarnation is the supreme taking-in-hand: "In all their distress he too was distressed... he lifted them up and carried them all the days of old" (Isa 63:9). Christ taking human suffering "in hand" — literally, physically, in his own body (Isa 53:4: "Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering"; 1 Pet 2:24) — is the fulfillment of this verse.
10.15: "Break the arm of the wicked man; call the evildoer to account for his wickedness that would not otherwise be found out" — the prayer for the defeat of the enemy's power. Christ "broke the arm" of the wicked through the cross (1 John 3:8: "The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work"; Col 2:15: "having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross"). The enemy's power is broken; its complete accounting awaits the final judgment (Rev 20:10).
10.16: "The LORD is King forever and ever; the nations will perish from his land" — Christ's eternal kingship declared. Rev 11:15: "The kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever." The nations that opposed Christ's kingdom will be purged from the renewed creation — not through ethnic exclusion but through the exclusion of rebellion itself. The new earth will have no room for the opposition described in Ps 10:1-15.
10.17: "You, LORD, hear the desire of the afflicted; you encourage them, and you listen to their cry" — Christ's ministry precisely exemplified this divine listening. He stopped for blind Bartimaeus when the crowd tried to silence him (Mark 10:46-52). He stopped for the hemorrhaging woman who touched his garment in a crowd (Mark 5:25-34). He heard the one leper who returned to give thanks (Luke 17:11-19). The Son's attentiveness to the afflicted was the visible, embodied form of this verse.
10.18: "...defending the fatherless and the oppressed, so that mere earthly mortals will never again strike terror" — the eschatological promise: the protection of the vulnerable, and the end of terror from human oppressors. This finds its fulfillment in the new creation (Rev 21:4: "there will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain") and its first-fruits in Christ's kingdom where the excluded are welcomed (Matt 19:14 — "let the children come to me"; Gal 3:28 — "there is neither slave nor free, neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus").

11.1: "In the LORD I take refuge. How then can you say to me: 'Flee like a bird to your mountain'?" — Christ as the refuge that makes flight unnecessary. Heb 6:18: those who have "fled to take hold of the hope set before us" — the imagery of fleeing to a city of refuge applied to the safety found in Christ. When crisis comes, the temptation is flight; Christ's answer to his disciples before Gethsemane (John 14:27 — "Do not let your hearts be troubled and do not be afraid") is the same confidence as this verse: the refuge is secure, flight unnecessary.
11.2: "For look, the wicked bend their bows; they set their arrows against the strings to shoot from the shadows at the upright in heart" — the hidden attacks of the enemy against Christ and his people. The shadow-attack typifies the Passion: the plotting in secret (Matt 26:3-4 — "the chief priests and elders... plotted to arrest Jesus secretly"), the betrayal by one of his own (John 13:21-30).
