The divine desire for inner truth — the integrity of tûmāh (truth/sincerity) in the hidden depths (ṭûhôt) of the person — is what Christ identifies as the heart of the commandment (Matt 5:8: "blessed are the pure in heart, for they will see God"; Matt 5:28: inward desire = the act). John 4:23-24 ("God is spirit, and his worshipers must worship in the Spirit and in truth") develops the "inner truth" of v6 into the pneumatological reality that Christ enables. The wisdom taught "in the hidden place" is the wisdom of the Spirit: 1 Cor 2:10 ("the Spirit searches all things, even the deep things of God").
51.7: A shadow: "Cleanse me with hyssop, and I will be clean; wash me, and I will be whiter than snow." Hyssop (ēzôv) is the ritual instrument of purification in the Mosaic law — used to apply blood in the Passover (Exod 12:22), in the cleansing of skin disease (Lev 14:4, 6, 51), and in the red heifer purification (Num 19:6, 18). At the crucifixion, hyssop is the instrument used to offer Jesus the sour wine (John 19:29). The hyssop of Psalm 51:7 — the instrument of ritual cleansing — becomes at Golgotha the instrument through which the ultimate cleansing sacrifice is administered. The ritual shadow of purification-by-hyssop is enacted in reverse at the cross.
51.8: A theme: "Let me hear the sounds of joy and gladness; let the bones you have crushed be glad." The crushed bones as the somatic image of guilt under divine judgment — and the hope for their restoration to joy — is a pattern the resurrection fulfills. Hebrews 12:11: "no discipline seems pleasant at the time, but painful; later on, however, it produces a harvest of righteousness and peace for those who have been trained by it." The bones crushed by divine discipline are healed not by the removal of the discipline but by its completion in Christ's suffering (1 Pet 4:1: "since Christ suffered in his body, arm yourselves with the same attitude").
51.9: A theme: "Look away from my sins; wipe out all my guilt." The plea for divine non-seeing of sin — the averting of the divine face from the record of transgression — is what atonement accomplishes. Hebrews 10:17 (citing Jer 31:34): "their sins and lawless acts I will remember no more." The mechanism of this divine non-seeing is Christ's sacrifice: "God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... He did this to demonstrate his righteousness, because in his forbearance he had left the sins committed beforehand unpunished" (Rom 3:25). The wiping out of guilt in Ps 51:9 is the "blotting out" (Col 2:14: "having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness... he took it away, nailing it to the cross").
51.10: A direct anticipation: "Create a pure heart in me, O God, and renew a steadfast spirit within me." The verb bārā' (create) is used here — the same word used only for divine creative action in the OT (Gen 1:1, 21, 27; 2:3, 4; Isa 40:26, 28; 45:12, 18). David is not asking for moral improvement but for a new creation. 2 Corinthians 5:17: "if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come: the old has gone, the new is here!" John 3:3-8 (born again from above by the Spirit) and Ezekiel 36:26 ("I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit in you") are the Christological and pneumatological fulfillments of this petition. The new creation of v10 is what Christ enables through the Spirit.
51.11: A direct anticipation: "Do not banish me from your presence; do not take your Holy Spirit from me." The fear of the loss of the Holy Spirit — ruah qodshekha (your Holy Spirit) — reflects the OT's conditional pneumatology: the Spirit could be withdrawn from Saul (1 Sam 16:14). Christ's promise in John 14:16-17 is the definitive answer to this fear: "I will ask the Father, and he will give you another advocate to help you and be with you forever — the Spirit of truth." The Spirit given through Christ is not taken away; the fear of Psalm 51:11 is met by the permanence of the Spirit's indwelling in the new covenant community.
51.12: A theme: "Give back to me the joy of your rescue, and sustain me with a generous, willing spirit." The "joy of rescue" — the gladness that belongs to the one who has experienced divine salvation — is what Christ restores to those who come to him. John 15:11: "I have told you this so that my joy may be in you and that your joy may be complete." The "willing spirit" (nādîvāh — generous, noble, freely-giving) that is asked as a sustaining gift is the Spirit that Christ pours out on those who believe (Acts 2:17-18: "I will pour out my Spirit on all people"). The rescue-joy and the willing spirit are both gifts of the new covenant through Christ.
51.13: A theme: "Then I will show other rebels your ways, and sinners will come back to you." The missional consequence of personal restoration — the forgiven one becoming the instrument of others' repentance — is the pattern of the apostolic testimony. Peter's restoration after his denials (John 21:15-19) leads directly to his Pentecost proclamation (Acts 2:14-41). Paul's pre-Christian violence against the church did not disqualify him; his testimony became a means of others' conversion (1 Tim 1:15-16). The "showing of rebels God's ways" in Ps 51:13 is the evangelistic fruit that genuine repentance and restoration produce.
51.14: A theme: "Rescue me from the guilt of bloodshed, O God — God of my salvation — and my tongue will ring out your righteousness." The plea for rescue from blood-guilt (dāmîm) — the most serious form of moral pollution in the OT (Num 35; 2 Sam 12) — points to the ultimate blood-guilt: the crucifixion itself, in which innocent blood was shed. Acts 5:28 (the Sanhedrin: "you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching and are determined to make us guilty of this man's blood") acknowledges the blood-guilt of the crucifixion. Christ's blood, paradoxically, "speaks a better word than the blood of Abel" (Heb 12:24) — not crying out for vengeance but for forgiveness of the very blood-guilt it creates.
51.15: A theme: "O Lord, open my lips, and my mouth will declare your praise." The request for divinely-opened lips — the acknowledgment that even praise requires divine enabling — is the posture of the NT community at prayer. Acts 2:4: "all of them were filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues as the Spirit enabled them." Ephesians 6:19: "pray also for me, that whenever I speak, words may be given me so that I will fearlessly make known the mystery of the gospel." The lips that God opens declare his praise — and the ultimate praise is the proclamation of Christ crucified and risen, which only the Spirit enables (1 Cor 12:3).
51.16: A direct anticipation: "You do not want sacrifice — otherwise I would bring it; you take no delight in burnt offerings." The prophetic principle that sacrifice is not YHWH's primary desire (cf. 1 Sam 15:22; Isa 1:11-13; Hos 6:6; Mic 6:6-8) is the OT critique of formalism that the NT fulfills. Hebrews 10:5-9 quotes Psalm 40:6-8 (LXX: "sacrifice and offering you did not desire... then I said, 'Here I am — it is written about me in the scroll — I have come to do your will, my God'") and applies it to Christ: "he sets aside the first [the sacrificial system] to establish the second [his obedience]." The sacrifice God does not want (animal offerings) gives way to the sacrifice he does: Christ's body.
51.17: A direct anticipation: "What God wants as sacrifice is a broken spirit; a broken and crushed heart, O God, you will not reject." The theology of the broken spirit as the acceptable sacrifice — replacing animal offerings with the moral and spiritual self — is fulfilled in Christ in two ways: (1) Christ himself was "broken" in the passion (Isa 53:10: "it pleased the LORD to crush him"), his spirit the ultimate broken offering; and (2) the community he forms is invited to bring broken hearts as worship. Romans 12:1: "offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God — this is your true and proper worship." The broken spirit that God will not reject is the heart of NT sacrificial theology.
51.18: A theme: "In your favor, do good to Zion; build up the walls of Jerusalem." The psalm's closing intercession for the restoration of Jerusalem — the communal vision after the individual's personal restoration — reflects the theology that the individual's righteousness is connected to the community's flourishing. In the NT, the "building up" that matters is the church: Ephesians 4:12 ("for the equipping of his people for works of service, so that the body of Christ may be built up") and 1 Peter 2:5 ("you also, like living stones, are being built into a spiritual house"). Christ builds his church (Matt 16:18), the new Jerusalem, as the restoration of what Israel's sin had broken.
51.19: A theme: "Then you will be pleased with right sacrifices, with burnt offerings and whole offerings; then bulls will go up on your altar." The closing verse restores sacrificial practice to its proper place — not replaced by repentance but re-grounded in it. This is not a contradiction of v16-17 but its fulfillment: sacrifice offered from a broken and contrite heart is the sacrifice God accepts. Hebrews 9:14: "how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from acts that lead to death, so that we may serve the living God!" The "right sacrifice" of Ps 51:19 is Christ's self-offering from a perfect, unbroken will — the one sacrifice that makes all subsequent offerings of praise and service acceptable.

52.1: A theme: "Why do you boast in evil, O mighty man? The steadfast love of God endures all day." The contrast of human wickedness and enduring divine hesed — the boastful evil of the "mighty man" set against the permanence of covenant love — is the psalm's structural irony. Romans 3:27: "where then is boasting? It is excluded." The boasting in human power that Psalm 52:1 ridicules is the same boasting that Paul says the cross eliminates: 1 Corinthians 1:29 ("so that no one may boast before him"). The "mighty man" who boasts in evil is the antithesis of Christ, who "being in very nature God, did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage" (Phil 2:6).
52.2: A theme: "Your tongue plots harm, sharp as a razor blade — you craftsman of deceit." The tongue as the weapon of the wicked — the razor-sharp, craft-worked deceit — is the failure that James 3:6 describes: "the tongue also is a fire, a world of evil among the parts of the body." The tongue of the "Doeg figure" (the informant who betrayed the priests of Nob, 1 Sam 22) is the tongue of all whose words destroy rather than build. Christ, by contrast, is the Word made flesh (John 1:14) — the divine speech whose every word gives life rather than death (John 6:63: "the words I have spoken to you — they are full of the Spirit and life").
52.3: A theme: "You love evil rather than good, and lies rather than truth." The moral inversion — the preference for evil over good, lies over truth — is what John 8:44 attributes to the devil: "he was a murderer from the beginning, not holding to the truth, for there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks his native language, for he is a liar and the father of lies." Those who follow the pattern of Doeg/Ps 52:3 participate in the devil's nature rather than Christ's: "I am the way and the truth and the life" (John 14:6). The love of lies over truth is the opposite of the one who is himself the truth.
52.4: A theme: "You love every word that destroys, you treacherous tongue." The destructive tongue — words as weapons rather than life-givers — is the pattern that Proverbs consistently contrasts with wisdom: "the mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence" (Prov 10:11). At Jesus's trial, false witnesses loved "words that destroy" (Mark 14:56-59: "many bore false witness against him — but their statements did not agree"). Christ endured the destroying words of false accusation without retaliation, "entrusting himself to him who judges justly" (1 Pet 2:23).
52.5: A theme: "But God will pull you down permanently; he will grab and rip you from your home; he will tear you out of the land of the living." The eschatological reversal of the wicked's apparent security — torn from the land of the living permanently — is the covenant consequence that the NT presents as eschatological judgment. Matthew 25:41 ("depart from me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels"); Revelation 20:15 ("anyone whose name was not found written in the book of life was thrown into the lake of fire"). The tearing down of the wicked in Psalm 52:5 is the eschatological judgment that Christ will execute as the enthroned judge (Acts 17:31).
52.6: A theme: "The righteous will see and be filled with awe, and they will laugh." The awe and laughter of the righteous at divine judgment — the vindication that the suffering righteous experience when divine justice is enacted — is the pattern of the eschatological reversal. Revelation 19:1-4 describes the heavenly multitude's worship at the fall of Babylon ("Hallelujah! The Lord our God, the Almighty, reigns"). Luke 6:21 (Jesus: "blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh") promises the laughter of eschatological vindication to those who mourn under the wicked's power. The righteous who fill with awe in Ps 52:6 are those whose faith has persisted through persecution.
52.7: A theme: "'Here is the man who would not take refuge in God, but relied on his great wealth and found his strength in destruction.'" The epitaph of the wicked — the negative inscription over a life built on the wrong foundation — is the warning of Jesus's parable of the two foundations (Matt 7:24-27). The man who builds on sand ("wealth and destruction") instead of the rock (Christ's words) comes to ruin when the storm comes. The "refuge in God" that the wicked of Ps 52 spurned is the refuge in Christ that the NT consistently identifies as the only stable foundation: "whoever trusts in him will never be put to shame" (Rom 10:11).
52.8: A theme: "But I am like a flourishing olive tree in the house of God; I trust in God's steadfast love forever and ever." The olive tree as the image of the righteous person rooted in YHWH's house — flourishing, fruitful, enduring — is the OT's horticultural image of covenant blessing (Ps 1:3; Jer 11:16). John 15:1-5: "I am the true vine, and my Father is the gardener... you are the branches. If you remain in me and I in you, you will bear much fruit." Christ is the true vine/tree in whom the believer is rooted; the flourishing olive tree of Ps 52:8 is the image fulfilled in those who abide in Christ, drawing life from his life.
52.9: A theme: "I will give you thanks forever, because of what you have done; I will hope in your name, for it is good, before your faithful people." The eternal thanksgiving grounded in divine action — "because of what you have done" — is the posture of the redeemed community. Revelation 11:17: "we give thanks to you, Lord God Almighty, the One who is and who was, because you have taken your great power and have begun to reign." The hope in the divine "name" that is "good" is the hope in the name of Jesus that is "above every name" (Phil 2:9-10) — the Name that is the ground of the eternal thanksgiving that Psalm 52:9 commits to.

53.1: A direct citation: "The fool has said in his heart, 'There is no God.' They are corrupt; they have done abominable deeds; there is no one who does good." Romans 3:10-12 quotes this verse (near-verbatim from LXX Ps 13/53, a near-duplicate of Ps 14) as part of the catena of OT texts proving universal human sinfulness: "There is no one righteous, not even one; there is no one who understands; there is no one who seeks God. All have turned away, they have together become worthless; there is no one who does good, not even one." The universal indictment of Psalm 53:1 is the theological precondition for Christ's universal atonement: "since all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, they are justified freely by his grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus" (Rom 3:23-24).
53.2: A revelation of God: "God looked down from heaven on all humanity to see if anyone understands, if anyone seeks after God." The divine omniscient survey of humanity — searching for understanding and God-seeking — and finding none is the universal negative that Paul's argument in Romans 1-3 establishes as the starting point of the gospel. The divine search of Ps 53:2 finds one: Christ himself, who is the only one who fully understands and fully seeks the Father (John 4:34: "my food is to do the will of him who sent me and to finish his work"). Christ is the exception to the universal finding of v2, and through union with him, the sinful are found by the God who searches.
53.3: A direct citation: "Every single one has turned aside; together they have become rotten; there is no one who does good — not a single person." Romans 3:12 quotes this directly. The universal apostasy — every person turned, all corrupted, none doing good — is the scriptural warrant for the universal need of grace. Without the exception that is Christ, there is no escape from this verdict. But Romans 5:18-19: "just as one trespass resulted in condemnation for all people, so also one righteous act resulted in justification and life for all people. For just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous." The "not a single one" of Psalm 53:3 is answered by the one whose obedience covers all.
53.4: A theme: "Don't those who work evil know anything — those who devour my people the way they eat bread and never call on God?" The cannibalistic metaphor for oppressors — devouring the people of God as casually as eating bread — is the OT's image of exploitative power. James 5:1-6 uses similar language about those who exploit their workers. The "never call on God" is the defining characteristic: the wicked have no covenantal relationship that would restrain their exploitation. Christ's community is constituted by calling on God (Rom 10:12-13: "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved") — the inverse of the characterization here.
53.5: A theme: "But there they are, overwhelmed with terror where there was no terror, for God scattered the bones of those who attacked you; you put them to shame, for God rejected them." The sudden terror of the wicked who had no reason to fear — the unexpected reversal of their confidence — is the eschatological pattern. 1 Thessalonians 5:3: "while people are saying, 'Peace and safety,' destruction will come on them suddenly, as labor pains on a pregnant woman, and they will not escape." The "scattering of bones" of those who attacked God's people is fulfilled eschatologically in the judgment that Christ pronounces: "Depart from me, you who are cursed" (Matt 25:41).
53.6: A theme: "Oh, that Israel's rescue would come out of Zion! When God restores the fortunes of his people, Jacob will rejoice and Israel will be glad." The messianic longing — the psalm closing with a cry for rescue from Zion — is the hope that Luke 2:38 describes as the expectation of Anna and others like her: "giving thanks to God and speaking about the child to all who were looking forward to the redemption of Jerusalem." Paul cites this hope in Romans 11:26 ("all Israel will be saved, as it is written: 'The deliverer will come from Zion; he will turn godlessness away from Jacob'") — explicitly applying the Zion-rescue hope to Christ's future work. The "restoration of fortunes" of God's people is the eschatological restoration that Christ's return will complete.

54.1: A theme: "To the director of music. With stringed instruments. A Maskil of David. When the Ziphites came to Saul and said, 'Is not David hiding among us?'" The superscription situates this lament in David's experience of betrayal by his own countrymen (1 Sam 23:19; 26:1) — fellow Israelites who reported his location to Saul. This pattern of betrayal by those who should be loyal is the pattern of Christ's passion: betrayed by Judas (one of the Twelve), denied by Peter (the leading disciple), abandoned by all. The historical context of David-as-fugitive is the shadow behind which the passion narrative stands.
54.2: A theme: "Hear my prayer, O God; listen to what I am saying." The opening petition for a hearing — the fundamental cry of the lament genre — is the prayer posture that Christ models. Hebrews 5:7: "In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The Gethsemane prayer (Matt 26:36-46) is the fullest instance of the "hear my prayer" of Psalm 54:2 — the cry of the righteous sufferer to the Father who hears.
54.3: A theme: "For outsiders are threatening me; violent men are hunting my life — people who have no thought of God." The threatening outsiders (zārîm — strangers/foreigners, here used of enemies) who hunt the psalmist's life are the hostile forces arrayed against the righteous. At Jesus's arrest, the crowd with swords and clubs (Mark 14:43-46), the Roman soldiers (John 18:12), and the religious authorities who plotted against him all qualify as those "hunting his life." "People who have no thought of God" — those who, in the moment of the passion, did not reckon with the divine identity of the one they were pursuing (Acts 3:17: "I know that you acted in ignorance, as did your leaders").
54.4: A revelation of God: "But God is my helper; the Lord is the one who sustains my life." The divine identity as helper-and-sustainer — the one who stands with the righteous sufferer when all human support fails — is the confidence of the passion narrative. Hebrews 13:6 (citing Ps 118:7): "The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?" Christ sustained his earthly life through the Father's presence (John 8:29: "the one who sent me is with me; he has not left me alone"), and after the cross, the Father's "sustaining of life" was the resurrection — the definitive divine answer to the violence of v3.
54.5: A theme: "He will bring the evil back on my enemies; in your faithfulness, silence them." The prayer for the return of evil upon the persecutors — covenant justice enacted — is not vindictiveness but faith in divine retribution. Romans 12:19: "do not take revenge, my dear friends, but leave room for God's wrath, for it is written: 'It is mine to avenge; I will repay,' says the Lord." Christ did not call down judgment on his persecutors; instead, "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing" (Luke 23:34). But the silencing of the wicked through divine faithfulness remains an eschatological certainty: "God will bring into judgment both the righteous and the wicked" (Eccles 3:17).
54.6: A theme: "With a generous, willing heart I will offer sacrifice to you; I will give thanks to your name, LORD, for it is good." The freewill offering (nĕdāvāh — the voluntary, unprompted sacrifice beyond what is required) given in thanksgiving — this is the sacrifice the NT community offers "through Jesus." 2 Corinthians 9:7: "Each of you should give what you have decided in your heart to give, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver." Christ himself is the archetypal freewill offering: his death was not compelled (John 10:18: "no one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord"). The generous, willing sacrifice of Ps 54:6 is the pattern Christ embodies and invites the redeemed to imitate.
54.7: A theme: "For he has rescued me from every distress, and my eye has seen the downfall of my enemies." The psalm closes with thanksgiving for comprehensive rescue — "from every distress" — and the testimony of witnessed enemy-defeat. The resurrection is the NT's comprehensive rescue from "every distress": death itself, the ultimate distress, has been defeated. 1 Corinthians 15:55-57: "'Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. But thanks be to God! He gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ." The eye that "sees the downfall of enemies" is the faith that looks to the resurrection and sees death already defeated.

62.1: shadow For God alone my soul waits in silence; from him comes my salvation. Christ before Pilate embodies this silence: he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge, so that the governor was greatly amazed (Matt 27:14; cf. Isa 53:7). His salvation comes not from human defense but from God alone — the resurrection is God's single act of vindication for the one who waits in silent trust.
62.2: shadow He alone is my rock and my salvation, my fortress; I shall not be greatly shaken. Christ is the rock — the rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). He is the one who shall not be shaken because God raised him: the resurrection is the ultimate steadying of the one whom death tried to shake. He who is the rock is also the one who rests on the rock in perfect trust.
62.3: shadow How long will all of you attack a man to batter him, like a leaning wall, a tottering fence? The sustained assault on the righteous man — from the Sanhedrin's arrest through Pilate's interrogation and the mockery at the cross — batters the one who appears like a tottering fence. Yet he is the cornerstone (Eph 2:20) who cannot ultimately fall, however violently he is struck.
62.4: shadow They only plan to thrust him down from his high position. They take pleasure in falsehood. They bless with their mouths, but inwardly they curse. The hypocrisy of those who hailed Jesus at the Triumphal Entry and then cried crucify him! five days later — blessing with mouths, cursing inwardly. Judas's kiss is the supreme instance: the gesture of friendship concealing betrayal.
62.5: shadow For God alone, O my soul, wait in silence, for my hope is from him. Christ is himself the hope: Christ in you, the hope of glory (Col 1:27). The soul that waits in silence for God waits for the one who is God's ultimate answer. Christ modeled this waiting — in Gethsemane, not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42) — and became the ground of all waiting hope.
62.6: shadow He only is my rock and my salvation, my fortress; I shall not be shaken. The repeated refrain deepens with Christ's resurrection: the tomb could not hold him, the stone was rolled away (Matt 28:2), and the one who rested in the rock-hewn tomb was raised. He is unmoved by death itself — the fortress that no enemy can storm.
62.7: shadow On God rests my salvation and my glory; my mighty rock, my refuge is God. Christ's glory rests on the Father's vindication through resurrection. He is simultaneously the one who takes refuge in God (as the righteous man) and the refuge itself (as the divine Son). Paul's Christ, who is our life (Col 3:4) and John's I am the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) both fulfill this verse.
62.8: theme Trust in him at all times, O people; pour out your heart before him; God is a refuge for us. The invitation to pour out the heart before God is the posture of prayer Christ both exemplified and enables. He poured out his soul to death (Isa 53:12); through him we have access to the Father by one Spirit (Eph 2:18), so that we may pour out our hearts at all times.
62.9: theme Those of low estate are but a breath; those of high estate are a delusion; in the balances they go up; they are together lighter than a breath. The vanity of human power — the Romans, the Sanhedrin, Pilate — all lighter than a breath against the purposes of God. Christ's kingdom cannot be shaken (Heb 12:28) while the empires that crucified him are dust. Human pretension is the backdrop against which divine power appears.
62.10: theme Put no trust in extortion; set no vain hopes on robbery; if riches increase, set not your heart on them. Christ's teaching on wealth and the heart (Matt 6:19-21; Luke 12:15) echoes this wisdom. He himself had nowhere to lay his head (Matt 8:20), renouncing every form of material security to reveal the kingdom that no theft can diminish.
62.11: shadow Once God has spoken; twice have I heard this: that power belongs to God, Divine power displayed supremely at the resurrection: the power of God (1 Cor 1:24) is Christ crucified and raised. The Father's power does not belong to Caesar or the Sanhedrin — it belongs to the one who raised Jesus from the dead and who will raise those who are in him (Rom 8:11).
62.12: direct and that to you, O Lord, belongs steadfast love. For you will render to a man according to his work. Paul cites this verse in Romans 2:6 (rendering to each according to works) and Christ applies it to himself: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done (Rev 22:12). The one who renders according to works is the risen Christ who holds the final judgment.

63.1: shadow O God, you are my God; earnestly I seek you; my soul thirsts for you; my flesh faints for you, as in a dry and weary land where there is no water. Christ in the wilderness of temptation (Matt 4:1-11) inhabits this desert thirst — yet he is also the one who satisfies: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst (John 6:35).
63.2: revelation of God So I have looked upon you in the sanctuary, beholding your power and glory. The sanctuary where God's glory is seen is Christ himself: we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth (John 1:14). The Transfiguration is the moment when disciples behold his power and glory in a foretaste of the resurrection (Matt 17:1-8).
63.3: shadow Because your steadfast love is better than life, my lips will praise you. For Christ, the Father's steadfast love was literally valued above his own life: I lay down my life (John 10:17-18). Paul's echo — to live is Christ, and to die is gain (Phil 1:21) — reflects the same calculus. The one who counts love better than life gives his life freely and is raised.
63.4: shadow So I will bless you as long as I live; in your name I will lift up my hands. Christ's lifted hands at his Ascension: lifting up his hands he blessed them. While he blessed them, he parted from them and was carried up into heaven (Luke 24:50-51). The blessing with lifted hands is his final gesture — the one who praised the Father through his life blesses his people as he departs.
63.5: shadow My soul will be satisfied as with fat and rich food, and my mouth will praise you with joyful lips, The rich satisfaction of communion with God is realized in the Eucharist — the feast Christ provides where souls are satisfied (John 6:55-58). His mouth praised the Father even at the Last Supper (Matt 26:30 — when they had sung a hymn, they went out) and from the cross (Luke 23:46).
63.6: shadow when I remember you upon my bed, and meditate on you in the watches of the night; Christ's night prayers — in these days he went out to the mountain to pray, and all night he continued in prayer to God (Luke 6:12). Gethsemane is the supreme night watch: the Son meditates on the Father through the darkest hours before the Passion.
63.7: shadow for you have been my help, and in the shadow of your wings I will sing for joy. The shadow of God's wings as shelter: Christ applies this to Jerusalem — How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings (Matt 23:37). He longs to be the shelter his people refuse; yet for those who accept him, he is the wing-shadow of the Father's protection.
63.8: shadow My soul clings to you; your right hand upholds me. Christ's intimate clinging to the Father through the Passion: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit (Luke 23:46). The right hand that upholds is the one that raises — God exalted him at his right hand (Acts 5:31). Upheld through death, he is set at the right hand of the Majesty on high.
63.9: shadow But those who seek to destroy my life shall go down into the depths of the earth; Those who sought Christ's life — Herod (Matt 2), the Sanhedrin (Matt 26), Judas (Matt 26:47-50) — face the depths they intended for him. Christ himself descended into the depths of death (Eph 4:9 — he also descended into the lower regions) and rose; his enemies have no such resurrection.
63.10: shadow they shall be given over to the power of the sword; they shall be a portion for jackals. The judgment reserved for those who destroyed the righteous one — Judas (Acts 1:18-19, dying in the field of blood) and Jerusalem (destroyed in AD 70 as Jesus predicted in Matt 24). The one delivered to the sword rose; those who wielded it suffer the consequences.
63.11: shadow But the king shall rejoice in God; all who swear by him shall exult, for the mouths of liars will be stopped. The king who rejoices is the risen Christ: for the joy that was set before him he endured the cross, despising the shame (Heb 12:2). The mouths of liars — the false witnesses at the trial (Matt 26:59-61) — are stopped by the resurrection, which exposes every false accusation as void.

64.1: shadow Hear my voice, O God, in my complaint; preserve my life from dread of the enemy. God hears Christ's voice in the complaint of Gethsemane — with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard (Heb 5:7). The preservation of his life comes not by averting death but by raising him through it.
64.2: shadow Hide me from the secret plots of the wicked, from the throng of evildoers, The Sanhedrin's secret conspiracy: the chief priests and the scribes were seeking how to put him to death, for they feared the people (Luke 22:2). Their secret plot against Christ is the supreme instance of this verse — the righteous one surrounded by hidden conspirators.
64.3: shadow who whet their tongues like swords, who aim bitter words like arrows, The verbal weapons at the trial: false witnesses (Matt 26:59-61), the high priest's accusations (Matt 26:65), the soldiers' taunts (Luke 23:36), the crowd's mockery (Matt 27:39-43). Tongues sharpened like swords are aimed at the one who is the Word of God — the living Word against whom words are weaponized.
64.4: shadow shooting from ambush at the blameless, shooting at him suddenly and without fear. The arrest in Gethsemane is the ambush — Judas and the crowd arriving suddenly in the night (John 18:3). The blameless one is struck without warning by those who have no fear of God before their eyes (Rom 3:18, citing Ps 36:1). The ambush of the Son of God is the ultimate crime of the age.
64.5: shadow They hold fast to their evil purpose; they talk of laying snares secretly, thinking, Who can see us? Who can see us? — they do not reckon with the one who sees in secret (Matt 6:4) and before whom all things are naked and exposed (Heb 4:13). The Sanhedrin thought their night trial and secret burial sealed would go unwitnessed by God — but the resurrection reveals all.
64.6: shadow They search out injustice, saying, We have accomplished a diligent search. For the inward thought and heart of a man is deep. The deep inward scheming of those who plotted against Christ — their careful search for a legal pretext (Luke 22:2), the false witnesses sought (Matt 26:59). They thought they had accomplished their purpose; the resurrection overturns every deep scheme they devised.
64.7: shadow But God shoots his arrow at them; they are wounded suddenly. The divine reversal at the resurrection: those who shot arrows at the blameless are suddenly wounded by God's own action. The stone rolled away, the guards trembling like dead men (Matt 28:4), the reports of resurrection spreading despite sealed tombs — God shoots his arrow, and the plotters are undone.
64.8: shadow They are brought to ruin, with their own tongues turned against them; all who see them will wag their heads. The self-defeating plots of the Passion: Judas hangs himself (Matt 27:5); Pilate's inscription King of the Jews remains unchanged despite priestly objection (John 19:21-22); the sealed tomb is broken open. Their own measures turn against them and vindicate the one they condemned.
64.9: shadow Then all mankind fears; they tell what God has brought about and ponder what he has done. The centurion's response at the cross: Truly this was the Son of God! (Matt 27:54). The fear that falls on all — the guards, the women, the disciples at the empty tomb — is the fear of beholding what God has brought about. The pondering of God's deeds is the first movement of Christian proclamation.
64.10: shadow Let the righteous one rejoice in the LORD and take refuge in him! Let all the upright in heart exult! The righteous one who rejoices is Christ — raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep (1 Cor 15:20). All the upright in heart exult because his resurrection is their own: if the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you (Rom 8:11), the same exultation is theirs.

65.1: shadow Praise is due to you, O God, in Zion; and to you shall vows be performed. The praise due in Zion is the praise of the church gathered around the risen Christ. He is the one who performs the ultimate vow — the vow of the Servant who gives himself for the many (Isa 53:12). Through him all vows to God are fulfilled in the Yes of Christ (2 Cor 1:20).
65.2: direct O you who hear prayer, to you shall all flesh come. Jesus applies this universal coming to himself: And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself (John 12:32). The one to whom all flesh comes is the crucified and risen Christ — the fulfillment of this verse's promise that prayer reaches the one who gathers all humanity.
65.3: shadow When iniquities prevail against me, you atone for our transgressions. The atonement — you atone for our transgressions — is God's own act, accomplished through Christ: God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them (2 Cor 5:19). Iniquity prevails against Christ on the cross; God atones through that very event.
65.4: shadow Blessed is the one you choose and bring near, to dwell in your courts! The chosen one brought near is Christ — the elect Servant (Isa 42:1; 1 Pet 2:4): he chose us in him before the foundation of the world (Eph 1:4). Through Christ, believers too are brought near to dwell in God's courts (Eph 2:13 — brought near by the blood of Christ).
65.5: shadow By awesome deeds you answer us with righteousness, O God of our salvation, the hope of all the ends of the earth and of the farthest seas; The hope of all the ends of the earth — you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses... to the end of the earth (Acts 1:8). Christ is the hope (Col 1:27) brought to the farthest seas by the missionary church empowered by the Spirit.
65.6: revelation of God the one who by his strength established the mountains, being girded with might; The creator God whose might established the mountains is the one who, in Christ, says I have overcome the world (John 16:33). The same power that set the mountains in place raised Jesus from the dead — Paul identifies it as the same divine strength (Eph 1:19-20).
65.7: shadow who stills the roaring of the seas, the roaring of their waves, the tumult of the peoples, Christ stills the storm: he rebuked the wind and said to the sea, Peace! Be still! And the wind ceased, and there was a great calm (Mark 4:39). The disciples asked, Who then is this? — the creator God who stills the seas is present in human flesh.
65.8: shadow so that those who dwell at the ends of the earth are in awe at your signs. You make the shouts of the morning and the evening ring out with joy. The signs of Christ — healings, resurrections, the cross and empty tomb — inspire awe at the ends of the earth (Acts 4:16 — a notable sign has been performed, and we cannot deny it). The shouts of the morning ring out at the resurrection announcement.
65.9: shadow You visit the earth and water it; you greatly enrich it; the river of God is full of water. Christ the living water visits the earth in the Incarnation. The river of God full of water is the Spirit poured out: out of his heart will flow rivers of living water (John 7:38-39 — he said this about the Spirit). The enrichment of the earth by Christ's coming and the Spirit's outpouring is the new covenant's abundance.
65.10: shadow You water its furrows abundantly, settling its ridges, softening it with showers, and blessing its growth. The parable of the sower (Matt 13:1-23) — Christ the sower watering the earth with the word, seeking fruitful ground. The rain that softens soil is the word and Spirit working together to produce the harvest of the kingdom.
65.11: shadow You crown the year with your bounty; your wagon tracks overflow with abundance. The abundance of the messianic age: the wedding at Cana (John 2:1-11 — 180 gallons of finest wine), the feeding of the 5,000 (twelve baskets left over), the feeding of the 4,000. Christ's presence crowns every moment with bounty — the overflowing abundance of the kingdom.
65.12: shadow The pastures of the wilderness overflow, the hills gird themselves with joy; The wilderness restored: Isaiah's vision of the desert rejoicing (Isa 35:1) finds its fulfillment in Christ who makes the wilderness blossom — healing in deserted places (Matt 14:13-21), feeding crowds in the wilderness (Matt 15:32-38), transformed lives spreading joy through desolate regions.
65.13: shadow the meadows clothe themselves with flocks, the valleys deck themselves with grain, they shout and sing together for joy. The eschatological harvest: Christ's parable of the harvest (Matt 13:39 — the harvest is the end of the age); the reaper's joy (John 4:35-36 — the fields are white for harvest). The whole creation's singing anticipates Paul's vision: the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption (Rom 8:21).

66.1: shadow Shout for joy to God, all the earth; The universal shout of joy directed at God reaches its fulfillment in the praise of the Lamb: Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing! (Rev 5:12). All the earth's joy — the eschatological shout — is concentrated in the risen Christ.
66.2: shadow sing the glory of his name; give to him glorious praise! The glorious name sung by all the earth is the name above every name (Phil 2:9-11). Paul commands: in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him (Col 3:17). The glorious praise is Christ-centered praise — the Father glorified in the Son.
66.3: shadow Say to God, How awesome are your deeds! So great is your power that your enemies come cringing to you. The awesome deed of the cross and resurrection: enemies who come cringing — in Paul's framework, every power and principality prostrating before the name of Christ (Phil 2:10), and ultimately he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet (1 Cor 15:25).
66.4: shadow All the earth worships you and sings praises to you; they sing praises to your name. The universal worship anticipated here is the worship of the Lamb in Revelation (Rev 5:13 — every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea). Christ is the one to whom all the earth's praise is ultimately directed (John 5:23 — that all may honor the Son, just as they honor the Father).
66.5: shadow Come and see what God has done: he is awesome in his deeds toward the children of man. The angel's invitation: Come, see the place where he lay (Matt 28:6). The awesome deed toward humanity is the resurrection — the decisive act by which God demonstrates his power over death for all who trust in Christ (1 Cor 15:20-22).
66.6: type He turned the sea into dry land; they passed through the river on foot. There did we rejoice in him, The Exodus crossing is a type of Christ: Paul identifies the sea-crossing as baptism — all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea (1 Cor 10:1-2). The crossing through water into freedom is the pattern Christ fulfills in baptism into his death and resurrection (Rom 6:3-4).
66.7: shadow who rules by his might forever, whose eyes keep watch on the nations — let not the rebellious exalt themselves. Christ rules by might forever from the Father's right hand (Heb 1:3; 1 Cor 15:25). His eyes as Lord of the nations: his eyes are like a flame of fire (Rev 1:14; 2:18) — watching over the nations and addressing each church with divine authority.
66.8: theme Bless our God, O peoples; let the sound of his praise be heard, The peoples blessing God in Christ: Paul's vision in Rom 15:9-11 of Gentiles glorifying God alongside Israel, citing multiple OT texts showing the universal praise of God. The sound of praise heard among all peoples is the church's mission — the worship of Christ spreading through every tongue.
66.9: shadow who has kept our soul among the living and has not let our feet slip. Christ keeps those who are his among the living — because I live, you also will live (John 14:19). He who was kept by the Father through death (Acts 2:24 — God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death) now keeps his people so that their feet do not slip into permanent death.
66.10: shadow For you, O God, have tested us; you have tried us as silver is tried. The testing of Christ in the wilderness (Matt 4:1-11), in Gethsemane, and on the cross: he is tried and emerges as refined silver — a lamb without blemish or spot (1 Pet 1:19). The testing of believers is patterned on his: the testing of your faith... more precious than gold (1 Pet 1:7).
66.11: shadow You brought us into the net; you laid a crushing burden on our backs; The crushing burden: Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows (Isa 53:4). Christ takes the burden on himself — the yoke of the cross (Matt 11:28-30 contrasts his easy yoke with the crushing burden). He enters the net of suffering to carry what no one else can bear.
66.12: shadow you let men ride over our heads; we went through fire and through water; yet you have brought us out to a place of abundance. Fire and water as trials — 1 Pet 1:7 (faith tested by fire); the place of abundance is the new creation. Christ himself went through the fire of judgment and the water of death to bring his people out to the resurrection abundance (2 Cor 4:17 — an eternal weight of glory beyond all comparison).
66.13: shadow I will come into your house with burnt offerings; I will perform my vows to you, Christ is the ultimate burnt offering — who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God (Heb 9:14). He performs the vow of the Servant (Isa 53:10 — when his soul makes an offering for guilt), entering the house of God not with animal sacrifice but with his own blood (Heb 9:12).
66.14: shadow that which my lips uttered and my mouth promised when I was in trouble. The vow made in trouble — Gethsemane's not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42) is the vow Christ keeps. He promised obedience to the Father through the cup of suffering and delivered it fully on the cross, the faithful keeper of every promise made in the hour of trouble.
66.15: shadow I will offer to you burnt offerings of fattened animals, with the smoke of the sacrifice of rams; I will make an offering of bulls and goats. The sacrificial system this verse celebrates is fulfilled and ended in Christ: it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins (Heb 10:4). Christ's single offering perfects what the repeated offerings of rams and bulls could only shadow (Heb 10:10-14).
66.16: theme Come and hear, all you who fear God, and I will tell what he has done for my soul. The personal testimony of what God has done is the shape of Christian witness: what we have seen and heard we proclaim also to you (1 John 1:3). Christ himself declares the Father's name to his brothers (Heb 2:12) — the testimony of what God has done for his soul is the resurrection proclamation.
66.17: shadow I cried to him with my mouth, and high praise was on my tongue. Christ's cry to the Father from the cross (Matt 27:46; Luke 23:46) and the praise that followed — the resurrection doxology. He who cried in abandonment also cried in trust; both cries are heard; the praise on his tongue becomes the church's song (Heb 13:15).
66.18: shadow If I had cherished iniquity in my heart, the Lord would not have listened. Christ cherished no iniquity — which of you convicts me of sin? (John 8:46); in him there is no sin (1 John 3:5). Therefore the Lord listened: Heb 5:7 — he was heard because of his reverence. His sinless heart is the ground of his efficacious prayer and perfect atonement.
66.19: shadow But truly God has listened; he has attended to the voice of my prayer. God listened to Christ in Gethsemane — not by removing the cup but by sustaining him through it and raising him from the dead. The ultimate answer to Christ's prayer is the resurrection: God attended to the voice of his prayer and answered with the power that brings life from death (Heb 5:7).
66.20: revelation of God Blessed be God, because he has not rejected my prayer or removed his steadfast love from me! The apparent rejection of the cross — My God, why have you forsaken me? — is overturned by this blessing: God did not ultimately reject his prayer or remove his steadfast love. The resurrection is the proof that divine love was present through every moment of the Passion, never withdrawn, always keeping (Rom 8:38-39).

67.1: direct May God be gracious to us and bless us and make his face to shine upon us, The Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24-26) is fulfilled in Christ: the blessing of Abraham comes to the Gentiles through Christ Jesus (Gal 3:14). The face of God shining — God who said let light shine out of darkness, has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Cor 4:6). Christ is the shining face of God.
67.2: direct that your way may be known on earth, your saving power among all nations. Christ declares: I am the way, and the truth, and the life (John 14:6). His saving power is proclaimed among all nations through the Great Commission (Matt 28:19-20). The way of God known on earth is the way of the cross and resurrection — the saving power that extends to every nation.
67.3: direct Let the peoples praise you, O God; let all the peoples praise you! Paul quotes this verse in Romans 15:11 as part of his catena demonstrating that the Gentiles were always meant to glorify God alongside Israel: Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles, and let all the peoples extol him. The universal praise is the telos of Christ's mission — the nations brought in to glorify the God of Israel through Jesus Christ.
67.4: shadow Let the nations be glad and sing for joy, for you judge the peoples with equity and guide the nations upon earth. The equitable judgment and guidance of the nations is vested in the risen Christ: he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed (Acts 17:31). The nations' gladness is eschatological — the joy of receiving just governance from the one King.
67.5: direct Let the peoples praise you, O God; let all the peoples praise you! The refrain repeated — the insistence of universal praise. Paul's citations in Rom 15:9-11 culminate in this missional vision: Christ has become a servant to the circumcised so that the Gentiles might glorify God (Rom 15:8-9). Every repetition of the refrain is a prayer that the Great Commission reaches its completion.
67.6: shadow The earth has yielded its increase; God, our God, shall bless us. The earth's yield reaches its eschatological fullness in Christ the firstfruits (1 Cor 15:20,23): his resurrection is the first harvest of the new creation. The blessing God gives is the blessing of the Spirit poured out through Christ — the firstfruits of the Spirit (Rom 8:23) guaranteeing the full harvest to come.
67.7: shadow God shall bless us; let all the ends of the earth fear him! The final verse completes the universalizing arc: the fear of the LORD spreading to all the ends of the earth. Acts 1:8 is the commission that drives this: you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth. God's blessing through Christ propels the church to the ends of the earth in holy reverence.

55.1: A theme: Psalm 55 is a Maskil (an instructional poem) of David, beginning with the raw plea "Give ear to my prayer, O God; do not hide yourself from my plea for mercy." The hidden God who does not answer is the deepest fear in the Psalter's lament tradition (cf. Ps 10:1; 22:1; 44:24). Christ himself prays this petition in Gethsemane: "Abba, Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me" (Mark 14:36). The cry that God not hide himself is the prayer that the cross appears to negate ("My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Matt 27:46) but the resurrection finally answers.
55.2: A theme: "Pay attention to me and answer me; I am troubled in my lament and I am agitated." The agitation (ʾārîd bĕśîḥî, I wander/moan in my concern) of the Psalmist in crisis is the emotional register of Gethsemane: "My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death" (Matt 26:38). Christ enters the full reality of human anguish — not performing calm — and the prayer to be heard and answered is the prayer the Father answers not by removing the cup but by raising Christ through death.
55.3: A shadow: "because of the threatening voice of the enemy, the oppressive presence of the wicked; they bring evil upon me, and in anger they press hard against me." The threatening pressure of the enemy — voices and presence pressing hard — is the Passion narrative's texture. John 11:53 ("they plotted to take his life"), Matthew 26:3-5 (the Sanhedrin's deliberate plan), and the crowd's escalating pressure on Pilate (John 19:12-15: "Crucify him!") are the fulfillment of the hostile pressing of Psalm 55:3.
55.4: A shadow: "My heart is writhing in anguish within me; the terrors of death have fallen on me." The terrors of death (ʾêmôt māwet) that descend on the Psalmist are the Passion's emotional reality. Acts 2:24 states that God "raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (lysas tas ōdinas tou thanatou) because it was impossible for death to hold him." The "pangs" / "terrors" of death fell on Christ at Gethsemane and the cross — and the resurrection is the divine loosing of those very terrors.
55.5: A shadow: "Fear and trembling have overtaken me, and shuddering has covered me over." The triple collapse of composure — fear, trembling, shuddering — is the extreme of human vulnerability before mortal threat. In Gethsemane, Luke 22:44 records: "And being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly, and his sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground." The shuddering of Psalm 55:5 is enacted literally in the Agony in the Garden — the one who is the source of all courage entered the full experience of human terror before death.
55.6: A revelation of God: "And I said, 'If only I had wings like a dove! I would fly away and find rest.'" The wish for escape — dove's wings to flee suffering — is the honest prayer that did not override duty. Christ did not escape: "Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me; yet not my will, but yours be done" (Luke 22:42). The dove-wing escape of Psalm 55:6 is the human desire that the incarnate Son also felt, and which he voluntarily set aside. The Spirit descends as a dove at his baptism (Matt 3:16) — the dove that stays, rather than flees, empowering the mission.
55.7: A theme: "Yes, I would flee far away; I would make my dwelling in the wilderness." The wilderness as refuge — distance from human violence — is the pattern Christ actually entered at the beginning of his ministry (Matt 4:1: "Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness"). But where Psalm 55:7 wishes for escape to the wilderness, Christ's wilderness is not escape but preparation for conflict. He emerges from the wilderness to face the very opposition Psalm 55 describes — he chose engagement over escape.
55.8: A theme: "I would hurry to my refuge from the driving wind and the storm." The shelter from storm is the image of YHWH as refuge (Ps 46:1; Isa 25:4: "a shelter from the storm"). In the NT, Christ is the shelter who calms the storm itself (Mark 4:39: "Quiet! Be still!") — not merely a refuge from the storm but the one who ends the storm. The desperate flight to shelter in Psalm 55:8 becomes, in Christ, the arrival at the one who has already stilled the chaos that threatens.
55.9: A shadow: "Confuse them, O Lord, split their tongues apart; for I see violence and contention in the city." The prayer for the confusion of violent, divisive speech in the city recalls Babel (Gen 11:7-9: "Come, let us go down and confuse their language") and echoes the judgment on Babylon of Revelation 18. The "city" of Psalm 55:9 racked by violence is Jerusalem in its corrupt state — and it is in Jerusalem that Christ is rejected and killed. The judgment on the city that opposed the Messiah comes in 70 AD (Matt 24:2).
55.10: A shadow: "Day and night they patrol its walls; iniquity and trouble are entrenched within it." The city fortified by iniquity — the watchers who enforce the corrupt order — is the Jerusalem of the Passion narrative. The chief priests, scribes, and elders (Matt 26:3) who plan against Jesus within the holy city represent the iniquity "entrenched" at the center of what should be the place of righteous rule. The city that should shelter the righteous becomes the place where the Righteous One is killed.
55.11: A shadow: "Ruin is entrenched at its center; oppression and fraud never leave its public spaces." The corruption of the city's center — the marketplace of the heart of the covenant community — is what Christ confronts directly in the temple cleansing (John 2:13-17; Matt 21:12-13). "My house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers" (Matt 21:13): the "fraud" that "never leaves its public spaces" in Psalm 55:11 is the commercial exploitation that Christ drives out of the temple courts.
55.12: A type: "For it is not an enemy who insults me — that I could endure; nor is it someone who hates me who has risen up against me — from that I could hide." The manageable grief of an enemy's opposition — bearable, even hideable from — establishes the contrast with what follows. This verse frames the specific quality of Judas's betrayal of Christ: it was not the opposition of Rome or the Sanhedrin that wounds most deeply, but the betrayal by one of the Twelve. The Psalm's structure of "what I could endure" vs. "what I cannot" mirrors the Gospel's framing of Judas as the deepest wound.
55.13: A type: "But it is you — a person like me, my peer, my companion, the one I confided in." The identity of the betrayer as intimate peer and confidant — "my peer (kĕʿerĕkî), my companion (ʾallûpî), the one I confided in (mĕyuddāʿî)" — is the precise profile of Judas Iscariot. John 13:18 quotes Psalm 41:9 in the betrayal context ("He who shared my bread has turned against me"), but Psalm 55:13 provides the fuller emotional portrait. Luke 6:16 simply notes "Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor" — the one who had been confided in fully.
55.14: A type: "We shared deep, sweet conversation together; together we walked in God's house among the festival crowd." The shared worship and intimate dialogue — the table fellowship of Psalm 55:14 — is precisely the context of the Last Supper where the betrayal is announced (John 13:21-30). The "festival crowd" in God's house is the Passover setting: together they reclined at table for the covenant meal, and it is Judas who leaves that meal to betray the one who had shared everything with him. The intimacy of Psalm 55:14 is the Last Supper's intimacy betrayed.
55.15: A shadow: "Let death ambush them; let them go down to Sheol alive; for wickedness lives in their dwellings and among them." The imprecatory prayer for the sudden death of the betrayers and violent enemies is the justice cry of the Psalmist. Acts 1:18 records the judgment on Judas: "he fell headlong, his body burst open and all his intestines spilled out." The "going down to Sheol" language of Psalm 55:15 is a type of the eschatological judgment on those who betray covenant relationship — the judgment is already enacted in miniature in Judas's end.
55.16: A revelation of God: "As for me, I will call upon God, and the LORD will rescue me." The personal declaration of trust contrasted with the enemies' fate is the pivot of Psalm 55. This is the pattern of Gethsemane → Easter: Christ calls upon God in prayer ("Father, if you are willing, take this cup from me," Luke 22:42) and the LORD rescues him — not by removing the cup but by raising him from the dead. The rescue of Psalm 55:16 is the resurrection, which comes through the very crisis that produces the prayer.
55.17: A revelation of God: "Evening and morning and at noon I cry out my lament and sigh; and he hears my voice." The threefold daily prayer — evening, morning, noon — is the pattern of constant access to YHWH (cf. Daniel's thrice-daily prayer, Dan 6:10). The NT promise is even broader: "pray continually" (1 Thess 5:17). Christ as the great high priest "always lives to make intercession" for his people (Heb 7:25) — the continuous intercession that Psalm 55:17's rhythm points toward. The "he hears my voice" is the confidence that resurrection grounds.
55.18: A revelation of God: "He ransoms me safely from the battle I am in, for they are many who oppose me." The ransom from the hostile many (pādāh bĕšālôm napšî miqĕrāb-lî, he redeemed my life in peace from the battle against me) is the covenant vocabulary of the Exodus. Christ's ransom is the ultimate redemption: Mark 10:45, "the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many." The "many who oppose" in Psalm 55:18 become the "many" for whom the ransom is paid in Mark 10:45.
55.19: A revelation of God: "God will hear and afflict them, the one who is enthroned from of old — because they show no sign of change and have no fear of God." The divine response to the unrepentant — hearing, afflicting — is grounded in the eternal enthronement of YHWH. "No fear of God" (ʾên ḥălîpôt lāhemô wĕlōʾ yārĕʾû ʾĕlōhîm) is cited by Paul in Romans 3:18 as the culminating indictment of universal human sinfulness: "There is no fear of God before their eyes." The lack of repentance and God-fear that Psalm 55:19 condemns is the condition Paul declares universal — and which Christ addresses in the atonement.
55.20: A shadow: "He raised his hand against those who were at peace with him; he broke his covenant." The covenant-breaking by the one who was at peace — lifting the hand against friends, violating the solemn bond — is the deepest form of treachery in the ancient world. The betrayal of Jesus by Judas (who had received the dipped bread, the final sign of table-fellowship, John 13:26-27) is this covenant-breaking at its most extreme. Matthew 26:50: "Friend, do what you came for" — Jesus addresses the covenant-breaker as "friend" even in the act of betrayal.
55.21: A type: "His words were smoother than butter, yet war was in his heart; his speech was softer than oil, but it was all drawn swords." The gap between smooth speech and violent intent — honeyed words concealing weaponized intent — is the profile of hypocrisy that Christ repeatedly confronts. Matthew 26:48-49: Judas had arranged a signal — "The one I kiss is the man; arrest him." "Going at once to Jesus, Judas said, 'Greetings, Rabbi!' and kissed him." The kiss is the "smooth as butter" speech of Psalm 55:21 — and it is the signal for arrest.
55.22: A direct citation: "Throw your burden onto the LORD, and he will hold you up; he will never let the righteous be shaken." 1 Peter 5:7 quotes this verse in the context of community care under suffering: "Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you." The Psalm's command to throw (šĕlēk ʿal-YHWH yĕhābĕkā) the burden onto YHWH is the OT grounding for the NT's call to anxiety-free trust in God's care. Christ is the mediator through whom the casting of burdens reaches the Father — Matthew 11:28: "Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest."
55.23: A revelation of God: "But you, O God, will bring them down into the pit of ruin; men of bloodshed and treachery will not reach half their days. But I — I will trust in you." The final declaration of personal trust despite the enemies' violence is the Psalm's resolution. The emphatic "But I — I (waʾănî)" stands against the enemies' fate. Hebrews 2:13 applies the trust-language of the Psalms to Christ: "I will put my trust in him" (citing Isa 8:17). The one who trusts in Psalm 55:23 is the type of Christ who "entrusted himself to him who judges justly" (1 Pet 2:23) through the entire Passion.

56.1: A theme: Psalm 56 is a Michtam of David composed during his capture by the Philistines at Gath (1 Sam 21:10-15), when he feigned madness to escape. "Be merciful to me, O God, for men are trampling on me." The mercy-cry under the trampling of enemies is the Passion's recurring petition. Hebrews 4:16: "Let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need." Christ, who prays for mercy in extremity, becomes the mercy-giver through whom we approach the throne.
56.2: A shadow: "My enemies trample on me all day long, for many are arrayed against me proudly." The proud array of many enemies all day long is the sustained opposition that Christ faces throughout his ministry — not only at the cross. John 7:32 (the Pharisees send temple guards to arrest him), John 11:47 (the Sanhedrin convenes against him), John 12:10 (the chief priests plot to kill Lazarus too). The "trampling all day long" of Psalm 56:2 is the cumulative pressure of sustained institutional opposition against the Messiah.
56.3: A revelation of God: "When I am afraid, I will put my trust in you." The simplest and most fundamental response to fear — trust in YHWH — is the bedrock of the Psalm. Romans 8:15: "The Spirit you received does not make you slaves, so that you live in fear again; rather, the Spirit you received brought about your adoption to sonship. And by him we cry, 'Abba, Father.'" The fear that would drive us away from God is replaced, through Christ's Spirit, with the same "trust in you" posture that Psalm 56:3 models.
56.4: A direct citation: "In God — whose word I praise — in God I trust; I will not be afraid. What can a mere human being do to me?" Hebrews 13:6 quotes this verse directly: "So we say with confidence, 'The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?'" The Psalm's original context (David surrounded by hostile Philistines) and the NT application (the Christian community under persecution) share the same theological ground: the human power to harm is relativized by the divine power to protect. Christ himself demonstrates this confidence before Pilate (John 19:10-11: "Do you refuse to speak to me? Don't you realize I have power either to free you or to crucify you?" Jesus answered, "You would have no power over me if it were not given to you from above").
56.5: A shadow: "All day long they distort my words; all their scheming is against me for evil." The distortion of the Psalmist's words — twisting speech to use it against him — is the Passion narrative's legal strategy. Matthew 26:60-61: false witnesses testify against Jesus, misrepresenting his words about the temple ("We heard him say, 'I will destroy this temple made with human hands and in three days will build another, not made with hands'"). The deliberate distortion of Christ's words about his resurrection is the fulfillment of Psalm 56:5.
56.6: A shadow: "They gather themselves to stir up trouble, they lie in ambush, they track my steps as they wait to take my life." The organized surveillance and ambush — watching every step to find the pretext — is the Synoptic Gospels' recurring description of the religious leaders' strategy against Jesus. Luke 20:20: "Keeping a close watch on him, they sent spies, who pretended to be sincere. They hoped to catch Jesus in something he said, so that they might hand him over to the power and authority of the governor." The "tracking steps" of Psalm 56:6 is the Pharisees' surveillance of Jesus.
56.7: A revelation of God: "For their crime, should they escape? In wrath cast down the peoples, O God!" The justice-prayer — that those who plot crime should not escape — is the Psalm's appeal to divine governance. Acts 17:31: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The judge through whom no crime escapes is the same one who was himself the target of the criminal plot — Christ vindicated and appointed as judge of those who conspired against him.
56.8: A revelation of God: "You have recorded my restless wandering; store my tears in your wineskin — are they not written in your book?" The divine preservation of the Psalmist's wandering and tears — everything recorded in the divine book — is the assurance of divine attentiveness to human suffering. Revelation 21:4: "He will wipe every tear from their eyes. There will be no more death or mourning or crying or pain, for the old order of things has passed away." The tears stored in the wineskin of Psalm 56:8 are the tears that Christ, who wept at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35) and over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41), will one day wipe away permanently.
56.9: A revelation of God: "On the day I call out, my enemies will turn back; this I know: God is on my side." The confident knowledge — "this I know (zeh ʾēdāʿ)" — that God is on the side of the one who calls is the resurrection's epistemological ground. Romans 8:31: "What, then, shall we say in response to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us?" The enemies who "turn back" at the Psalmist's cry are the powers that turn back at the resurrection: the stone rolled away, the guards fleeing (Matt 28:2-4), the grave's power broken.
56.10: A revelation of God: "In God — whose word I praise — in the LORD — whose word I praise." The doubled praise of the divine word (bĕʾlōhîm ʾăhallēl dābār bĕYHWH ʾăhallēl dābār) — first ʾĕlōhîm, then the covenant name YHWH — is the Psalm's structural affirmation of trust in the divine word. John 1:1 opens with "In the beginning was the Word (Logos)." The word of God that Psalm 56:10 praises finds its ultimate referent in the one who is the Word — Christ as the embodiment of every divine promise and declaration.
56.11: A revelation of God: "in God I trust; I will not be afraid. What can human beings do to me?" The refrain of vv4 and 11 brackets the Psalm's central crisis with the double declaration of fearlessness through divine trust. Matthew 10:28: "Do not be afraid of those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul." Christ gives to his community the same fearlessness that the Psalm models — the eschatological perspective in which human power to harm is relativized by the one whose power extends beyond death.
56.12: A theme: "I have made vows to you, O God, and I will fulfill them; I will offer you my thanksgiving." The fulfillment of vows as thanksgiving after rescue is the covenant response to divine rescue. Christ's Gethsemane prayer ("Not my will, but yours be done") is the vow of total obedience — and Hebrews 2:12 shows Christ fulfilling his praise-vow in the post-resurrection community: "I will declare your name to my brothers and sisters; in the assembly I will sing your praises" (citing Ps 22:22). The thanksgiving after rescue is Christ's own praise to the Father in the gathered community.
56.13: A direct connection: "For you have saved my life from death, and my feet from stumbling, so that I may walk before God in the light that belongs to the living." The rescue from death to walk in the light of the living is resurrection language. John 8:12: "I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life." Psalm 56:13's "light that belongs to the living (ʾôr haḥayyîm)" is the resurrection light in which Christ now walks and into which he brings his people. The one who was rescued from death is himself the light of life for those who walk in him.

57.1: A direct connection: "Be merciful to me, O God, be merciful to me, for my soul takes refuge in you; in the shadow of your wings I will take shelter until the storms of destruction pass." The "shadow of your wings" as refuge is a Psalter image for divine protection (Pss 17:8; 36:7; 61:4; 63:7; 91:4). Jesus applies the wings-image to himself in Matthew 23:37: "Jerusalem, Jerusalem, you who kill the prophets and stone those sent to you, how often I have longed to gather your children together, as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing." Christ is the one under whose wings shelter is found — and also the one whose longing to shelter was rejected.
57.2: A revelation of God: "I cry out to God Most High, to God who carries out his purpose for me." The divine purpose-fulfillment — YHWH ʾElyôn working out his plan for the Psalmist — is the providence that governs every apparent crisis. Acts 2:23: "This man was handed over to you by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge; and you, with the help of wicked men, put him to death by nailing him to the cross." The God who "carries out his purpose" for David's crisis is the same God whose deliberate plan governs the cross — what appears as defeat is the execution of divine purpose.
57.3: A revelation of God: "He will send from heaven and rescue me; he will rebuke the one who tramples me. God will send his steadfast love and his faithfulness." The divine sending from heaven of rescue — and of ḥesed and ʾĕmet (steadfast love and faithfulness) — is the Incarnation. John 3:17: "For God did not send his Son into the world to condemn the world, but to save the world through him." The rescue sent from heaven is the Son; the steadfast love and faithfulness that accompany it are the attributes of Christ himself — "full of grace and truth" (John 1:14, the NT equivalent of ḥesed wĕʾĕmet).
57.4: A shadow: "My soul is surrounded by lions; I must lie down among those who breathe fire — human beings whose teeth are spears and arrows and whose tongue is a sharp sword." The bestial imagery of dangerous enemies — lion-people with weapon-mouths — is the Psalm's vivid depiction of hostile power. 1 Peter 5:8: "Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour." The "lions" of Psalm 57:4 are the human instruments of the satanic opposition that Christ defeats (1 John 3:8: "The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the devil's work"). The tongue-as-sword imagery recurs in Revelation 1:16 (the sword from Christ's mouth).
57.5: A direct connection: "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" The refrain of vv5 and 11 is the doxological prayer for divine cosmic exaltation — YHWH's glory covering the whole earth. This is fulfilled in Christ's ascension and glorification: Philippians 2:9-11, "Therefore God exalted him to the highest place and gave him the name that is above every name, that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father." The exaltation above the heavens of Psalm 57:5 is Christ's ascension; the glory over all the earth is the universal Lordship he now holds.
57.6: A shadow: "They spread a net for my feet; my soul was beaten down; they dug a pit in my path, but they themselves have fallen into it." The reversal — the pit dug for the righteous becomes the trap of the wicked — is the Wisdom pattern of evil self-defeating. Acts 4:27-28: "Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel in this city to conspire against your holy servant Jesus... They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The pit they dug — the cross — became the instrument of their own defeat. 1 Corinthians 2:8: "None of the rulers of this age understood it, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory."
57.7: A revelation of God: "My heart is fixed, O God, my heart is firm; I will sing and make melody!" The fixedness of the Psalmist's heart — unmovable, settled in trust despite the surrounding net and pit — is the foundation of praise even before rescue. Hebrews 12:2: Christ endured the cross "for the joy set before him" — the fixed heart of Psalm 57:7 is the same joy-grounded steadiness that Christ maintains through the Passion. The "singing and making melody" from a firm heart is the resurrection praise: Hebrews 2:12 shows Christ singing in the post-resurrection assembly (citing Ps 22:22).
57.8: A theme: "Awake, my honor! Awake, harp and lyre! I will rouse the dawn!" The command to rouse the dawn — to summon morning before it comes, through anticipatory praise — is the posture of the one who trusts in resurrection before it arrives. Mark 16:2: "Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they were on their way to the tomb." The "rousing of the dawn" in Psalm 57:8 is the dawn of Easter — the first light of the resurrection morning that vindicates the trust of all who praised before the answer came.
57.9: A direct connection: "I will give you thanks, O Lord, among the peoples; I will sing your praise among the nations." Paul quotes this verse in Romans 15:9 as one of four OT texts demonstrating that Christ came to bring the Gentiles into Israel's praise: "Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles; I will sing hymns to your name" — citing it alongside Psalm 18:49, Deuteronomy 32:43, and Isaiah 11:10 to show that the Gentile mission was always in the OT script. The thanksgiving "among the nations" of Psalm 57:9 is the Messianic community's praise that the apostolic mission enacts in every people group.
57.10: A revelation of God: "For your steadfast love reaches to the heavens, your faithfulness to the skies." The cosmic reach of divine ḥesed — touching the heavens — is John 1:14's content: "full of grace and truth." The steadfast love and faithfulness that reach to the heavens have descended to earth in the Incarnation: the infinite ḥesed of YHWH has become personal in Jesus Christ. Ephesians 3:18-19: "to grasp how wide and long and high and deep is the love of Christ, and to know this love that surpasses knowledge." The heavens-reaching love of Psalm 57:10 is the love that surpasses knowledge in Christ.
57.11: A direct connection: "Be exalted above the heavens, O God; let your glory be over all the earth!" The closing refrain repeats v5 — the doxological petition that bookends the Psalm. The structural repetition underscores the centrality of divine exaltation as the goal and ground of all human experience — crisis, pit, net, and rescue are all subsumed under the cosmic desire for YHWH's glory. Christ's ascension ("he was taken up before their very eyes, and a cloud hid him from their sight," Acts 1:9) and present session ("he sat down at the right hand of the throne of God," Heb 12:2) are the fulfillment: exalted above the heavens, his glory over all the earth.
