58.1: A revelation of God: "Do you indeed decree what is right, you rulers? Do you judge human beings with justice?" Psalm 58 opens with a direct challenge to unjust human governance — the rhetorical question that exposes corrupt power. This is the prophetic function of the Psalter at its most confrontational. Acts 17:31 grounds all human accountability to divine justice: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed." Christ is the appointed judge before whom all human "rulers" will give account for whether they "decreed what is right." The Psalm's challenge to unjust rulers anticipates the final judgment through Christ.
58.2: A revelation of God: "No — in your heart you devise wrongs; your hands administer violence throughout the land." The gap between the judicial office (decreeing right) and the actual conduct (devising wrongs, administering violence) is the corruption Psalm 58 exposes. Romans 2:21-23 applies similar logic to those who teach the law while breaking it. Jesus applies it directly: Matthew 23:3, "So you must be careful to do everything they tell you. But do not do what they do, for they do not practice what they preach." The devising-of-wrongs in the heart while holding judicial power is the hypocrisy Christ confronts most consistently in the Gospels.
58.3: A revelation of God: "The wicked are alienated from the womb; from their very birth they go astray, speaking lies." The radical origin of human sin — alienation from birth, going astray from the womb — is the Psalm's assertion of what later theology calls original sin. Psalm 51:5 parallels this: "Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me." Romans 3:23: "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." The universal sinfulness from birth that Psalm 58:3 asserts is the condition that makes the Incarnation necessary — the one born without alienation (Luke 1:35: "the holy one to be born will be called the Son of God") to rescue those born alienated.
58.4: A direct citation: "Their venom is like the venom of a snake; they are like the deaf cobra that blocks its ear." Paul cites the next clause ("their throat is an open grave" from Ps 5:9) and the viper-venom image (combined from Pss 58:4 and 140:3) in Romans 3:13: "their throats are open graves; with their tongues they practice deceit; the poison of vipers is on their lips." Paul uses this cluster of Psalm-texts to prove universal human sinfulness. The snake-venom of Psalm 58:4 becomes part of Paul's sweeping indictment of all humanity before Christ — and the antidote to viper-venom is the one "lifted up like a snake in the desert" (John 3:14), whose lifting reverses the venom's effect.
58.5: A revelation of God: "that will not listen to the voice of those who charm, no matter how skillfully they do it." The deaf cobra that will not hear the charmer — implacable in its hostility — is the Psalm's image for those who resist every appeal. Luke 16:31: "If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead." The one who rises from the dead is precisely the "charmer" the implacable reject: Christ risen still does not convince those who have closed their ears. The deafness of Psalm 58:5 is the deafness of those who reject even the resurrection.
58.6: A shadow: "Smash their teeth in their mouths, O God; tear out the fangs of the young lions, O LORD!" The imprecatory prayer against the weapon-teeth of the violent is the Psalm's justice cry. Christ's enemies are described in lion-and-fang imagery throughout the Psalter (Pss 7:2; 22:13; 57:4). The divine smashing of their teeth is the eschatological defeat of all hostile power: Revelation 19:15 (the sword from Christ's mouth conquers the armies), and the final defeat of all enemies "under his feet" (1 Cor 15:25-26). The prayer for tooth-smashing in Psalm 58:6 is the raw form of the eschatological victory that Christ achieves.
58.7: A shadow: "Let them drain away like water flowing downhill; when they draw their bows, let their arrows fall harmless." The draining of violent power — arrows rendered harmless — is the imprecatory prayer's image of judgment defanging evil. Ephesians 6:16: "take up the shield of faith, with which you can extinguish all the flaming arrows of the evil one." The arrows that fall harmless in Psalm 58:7 are the arrows extinguished by the shield of faith — a defense available only through the victory Christ has already won over the powers.
58.8: A shadow: "Let them be like a snail that dissolves as it goes, like a stillborn child that never sees the light of the sun." The imagery of futility — the snail that disappears in its own trail, the stillborn that never sees the light — is the Psalm's most vivid picture of those who never participate in the life of the coming age. John 3:19-20: "Light has come into the world, but people loved darkness instead of light because their deeds were evil... everyone who does evil hates the light." Those who "never see the light of the sun" in Psalm 58:8 are those who reject the Light of the world (John 8:12) — self-chosen non-participation in the resurrection's light.
58.9: A shadow: "Before your cooking pots have felt the heat of the thornwood — both green and dry — he will whirl them away." The sudden divine judgment that comes before human preparations are complete — before the pot even heats — is the Psalm's imagery of the unexpectedness of divine action against the violent. Luke 12:20: "You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you." The divine whirlwind that sweeps away the wicked before they complete their plans is the eschatological judgment that Christ both announces and will execute at his return (Matt 24:27: "For as lightning comes from the east and is visible even in the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man").
58.10: A revelation of God: "The righteous will rejoice when they see justice carried out; they will wash their feet in the blood of the wicked." The eschatological rejoicing of the righteous at divine justice — a visceral image of vindication — must be understood within ANE warfare imagery. Revelation 19:2: "for true and just are his judgments. He has condemned the great prostitute who corrupted the earth by her adulteries. He has avenged on her the blood of his servants." The righteous rejoicing at divine justice in Psalm 58:10 is the heavenly worship of Revelation 19 — the declaration that YHWH's judgments are "true and just" as the vindication of the martyred righteous.
58.11: A revelation of God: "Then people will say, 'There really is a reward for the righteous; truly there is a God who judges the earth.'" The eschatological confession — that divine governance of the earth is real, that righteousness is rewarded — is the conclusion the Psalm drives toward. Acts 17:31: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The "God who judges the earth" of Psalm 58:11 acts through the one raised from the dead — Christ as the appointed judge whose resurrection proves the eschatological governance of YHWH is real.

59.1: A theme: Psalm 59 is a Michtam of David when Saul sent men to watch the house and kill him (1 Sam 19:11-17). The historical specificity of the superscription makes this a "David as fugitive" psalm. "Rescue me from my enemies, O my God; set me beyond the reach of those who rise up against me." The prayer for rescue from organized, state-sponsored assassination is the prayer that Christ's community prays under persecution. Hebrews 13:6: "So we say with confidence, 'The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?'" Christ himself was the object of organized state violence (Pilate + Herod + Sanhedrin) — and was rescued through the resurrection.
59.2: A theme: "Rescue me from those who do evil, and save me from men who want my blood." The bloodthirst of the evildoers — men who specifically "want my blood (ʾanšê dāmîm)" — is the Passion's language. Matthew 27:25: "His blood is on us and on our children!" — the very blood that the crowd invokes is the blood of the new covenant (Matt 26:28: "This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins"). The men who wanted Christ's blood, in demanding it, unwittingly invoked its redemptive power upon themselves.
59.3: A type: "For see — they lie in ambush for my life; fierce men are gathering against me — not for any offense or sin of mine, LORD." The declaration of innocence — no offense or sin on the Psalmist's part justifying the attack — is fulfilled literally in Christ. John 19:4: "Pilate announced again, 'Look, I am bringing him out to you to let you know that I find no basis for a charge against him.'" Luke 23:47: "the centurion... said, 'Surely this was a righteous man.'" The innocence-before-attackers of Psalm 59:3 is not merely David's aspirational claim but Christ's literal truth: no offense, no sin, yet surrounded.
59.4: A type: "For no fault of mine they have mobilized against me. Arouse yourself, come to meet me, and look!" The prayer "arouse yourself, come to meet me" is the prayer for divine waking — YHWH rousing himself from apparent inactivity in the face of innocent suffering. This prayer appears to be unanswered at the cross — God seems asleep. But Acts 2:24: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." The divine self-rousing that the Psalm prays for is the resurrection — YHWH "coming to meet" the innocent one entombed by his enemies.
59.5: A revelation of God: "You are the LORD God of hosts, the God of Israel — rouse yourself to punish all the nations; show no mercy to those who commit treason with evil intent." The invocation of YHWH Ṣĕbāʾôt ("LORD of hosts/armies") is the divine warrior title that grounds the prayer for just punishment of treacherous nations. The NT situates this judgment in Christ: Revelation 19:11-16 presents Christ as the divine warrior on the white horse, "KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS" — the YHWH of hosts who now rides in final judgment through his anointed Son. The nations who committed treason against the Messiah face the LORD of hosts through Christ.
59.6: A shadow: "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling around the city." The nocturnal surveillance — prowling, howling, circling — is the imagery of predatory persistent threat. John 18:3: "So Judas came to the garden, guiding a detachment of soldiers and some officials from the chief priests and the Pharisees. They were carrying torches, lanterns and weapons." The evening arrival at Gethsemane with torches and weapons is the fulfillment of the nocturnal predators of Psalm 59:6 — they come at night, circling the place of prayer.
59.7: A shadow: "Listen — they pour out venom with their mouths; their lips are full of swords, for they think, 'Who is going to hear?'" The self-confident secrecy of those who conspire with weapon-words — "who is going to hear?" — is the arrogance of those who believe their plans are hidden from YHWH. Matthew 26:3-4 records the Sanhedrin plotting in the high priest's palace — in secret, they believe. But Acts 2:23 reveals the divine surveillance: God's "deliberate plan and foreknowledge" covered even this secret conspiracy. The "who is going to hear?" of Psalm 59:7 is answered: YHWH heard and incorporated it into the redemptive plan.
59.8: A revelation of God: "But you, LORD, laugh at them; you scoff at all the nations." The divine laughter at those who conspire against the anointed is the exact phrase of Psalm 2:4 ("He who sits in the heavens laughs; the Lord scoffs at them"). Psalm 2 is explicitly Messianic (2:2 "against the LORD and against his Anointed"). The divine laugh in Psalm 59:8 at those who surrounded David with weapons is the same divine scoff at those who surrounded Christ with torches and arrested him — in the absolute confidence that YHWH's plan would not be derailed by human weapons and conspiracies.
59.9: A revelation of God: "O my Strength, I will wait on you; for God is my fortress." The divine title "my Strength" (ʿuzzî) and "my fortress" (miśgabbî) establishes YHWH as the source of both active power and defensive security. 2 Corinthians 12:9-10: "My power is made perfect in weakness... For when I am weak, then I am strong." The paradox that Paul articulates — weakness as the context for divine strength — is the shape of Christ's entire ministry: the vulnerable incarnate one waiting on the Father, whose power is made perfect through and despite weakness.
59.10: A revelation of God: "The God of my steadfast love will come ahead of me; God will let me look in triumph on my enemies." The divine precedence — YHWH going ahead of the Psalmist — and the promised triumph-view over enemies is the covenant military pattern. The resurrection is the divine going-ahead: Christ rises before the disciples see him; the angels precede the disciples to the tomb (Matt 28:6: "He has risen, just as he said"). Romans 8:37: "in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us." The God who "comes ahead" to grant triumph is the Father who raised Christ first, enabling the community's triumph through him.
59.11: A shadow: "Do not kill them, so my people will not forget; bring them down by your power and humble them, O Lord our shield!" The prayer to humiliate rather than annihilate — so the lesson is not lost — is the Psalm's pedagogical approach to justice. The NT's vision of eschatological judgment includes a memorial function: Revelation 20:12, "the dead were judged according to what they had done as recorded in the books." The divine remembering and recording that makes judgment pedagogically meaningful is the same divine attentiveness that ensures the enemies of the Messiah do not simply disappear — their accountability stands.
59.12: A shadow: "For the sin of their mouths and the words of their lips, let them be ensnared in their own pride. For the cursing and deception that they utter." The judgment corresponding to weapon-speech — "ensnared in their own pride" — is the justice principle of Proverbs 18:7: "The mouths of fools are their undoing." At the trial of Jesus, the false witnesses contradict each other (Mark 14:56-59), the high priest tears his robes in a forbidden act (Lev 21:10, making him ritually impure), and Pilate's verdict is written in three languages as a permanent testimony (John 19:20). The conspirators are repeatedly ensnared by their own words and actions.
59.13: A revelation of God: "Consume them in your wrath; consume them until they are no more, so that people may know that God rules over Jacob to the ends of the earth." The eschatological consumption of the enemies is not vindictiveness but revelation: the goal is that all the earth know that God rules. Acts 17:31 frames the judgment through Christ as the demonstration of divine governance: "He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." The resurrection is the "so that people may know that God rules" of Psalm 59:13 — the proof of YHWH's sovereignty enacted through the Messiah.
59.14: A shadow: "They return each evening, howling like dogs and circling the city." The refrain of v6 repeated is the Psalm's structural reminder that the threat is persistent — it returns each evening, not a one-time crisis. The sustained, returning opposition to Christ across his ministry (John 7:1, 7:32, 8:59, 10:31, 11:53) is this same pattern: each new encounter brings renewed attempts to arrest or kill him. The howling-dogs-circling-city is the accumulated hostility that the Gospels chronicle over three years of ministry.
59.15: A theme: "They roam around in search of food; they growl when they are not satisfied." The predatory unsatisfied hunger of the enemies — prowling, growling without satiation — is the portrait of those whose hunger for power and control cannot be fulfilled. Luke 23:23: "But with loud shouts they insistently demanded that he be crucified, and their shouts prevailed." The crowd's insatiable demand — demanding more (crucifixion after flogging, thorns, and mockery were not enough) — is the unsatisfied hunger of Psalm 59:15, whose appetite grows with feeding.
59.16: A revelation of God: "But I will sing of your strength; I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning; for you have been my fortress and my place of refuge in my day of distress." The morning singing of divine strength and steadfast love — the dawn of praise after the night of howling enemies — is the resurrection-morning image. Mark 16:2: "Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they were on their way to the tomb." The morning of the resurrection is the "morning" of Psalm 59:16 — the dawn after the dark night of the Passion in which the community sings of strength and steadfast love.
59.17: A revelation of God: "O my Strength, I will sing praise to you; for God is my fortress, the God who shows me steadfast love." The closing declaration — God as Strength, Fortress, and Steadfast Love — completes the Psalm with a triple assertion of divine character against which no enemy prevails. These three divine attributes are embodied in Christ: 1 Corinthians 1:24 ("Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God"), Ephesians 2:14 ("he himself is our peace"), and Romans 8:39 ("nothing will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord"). The Psalm's closing divine portrait is the character of the Father as expressed in the Son.

60.1: A revelation of God: Psalm 60 is a communal lament after military defeat — historically connected to the Aramean campaigns of 2 Samuel 8 and 10. "O God, you have rejected us and broken our defenses; you have been angry — restore us!" The experience of divine rejection in military defeat is the covenant community's crisis. The NT community's lament is structured similarly: Romans 8:36 (quoting Ps 44:22: "For your sake we face death all day long") — the apparent rejection by God is reframed in the resurrection. The restoration prayer ("restore us") is answered in Christ who takes the covenant people through apparent rejection to vindication.
60.2: A revelation of God: "You have made the earth shake; you have cracked it open; heal its fractures, for it is collapsing." The earthquake of divine judgment on the covenant community — the ground splitting under them — echoes the creation accounts where the earth is the arena of divine action. At the crucifixion, Matthew 27:51: "the earth shook, the rocks split." The earthquake of Psalm 60:2 is a type of the cosmic disturbance of the cross — the moment when YHWH's judgment-and-salvation falls in a way that shakes the earth literally. The prayer "heal its fractures" is answered in the new creation (Rev 21:5: "I am making everything new!").
60.3: A theme: "You have put your people through severe trials; you have made us drink disorienting wine." The wine of disorientation — the divine cup of judgment that produces incoherence — is the Passion's own language. Mark 14:36: "Take this cup from me." John 18:11: "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?" Christ drinks the cup of divine judgment that the covenant people could not bear — 2 Corinthians 5:21: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us." The "disorienting wine" of Psalm 60:3 is the cup that Christ drinks so his people do not have to.
60.4: A direct connection: "You have set up a banner (nēs) for those who fear you, for them to rally to in the face of the enemy's bow." The battle-banner or standard (nēs) raised for the fearers to rally around is a theme that runs through Isaiah: 11:12 ("He will raise a banner for the nations and gather the exiles of Israel"), 49:22 ("I will lift up my banner to the peoples"). Isaiah 11:10 uses the same term for the Messianic Root of Jesse as the banner for the nations — cited by Paul in Romans 15:12. Most directly: John 3:14, "Just as Moses lifted up the snake in the desert, so the Son of Man must be lifted up" — Christ crucified is the nēs raised for all who fear God to rally around.
60.5: A revelation of God: "Save your dear ones — rescue them by your right hand, and answer our prayer." The prayer for salvation "by your right hand" is the Exodus language: Exodus 15:6, "Your right hand, LORD, was majestic in power; your right hand, LORD, shattered the enemy." Acts 2:33: "Exalted to the right hand of God, he has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit." The "right hand" that saves is the right hand where Christ now sits — the one who is both the agent of the right-hand rescue and the one who occupies that position of power on behalf of his people.
60.6: A revelation of God: "God has spoken in his holiness: 'I will triumph; I will divide up Shechem and portion out the Valley of Succoth.'" The divine oracle of triumph — YHWH declaring victory over the territories — is the covenant-land speech pattern. The NT applies the territorial-triumph language eschatologically: Revelation 21:1-4 (the new heaven and earth), and Matthew 28:18 ("all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me"). The "I will triumph" of the divine oracle is fulfilled in Christ who has been given all authority — the triumph over the nations that Psalm 60:6 announces is his.
60.7: A direct connection: "'Gilead is mine, Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter.'" "Judah is my scepter (mĕḥōqĕqî)" — the lawgiver/commander's staff belonging to Judah — directly echoes Genesis 49:10: "The scepter will not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until he to whom it belongs shall come and the obedience of the nations shall be his." Revelation 5:5: "See, the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has triumphed." Christ is the one to whom the scepter of Judah belongs — the fulfillment of Jacob's blessing and the divine oracle of Psalm 60:7.
60.8: A shadow: "'Moab is my washbasin; over Edom I throw my sandal; over Philistia I shout in triumph.'" The dismissive, humiliating language about the surrounding enemies — Moab as a basin, Edom receiving a thrown sandal — is the imagery of total suzerainty. The nations around Israel that opposed the Davidic kingdom are types of all powers hostile to the Messianic reign. Revelation 19:15: "He will rule them with an iron scepter. He treads the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty." The contemptuous dominion of Psalm 60:8 is the eschatological dominion of Christ over all hostile powers.
60.9: A theme: "Who will bring me into the fortified city? Who will guide me all the way to Edom?" The military question — what human commander can conquer the impregnable fortress? — is the admission of human inadequacy before the task. John 15:5: "apart from me you can do nothing." The community that cannot take the fortified city by its own power needs a guide and captain. Hebrews 2:10 calls Christ the "pioneer of their salvation" — the archēgos who leads the community through what they cannot accomplish themselves. The guide to Edom is the one who has already gone ahead.
60.10: A revelation of God: "Have you not rejected us, O God? You no longer go out, God, with our armies." The apparent divine absence from the covenant community's military struggles is the lament at the center of Psalm 60. This is the "dark night" of the Psalm — God seems to have abandoned the enterprise. Romans 8:31-32: "If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?" The apparent abandonment of God going out with the armies is resolved in Christ: God's "going out" with the covenant community is now his going out in Christ, through the Spirit.
60.11: A revelation of God: "Give us help against the enemy, for human deliverance is worthless." The definitive confession of human inability — "human deliverance is worthless (šāwʾ tĕšûʿat ʾādām)" — is the OT anti-humanism that grounds the necessity of divine salvation. Acts 4:12: "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." The "worthless" human deliverance of Psalm 60:11 is the backdrop against which the name of Jesus stands out as the one non-human, non-worthless salvation available. Christ alone is the help against the enemy that no human power can supply.
60.12: A revelation of God: "Through God we will do great deeds; he himself will crush our enemies." The final declaration of divine-human cooperation — "through God" (bēʾlōhîm) great deeds, but YHWH himself doing the crushing — is the Christological framework of the NT. Philippians 4:13: "I can do all this through him who gives me strength." Colossians 2:15: "And having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross." The "through God we do great deeds / he himself crushes our enemies" of Psalm 60:12 is the cross: the community works through Christ; Christ crushes the enemies by his own action at Golgotha.

61.1: A theme: Psalm 61 is a brief royal prayer for the king's long life, attributed to David. "Hear my cry, O God; listen to my prayer." The simplicity of the opening petition — hear, listen — is the model of prayer that Christ teaches (Matt 6:9: "Our Father in heaven"). Hebrews 5:7: "During the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission." The "hear my cry" of Psalm 61:1 is the prayer posture that Christ himself occupied — and the Father heard.
61.2: A direct connection: "From the ends of the earth I call out to you when my heart grows faint; lead me to the rock that is higher than I." The "rock that is higher than I (ṣûr yārûm mimmennî)" — the inaccessible rock that only God can reach — is the Christological image that 1 Corinthians 10:4 makes explicit: "they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ." The rock higher than the Psalmist is the one the Psalmist cannot reach by himself — and which, in Christ, descends to the level of human accessibility. The high rock comes down; the Incarnation is the rock becoming reachable.
61.3: A revelation of God: "For you have been a shelter for me, a strong tower against the enemy." The strong tower as divine protection recalls Proverbs 18:10: "The name of the LORD is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe." In the NT, the "strong tower" is the one who prays for his people: John 17:15, "My prayer is not that you take them out of the world but that you protect them from the evil one." Christ as the sheltering tower for his community — the intercessor who prays for their protection — fulfills the imagery of Psalm 61:3 in the most personal way possible.
61.4: A theme: "I will dwell in your tent forever; I will take refuge under the shelter of your wings." The desire for permanent divine dwelling — "forever" (ʿôlāmîm) — is the eschatological longing beneath every lament. John 14:2-3: "My Father's house has many rooms... I am going there to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and take you to be with me that you also may be where I am." The permanent dwelling in the divine tent of Psalm 61:4 is the "room in the Father's house" that Christ prepares — the eschatological shelter under divine wings that his people will inhabit forever.
61.5: A revelation of God: "For you, O God, have heard my vows; you have given me the inheritance of those who fear your name." The inheritance of the fearers of YHWH — given as the answer to heard prayer — is the covenant gift. Ephesians 1:11: "In him we were also chosen, having been predestined according to the plan of him who works out everything in conformity with the purpose of his will." And 1:14: "who is a deposit guaranteeing our inheritance." Christ is the guarantee of the inheritance — the one through whom the inheritance that God "gives to those who fear his name" is secured for all who are in him.
61.6: A direct connection: "You will extend the king's days; his years shall span many generations." The royal prayer for the king's longevity is a standard element of ancient Near Eastern royal liturgy — but it reaches its literal fulfillment only in Christ. Luke 1:33: "he will reign over Jacob's descendants forever; his kingdom will never end." Hebrews 7:24-25: "because Jesus lives forever, he has a permanent priesthood. Therefore he is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to intercede for them." The "many generations" of the king's years in Psalm 61:6 is fulfilled in the one whose years literally span eternity.
61.7: A direct connection: "May he dwell before God forever; appoint steadfast love and faithfulness to guard him." The king's permanent presence before God — and the divine appointment of ḥesed and ʾĕmet (steadfast love and faithfulness) as his personal guard — is fulfilled in Christ's ascension and present session. Hebrews 9:24: "Christ did not enter a sanctuary made with human hands... he entered heaven itself, now to appear for us in God's presence." Christ "dwells before God forever" — in the heavenly sanctuary, in the presence of the Father, perpetually interceding. And John 1:14: "full of grace and truth" — steadfast love and faithfulness personified, guarding the one who is their embodiment.
61.8: A revelation of God: "So I will sing praise to your name forever, as I fulfill my vows day after day." The eternal praise — name-singing that does not stop — and the daily vow-fulfillment are the liturgical response to the divine faithfulness that Psalm 61 celebrates. Revelation 5:13: "Then I heard every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and on the sea, and all that is in them, saying: 'To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and power, for ever and ever!'" The "forever" of Psalm 61:8's praise is enacted in the eternal worship of Christ the Lamb — the praise that all creation sustains without ceasing around the throne of the one who extended the King's days forever.

68.1: shadow God shall arise, his enemies shall be scattered; and those who hate him shall flee before him! The resurrection is God arising: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it (Acts 2:24). Death and its author scatter before the risen Christ — he disarmed the rulers and authorities (Col 2:15), routing them as the enemies flee at God's arising.
68.2: shadow As smoke is driven away, so you shall drive them away; as wax melts before fire, so the wicked shall perish before God! The wicked melting before God — eschatological imagery: every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be made low (Luke 3:5); the elements will melt with fervent heat (2 Pet 3:10). The wicked who opposed Christ are driven away at his vindication.
68.3: shadow But the righteous shall be glad; they shall exult before God; they shall be jubilant with joy! The joy of the resurrection community: though you have not seen him, you love him. Though you do not now see him, you believe in him and rejoice with joy that is inexpressible and filled with glory (1 Pet 1:8). The righteous exult before God through Christ who is their righteousness (Phil 3:9).
68.4: shadow Sing to God, sing praises to his name; lift up a song to him who rides through the deserts; his name is the LORD; exult before him! The one who rides through the deserts is the rider on the white horse (Rev 19:11-16) — Christ whose name is the Word of God (Rev 19:13). The LORD whose name is lifted up is the Lord Jesus, at whose name every knee bows (Phil 2:9-10).
68.5: revelation of God Father of the fatherless and protector of widows is God in his holy habitation. God's fatherly care for the vulnerable is most fully revealed in Christ, who ministered to the widow of Nain (Luke 7:11-15), honored the widow's offering (Luke 21:1-4), and entrusted his mother to the beloved disciple from the cross (John 19:26-27). James grounds care for orphans and widows in God's holy character (James 1:27).
68.6: shadow God settles the solitary in a home; he leads out the prisoners to prosperity, but the rebellious dwell in a parched land. Christ's Nazareth sermon cites Isaiah's prisoner-release as his own mission: he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed (Luke 4:18, citing Isa 61:1). The Ascension text in Eph 4:8 connects to v18 of this psalm — the same liberating theme.
68.7: type O God, when you went out before your people, when you marched through the wilderness, The Exodus march prefigures Christ leading his people through the wilderness of this age. Paul identifies the wilderness generation's experience with Christ: they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). The divine march through the wilderness is Christ's own pattern of faithfulness through suffering.
68.8: type the earth quaked, the heavens poured down rain, before God, the One of Sinai, before God, the God of Israel. Sinai's theophany — quaking, rain, fire — is the pattern the NT applies to the Passion and Parousia: earthquake at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51), fire at Pentecost (Acts 2:3), cosmic signs at the consummation (Rev 6:12-17). The God of Sinai is the God whose glory shines in the face of Christ (2 Cor 4:6).
68.9: type Rain in abundance, O God, you shed abroad; you restored your inheritance as it languished; The abundant rain as divine provision — the Spirit poured out at Pentecost like rain: in the last days... I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh (Acts 2:17, citing Joel 2:28). Christ restores the languishing inheritance by sending the Spirit of adoption (Gal 4:6) to renew God's people.
68.10: shadow your flock found a dwelling in it; in your goodness, O God, you provided for the needy. Christ the Good Shepherd provides for the needy flock: I am the good shepherd... I lay down my life for the sheep (John 10:11). The dwelling the flock finds is the Father's house: in my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you (John 14:2).
68.11: shadow The Lord gives the word; the women who announce the news are a great host: The first proclaimers of the resurrection were women — a great host carrying the news: Go quickly and tell his disciples that he has risen from the dead (Matt 28:7). The Lord gives the word of resurrection and chooses women as the first heralds — the great host of those who announce the good news.
68.12: shadow The kings of the armies — they flee, they flee! — the women at home divide the spoil. The kings who fled at Christ's resurrection — death, sin, the powers — are routed. The spoil divided is the grace poured out: to each one of us grace was given according to the measure of Christ's gift (Eph 4:7), which Paul grounds in v18 of this psalm about the Ascension and its gifts.
68.13: shadow though you men lie among the sheepfolds — the wings of a dove covered with silver, its pinions with shimmering gold. The dove of peace and beauty — the Spirit descending as a dove at Christ's baptism (Matt 3:16). The shimmering silver and gold of the dove's wings reflect the transfiguration glory: his clothes became radiant, intensely white (Mark 9:3).
68.14: shadow When the Almighty scatters kings there, let snow fall on Zalmon. The scattering of kings at the Ascension and parousia: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matt 28:18). The transformation of dark mountains to gleaming snow mirrors the resurrection transformation — what was dead and dark becomes luminous under the power of God.
68.15: shadow O mountain of God, mountain of Bashan; O many-peaked mountain, mountain of Bashan! The many-peaked mountain — a type of Zion, the mountain of God's presence. Christ as the new Zion: you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem (Heb 12:22). The mountain where God dwells is now the risen Christ — the meeting place of heaven and earth.
68.16: shadow Why do you look with hatred, O many-peaked mountain, at the mount that God desired for his abode, yes, where the LORD will dwell forever? God's chosen dwelling is not the impressive peaks but the humble mount — Zion, and ultimately Christ himself: the one whom God has chosen and precious... a cornerstone chosen and precious (1 Pet 2:4-6). The unexpected, humble mountain is where God forever dwells.
68.17: shadow The chariots of God are twice ten thousand, thousands upon thousands; the Lord is among them; Sinai is now in the sanctuary. The Lord among his innumerable heavenly armies — Christ who said he could call twelve legions of angels (Matt 26:53) but chose not to. At the Ascension, Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary (Heb 9:12) where ten thousand times ten thousand angels surround the throne (Rev 5:11).
68.18: direct You ascended on high, leading a host of captives in your train and receiving gifts among men, even among the rebellious, that the LORD God may dwell there. Ephesians 4:8 quotes and transforms this verse to describe Christ's Ascension: When he ascended on high he led a host of captives and gave gifts to men. Paul reads the Ascension as the fulfillment of this victory march — Christ ascending to the Father's right hand and distributing the Spirit's gifts to the church.
68.19: shadow Blessed be the Lord, who daily bears us up; God is our salvation. Christ daily bearing his people: his continual intercession at the Father's right hand (Rom 8:34; Heb 7:25 — he always lives to make intercession for them). God is our salvation — Acts 4:12: there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.
68.20: shadow Our God is a God of salvation, and to GOD, the Lord, belong deliverances from death. Deliverances from death belong supremely to the resurrection: God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him (Acts 2:24). The plural deliverances encompasses not only Christ's resurrection but the resurrection of all who are in him.
68.21: shadow But God will strike the heads of his enemies, the hairy crown of him who walks in his guilty ways. The ultimate defeat of Satan — the serpent whose head is struck: the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet (Rom 16:20). Christ crushes the head of the enemy (Gen 3:15) through the cross, where the one who walks in guilty ways against the righteous one is condemned (Col 2:15).
68.22: shadow The Lord said, I will bring them back from Bashan, I will bring them back from the depths of the sea, Bringing back from the depths — the resurrection. Christ descends into the depths of death (Eph 4:9 — the lower regions of the earth) and is brought back: who will descend into the abyss? (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead) (Rom 10:7). From Bashan to the sea's depths, no depth is beyond the reach of resurrection.
68.23: shadow that you may strike your feet in their blood, that the tongues of your dogs may have their portion from the foe. The judgment imagery of the victorious return — the eschatological battle of Revelation (Rev 19:17-21). The graphic language of victory over those who opposed the righteous one is the language of divine vindication at the last day, when the one who was rejected returns as conqueror.
68.24: shadow Your procession is seen, O God, the procession of my God, my King, into the sanctuary — The triumphant procession into the sanctuary — Christ's entry into the heavenly sanctuary with his own blood: he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption (Heb 9:12). The processional into God's presence is the Ascension.
68.25: shadow the singers in front, the musicians last, between them virgins playing tambourines: The worshiping procession that accompanies Christ into the sanctuary is the church — the great assembly gathered to praise. Heb 2:12 (citing Ps 22:22) shows Christ leading the praise in the midst of the congregation: he is both the processional leader and the chief singer of God's praises.
68.26: theme Bless God in the great congregation, the LORD, O you who are of Israel's fountain! The great congregation blessing God — the universal church gathered in Christ. Paul's vision: that together you may with one voice glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ (Rom 15:6). Israel's fountain is Christ himself — from his fullness we have all received, grace upon grace (John 1:16).
68.27: shadow There is Benjamin, the least of them, in the lead, the princes of Judah in their throng, the princes of Zebulun, the princes of Naphtali. The unity of the tribes under God's king — fulfilled in Christ who gathers the scattered children of God into one (John 11:52). The twelve tribes assembled become the twelve apostles and the new Israel, the church drawn from every tribe (Rev 7:4-8).
68.28: shadow Summon your power, O God, the power by which you have worked for us. The power of God summoned — the resurrection power: what is the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead (Eph 1:19-20). The power that raised Christ is the power working for his people.
68.29: shadow Because of your temple at Jerusalem kings shall bear gifts to you. The temple that draws kings to bring gifts is Christ himself — I tell you, something greater than the temple is here (Matt 12:6). The Magi bring gifts to the infant king (Matt 2:11); the kings of the earth bring their glory into the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:24). The universal tribute to Christ fulfills this verse.
68.30: shadow Rebuke the beasts that dwell among the reeds, the herd of bulls with the calves of the peoples. Trample underfoot those who lust after tribute; scatter the peoples who delight in war. Christ rebukes the powers — the beast imagery of Revelation connects here. He tramples those who delight in war through his cross (Col 2:15) and at the parousia scatters those who wage war against the Lamb (Rev 19:19-21).
68.31: shadow Nobles shall come from Egypt; Cush shall hasten to stretch out her hands to God. The nations streaming to God — Acts 8:26-40 (the Ethiopian eunuch from Cush who receives Philip's testimony and is baptized). Egypt and Cush as the ends of the earth reaching out to God finds its first fulfillment in the earliest Gentile conversions and its final fulfillment in Rev 7:9 (all nations before the throne).
68.32: shadow O kingdoms of the earth, sing to God; sing praises to the Lord, The kingdoms singing to Christ — the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever (Rev 11:15). The singing of the kingdoms anticipates the new song of the redeemed from every kingdom, tribe, and tongue (Rev 5:9).
68.33: revelation of God to him who rides in the heavens, the ancient heavens; behold, he sends out his voice, his mighty voice. Christ who rides the heavens: behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him (Rev 1:7). His mighty voice — the voice like the roar of many waters (Rev 1:15) that John hears at Patmos — is the voice of the one who rides through the heavens and speaks creation into being.
68.34: shadow Ascribe power to God, whose majesty is over Israel, and whose power is in the skies. Ascribing power to God is ascribing power to Christ, in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily (Col 2:9). His majesty over Israel and his power in the skies is the ascended Lord's authority: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matt 28:18).
68.35: revelation of God Awesome is God from his sanctuary; the God of Israel — he is the one who gives power and strength to his people. Blessed be God! The God who gives power from his sanctuary is the Christ who gives the Spirit: you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you (Acts 1:8). The awesome God from his sanctuary is the ascended Christ at the Father's right hand, blessing his people with every spiritual blessing (Eph 1:3).

69.1: shadow Save me, O God! For the waters have come up to my neck. The waters of chaos threatening life — the depth-imagery that Paul applies to Christ's descent: who will descend into the abyss? (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead) (Rom 10:7). Christ takes the drowning that the sinner deserves; the waters of judgment rise to his neck so they need not overwhelm those in him.
69.2: shadow I sink in deep mire, where there is no foothold; I have come into deep waters, and the flood sweeps over me. The deep mire and flood sweeping over — the desolation of the cross where Christ is immersed in judgment. He who walked on water (Matt 14:25) sinks into the mire of human sin so that those who sink might be raised with him. The flood that swept over him is the one Noah's flood typified (1 Pet 3:20-21).
69.3: shadow I am weary with my crying out; my throat is parched. My eyes grow dim with waiting for my God. The exhaustion of the cross: hours of crying out, throat parched — I thirst (John 19:28). Eyes growing dim waiting for God — the darkened hours of Golgotha (Matt 27:45-46) when God's presence seems withdrawn. Christ drinks the full cup of human abandonment and waits for the Father.
69.4: direct More in number than the hairs of my head are those who hate me without cause; mighty are those who would destroy me, those who attack me with lies. What I did not steal must I now restore? John 15:25 cites the without cause motif from this psalm (and Ps 35:19): they hated me without a cause — Jesus applies it directly to himself. The causeless hatred, the lying accusers, the demand that he restore what he did not take: all fulfilled in the trial and crucifixion.
69.5: shadow O God, you know my folly; the wrongs I have done are not hidden from you. A puzzling verse: Christ who had no folly or wrong. He speaks here as the one who has taken the position of the sinner — he made him to be sin who knew no sin (2 Cor 5:21). Standing in the sinner's place before God, he voices what belongs to us; God's knowledge of the wrong is answered by Christ's bearing of it.
69.6: shadow Let not those who hope in you be put to shame through me, O Lord GOD of hosts; let not those who seek you be dishonored through me, O God of Israel. Christ's intercession for his own: he bears shame so that they are not put to shame. Everyone who believes in him will not be put to shame (Rom 10:11, citing Isa 28:16). His prayer that those who hope in God not be dishonored through his suffering is answered by the resurrection.
69.7: shadow For it is for your sake that I have borne reproach, that dishonor has covered my face. Christ bore reproach for the Father's sake — Paul grounds this in v9b (Rom 15:3): Christ did not please himself but bore the reproach that fell on him for God's honor. The dishonor covering his face is the blindfolding and striking of the soldiers (Luke 22:64), the shame of the cross.
69.8: shadow I have become a stranger to my brothers, an alien to my mother's sons. Christ's alienation from family: not even his brothers believed in him (John 7:5). He became a stranger to his own (John 1:11 — he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him). The estrangement of the righteous sufferer from family is Christ's actual experience in his earthly ministry.
69.9: direct For zeal for your house has consumed me, and the reproaches of those who reproach you have fallen on me. Two direct citations: (1) John 2:17 — after the Temple cleansing, his disciples remembered that it was written, Zeal for your house will consume me. (2) Romans 15:3 — For Christ did not please himself, but as it is written, The reproaches of those who reproached you fell on me. Both halves of this verse are applied explicitly to Christ in the NT.
69.10: shadow When I wept and humbled my soul with fasting, it became my reproach. Christ's fasting in the wilderness (Matt 4:2) and his weeping (John 11:35; Luke 19:41) — acts of genuine humility and grief that are mocked. His weeping at Lazarus's tomb caused some to mock his apparent inability to prevent death (John 11:37). Humility before God becomes the occasion for human reproach.
69.11: shadow When I made sackcloth my clothing, I became a byword to them. The byword — Christ as the crucified Messiah who was a stumbling block to Jews and folly to Greeks (1 Cor 1:23). The sackcloth-wearer who is mocked is the righteous sufferer; the cross becomes the byword of shame that God transforms into the power and wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24).
69.12: shadow I am the talk of those who sit in the gate, and the drunkards make songs about me. The public mockery — at the gate (the place of judgment) and in the streets. Those who mocked Christ at the trial (Matt 26:67-68) and at the cross (Matt 27:39-44) made him the subject of their taunts. The drunkards' songs become the soldiers' mockery: Hail, King of the Jews!
69.13: shadow But as for me, my prayer is to you, O LORD. At an acceptable time, O God, in the abundance of your steadfast love answer me in your saving faithfulness. The acceptable time — Paul cites Isaiah's in an acceptable time I have listened to you (2 Cor 6:2) to describe the present age of salvation: Behold, now is the acceptable time. Christ's prayer is answered at the acceptable time — the resurrection on the third day.
69.14: shadow Deliver me from sinking in the mire; let me be delivered from my enemies and from the deep waters. The deliverance from deep waters and mire — the resurrection is Christ's deliverance from the depths of death. God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (Acts 2:24). The enemy death and the deep waters of judgment are both overcome in the resurrection of the one who prays here.
69.15: shadow Let not the flood sweep over me, or the deep swallow me up, or the pit close its mouth over me. The pit closing its mouth — death sealing its claim over Christ. But the tomb cannot hold him (Acts 2:24 — it was not possible for him to be held by it). The pit that closed its mouth over him on Good Friday opened at the resurrection; the deep that swallowed him disgorged its conqueror.
69.16: revelation of God Answer me, O LORD, for your steadfast love is good; according to your abundant mercy, turn to me. God's steadfast love (chesed) is the motivation of the atonement: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son (John 3:16); in this is love, not that we have loved God but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins (1 John 4:10). The abundant mercy that turns toward the afflicted one is made flesh in Christ.
69.17: shadow Hide not your face from your servant, for I am in distress; make haste to answer me. The hidden face at the cross — My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matt 27:46). The servant in distress is Christ who experiences the full weight of divine hiddenness so that his people need never experience it permanently. The resurrection is the face of God turned again toward the servant.
69.18: shadow Draw near to my soul, and redeem it; ransom me because of my enemies! God draws near to redeem by sending the Son: when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son... to redeem those who were under the law (Gal 4:4-5). The ransom paid is the blood of Christ — the Son of Man came... to give his life as a ransom for many (Matt 20:28).
69.19: shadow You know my reproach, and my shame and my dishonor; my foes are all known to you. God knows the reproach Christ bore — the shame of the cross, which Christ despised but endured (Heb 12:2). The Father's knowledge of his Son's dishonor is the ground of vindication: precisely because God knows, he acts in the resurrection to reverse every shame and honor the one who was dishonored.
69.20: shadow Reproaches have broken my heart, so that I am in despair. I looked for pity, but there was none, and for comforters, but I found none. The heart broken by reproach — perhaps literally (John 19:34 — blood and water from the pierced side suggests cardiac rupture). Comforters found none: disciples fled (Matt 26:56), Peter denied (Matt 26:69-75), the crowd mocked. The comfortless suffering is Christ's alone, so that he might be the Comforter through the Spirit (John 14:16).
69.21: direct They gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink. The most direct fulfillment in the Passion: John 19:28-29 — Jesus, knowing that all was now finished, said (to fulfill the Scripture), I thirst. A jar full of sour wine stood there, so they put a sponge full of the sour wine on a hyssop branch and held it to his mouth. Matthew 27:34,48; Mark 15:36; Luke 23:36 all record the sour wine offered at the cross — the precise fulfillment of Psalm 69:21.
69.22: direct Let their table become a snare before them; make their sacrificial feasts a trap. Romans 11:9-10 quotes verses 22-23 to explain Israel's stumbling: And David says, Let their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling block and a retribution for them. Paul applies this to those who rejected Christ — the covenant blessings becoming occasions of judgment for those who refused the Messiah.
69.23: direct Let their eyes be darkened, so that they cannot see, and make their loins tremble continually. Romans 11:10 continues the citation: let their eyes be darkened so that they cannot see, and bend their backs forever. Paul uses this verse (combined with v22) to explain the hardening of Israel that has opened the door for Gentile salvation — the stumbling of some for the fullness of others, until all Israel is saved (Rom 11:25-26).
69.24: shadow Pour out your indignation upon them, and let your burning anger overtake them. The divine wrath poured out — which Christ absorbed at the cross (Rom 3:25 — God presented him as a propitiation). The indignation that should fall on the sinner fell on the righteous one; those who reject his propitiating work face the undiluted wrath (Rev 14:10).
69.25: direct May their camp be desolate; let no one dwell in their tents. Acts 1:20 applies this verse directly to Judas: For it is written in the Book of Psalms, Let his homestead become desolate, and let there be no one to dwell in it. Peter explicitly cites Psalm 69:25 as the scriptural warrant for Judas's fate and the need to appoint a replacement.
69.26: shadow For they persecute him whom you have struck, and they recount the pain of those you have wounded. Those who persecuted Christ when God struck him — the crowd that joined in the Passion, Pilate who handed him over, the soldiers who struck the already-condemned one. Paul's citation context (Rom 11) situates this in the hardening of those who would not recognize God's own Messiah in his suffering.
69.27: shadow Add to them punishment upon punishment; may they have no acquittal from you. The imprecation against those who rejected the righteous one — read through Christ, this is the solemn warning of Hebrews: how much worse punishment will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God (Heb 10:29). The no-acquittal clause is the unforgivable sin of final rejection.
69.28: shadow Let them be blotted out of the book of the living; let them not be enrolled among the righteous. The book of the living — the book of life (Rev 3:5; 13:8; 20:15). Christ promises: the one who conquers will be clothed thus in white garments, and I will never blot his name out of the book of life (Rev 3:5). Those who reject him are not enrolled among the righteous; those who receive him have their names written permanently in the Lamb's book of life.
69.29: shadow But I am afflicted and in pain; let your salvation, O God, set me on high! The afflicted and pained one set on high — the Ascension as God's answer to the lament. Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name (Phil 2:9). The one who descended into the depths of pain is set on high above all creation.
69.30: shadow I will praise the name of God with a song; I will magnify him with thanksgiving. The praise that follows deliverance — Christ leading his brothers in praise (Heb 2:12, citing Ps 22:22). The song of thanksgiving that emerges from lament is the Easter hymn — the church's praise grounded in the resurrection of the one who was in pain and is now set on high.
69.31: shadow This will please the LORD more than an ox or a bull with horns and hoofs. The sacrifice God prefers over bulls and oxen — the obedient heart of Christ: sacrifices and offerings you have not desired... behold, I have come to do your will (Heb 10:5-7, citing Ps 40:6-8). Christ's willing self-offering pleases the Father more than all the animal sacrifices of the Levitical system combined.
69.32: shadow When the humble see it they will be glad; you who seek God, let your hearts revive. The humble who see and are glad — those who receive the resurrection news: but to each one of us grace was given (Eph 4:7). The hearts that revive are the hearts of the disciples who see the risen Lord (John 20:20 — the disciples were glad when they saw the Lord).
69.33: revelation of God For the LORD hears the needy and does not despise his own people who are prisoners. The God who does not despise prisoners is revealed in Christ who identifies with the prisoner: I was in prison and you came to me (Matt 25:36); the Spirit of the Lord is upon me... to proclaim liberty to the captives (Luke 4:18). He who is God does not despise his own people who are captive.
69.34: shadow Let heaven and earth praise him, the seas and everything that moves in them. The universal praise directed at God through Christ — Revelation's vision: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! (Rev 5:13).
69.35: shadow For God will save Zion and build up the cities of Judah, and people shall dwell there and possess it; The new Jerusalem built up by God through Christ — I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God (Rev 21:2). God saves Zion not through military might but through the Lamb — the one who was slain becomes the lamp of the new city (Rev 21:23).
69.36: shadow the offspring of his servants shall inherit it, and those who love his name shall dwell in it. The inheritance of the servants' offspring — the heirs of God and co-heirs with Christ (Rom 8:17). Those who love his name shall dwell in it: in my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you (John 14:2). The dwelling is prepared by the one whose name is loved — Christ, who secures the inheritance for the offspring of his servants.

70.1: shadow Make haste, O God, to deliver me! O LORD, make haste to help me! The urgent petition — the eschatological cry of the church awaiting Christ's return: Come, Lord Jesus! (Rev 22:20). Christ inhabits this urgency in Gethsemane where the hour of trial comes upon him; the church prays it in his name, longing for the deliverer to hasten his coming.
70.2: shadow Let them be put to shame and confusion who seek my life! Let them be turned back and brought to dishonor who delight in my hurt! The shaming of those who sought Christ's life — overturned at the resurrection. Those who delighted in his hurt at the cross are confounded by the empty tomb: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (Acts 2:24), bringing dishonor on every scheme devised against the righteous one.
70.3: shadow Let them turn back because of their shame who say, Aha, Aha! The Aha! of mockery at the cross (Mark 15:29 — the crowd wagging their heads and saying Aha!) is the specific taunt turned back on the mockers at the resurrection. The shame they intended for Christ rebounds on those who said it, as they cannot deny the empty tomb (Matt 28:11-15).
70.4: shadow May all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you! May those who love your salvation say evermore, God is great! The joy of those who seek God — the disciples' joy at the resurrection (John 20:20 — the disciples were glad when they saw the Lord). Those who love his salvation say evermore God is great — the church's doxology grounded in the resurrection, the definitive display of God's greatness.
70.5: shadow But I am poor and needy; hasten to me, O God! You are my help and my deliverer; O LORD, do not delay! Christ is the poor and needy one — though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor (2 Cor 8:9). From this poverty he cries for the Father to hasten, as in Gethsemane (if it be possible, let this cup pass, Matt 26:39). The Father hastens in resurrection; the church prays the same cry for his parousia.

75.1: A revelation of God: Psalm 75 is an Asaphite Psalm of communal thanksgiving framed around a divine oracle (vv2-5) interrupting the praise. "We give thanks to you, O God; we give thanks, for your name is near; we declare your wondrous deeds." The "nearness" of the divine name (qārôb šĕmekā) — YHWH's active presence rather than remote abstraction — is the ground of thanksgiving. John 1:14 presents Christ as the ultimate nearness of the divine name: the Word who "dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us." In Christ, the divine name is not merely near but personally embodied.
75.2: A direct connection: "When I choose the appointed time (mōʿēd), I will judge with perfect equity." This is the voice of YHWH speaking directly — the divine first-person oracle within the communal Psalm. The mōʿēd (appointed time, the same term used for the sacred calendar of Leviticus 23) is the eschatological judgment seat. Acts 17:31: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." Christ is both the appointed time's judge and the proof of the appointment: the resurrection establishes who judges at the mōʿēd.
75.3: A revelation of God: "When the earth totters and all its people with it, it is I who hold its pillars firm." The divine upholding of creation — the pillars of the earth held firm when everything else totters — is the sovereignty behind all apparent chaos. Hebrews 1:3: "The Son is the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of his being, sustaining all things by his powerful word." The divine upholding of the earth's pillars in Psalm 75:3 is the cosmic sustaining that Hebrews attributes to Christ — the one through whom and for whom all things were made (Col 1:16-17) holds everything together even when it appears to totter.
75.4: A revelation of God: "I say to the arrogant, 'Stop your boasting,' and to the wicked, 'Do not raise your horn.'" The divine rebuke of arrogance — the authoritative word that deflates boastful human power — is the pattern of Christ's confrontation with those who rely on worldly status. 1 Corinthians 1:27-29: "God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise; God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong... so that no one may boast before him." The divine anti-boasting of Psalm 75:4 reaches its climax in the cross, which shatters every human confidence except confidence in the crucified Messiah.
75.5: A revelation of God: "Do not raise your horn on high; do not speak with an arrogant neck." The "arrogant neck" (tĕdabbĕrû bĕṣawwār ʿātāq — speak with a stiff/insolent neck) is the idiom for the pride that refuses submission. James 4:6: "God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble." 1 Peter 5:5-6 applies this directly to the Christian community under Christ: "God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble. Humble yourselves, therefore, under God's mighty hand, that he may lift you up in due time." The divine anti-arrogance oracle of Psalm 75:5 is the wisdom that Christ embodies (Matt 11:29: "I am gentle and humble in heart") and instills in his community.
75.6: A revelation of God: "For advancement comes neither from east nor west, nor from the wilderness of the south." The negation of all human geographic sources of power — east, west, and south (the three compass points where human kingdoms originated: Babylon, Egypt, and the southern wilderness peoples) — is the Psalm's anti-human-politics declaration. Acts 4:12: "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." The "not from east, west, or south" of Psalm 75:6 becomes the "no other name" of Acts 4 — promotion/salvation comes from YHWH alone, enacted through Christ alone.
75.7: A revelation of God: "but God is the judge; he brings one person down and raises another up." The divine sovereignty over status — YHWH as the one who deposes and elevates — is the pattern of Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:52: "He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble") and of the resurrection itself. The ultimate enactment of Psalm 75:7 is Christ: brought down to death (the ultimate humiliation) and raised up by the Father (the ultimate elevation). Acts 2:33: "Exalted to the right hand of God" — YHWH raised the one who was brought down, reversing the human verdict.
75.8: A direct connection: "For in the hand of the LORD there is a cup of foaming wine, well mixed; he pours it out, and all the wicked of the earth shall drain it to the very dregs." The cup of divine wrath — the cup that the wicked drain to the dregs — is the cup that Christ prays to have removed: Mark 14:36, "Abba, Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will." John 18:11: "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?" Christ drinks the cup of Psalm 75:8 on behalf of those who deserve it — the wrath that should fall on the wicked falls on the one who takes their place. Revelation 14:10 and 16:19 show this cup finally poured out on those who reject the substitute who drank it.
75.9: A revelation of God: "But as for me, I will declare this forever; I will sing praises to the God of Jacob." The personal declaration of eternal praise amid the oracle of judgment is the doxological response to divine sovereignty. Hebrews 2:12 shows Christ himself as the leader of this eternal praise: "I will declare your name to my brothers and sisters; in the assembly I will sing your praises" (citing Ps 22:22). The "forever" of Psalm 75:9's praise is the unceasing worship that Christ leads in the community of the resurrection — the great assembly where the risen one leads the praise of the God who raised him.
75.10: A direct connection: "All the horns of the wicked I will cut down, but the horns of the righteous shall be lifted high." The horn imagery — power symbolized as the horn of an ox (Deut 33:17; 1 Sam 2:1) — reaches its Christological fulfillment in Zechariah's song at the birth of John the Baptist: Luke 1:69, "He has raised up a horn of salvation (keras sōtērias) for us in the house of his servant David." The divine promise of Psalm 75:10 — wicked horns cut, righteous horns lifted — is enacted in the resurrection: the horn of the crucified one (whose power was apparently broken) is raised as the definitive horn of salvation.

76.1: A revelation of God: Psalm 76 is an Asaphite Zion Psalm celebrating YHWH's defeat of an unnamed enemy army (possibly the Assyrian siege of 701 BC, Isaiah 36-37). "God is known in Judah; his name is great in Israel." The particularity of divine self-revelation — in Judah, in Israel — is the covenant specificity that the NT takes up and universalizes in Christ. Romans 3:2: "much in every way! First of all, the Jews have been entrusted with the very words of God." The knowledge of God that begins in Judah and Israel reaches all nations through Christ (John 4:22: "salvation is from the Jews").
76.2: A direct connection: "His tabernacle is in Salem; his dwelling place is in Zion." Salem (šālēm) = Jerusalem, but also the Hebrew word for peace/wholeness. Genesis 14:18 records that Melchizedek was king of Salem — king of peace. Hebrews 7:2 makes the connection explicit: "First, the name Melchizedek means 'king of righteousness'; then also, 'king of Salem' means 'king of peace.'" YHWH's dwelling in Salem in Psalm 76:2 is the pre-figuration of Christ as the Melchizedekian priest-king of Salem — the one whose priesthood is permanent because he is "king of peace" in the eternal Salem (Heb 12:22: the heavenly Jerusalem).
76.3: A direct connection: "There he shattered the flashing arrows, the shield, the sword, and all the weapons of war." The divine warrior breaking all weapons at Zion is the type of Christ's disarmament of spiritual powers at the cross. Colossians 2:15: "And having disarmed the powers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross." The weapons of sin, death, and the accusation of the law — the arrows, shields, and swords of the spiritual realm — are shattered by the cross at the true Zion. What Psalm 76:3 describes as a military victory is the cross's spiritual-warfare victory in which all weapons against the community of Christ are broken.
76.4: A revelation of God: "You are glorious and majestic, more than the everlasting mountains of plunder." The incomparable divine glory — exceeding the most permanent and impressive human accumulation — is the foundation of doxology. 1 Timothy 6:15-16: "God, the blessed and only Ruler, the King of kings and Lord of lords, who alone is immortal and who lives in unapproachable light... to him be honor and might forever." In Christ, this incomparable divine glory becomes visible (John 1:14: "we have seen his glory, the glory of the one and only Son"). The majestic glory of Psalm 76:4 is the glory beheld in the face of Christ (2 Cor 4:6).
76.5: A shadow: "The valiant warriors have been stripped of their plunder; they have fallen into the sleep of death; not one soldier could lift a hand." The defeat of the mighty — valiant warriors sleeping in death, hands unable to rise — is the pattern of eschatological judgment on all human power that opposes YHWH. 1 Corinthians 15:25-26: "For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death." The sleep of death that overcomes the mighty warriors of Psalm 76:5 is the condition that Christ's resurrection begins to reverse — death itself falling into the sleep from which it will never wake (Rev 20:14: "death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire").
76.6: A shadow: "At your rebuke, O God of Jacob, both chariot and horse fell into a dead sleep." The divine rebuke rendering the war-machine (chariot and horse, the symbols of military might from Egypt to Assyria) inert — a "dead sleep" — is the pattern of the Exodus (Exod 14:25: "He jammed the wheels of their chariots"). Christ rebukes the storm (Mark 4:39: "Quiet! Be still!") — demonstrating the authority to "rebuke" the forces of chaos and power that only YHWH possesses. The chariot-silencing of Psalm 76:6 is a type of Christ's authority over every force that appears overwhelming to human beings.
76.7: A revelation of God: "But you — you alone are to be feared. Who can stand before you when your anger is kindled?" The divine uniqueness in terror — YHWH alone worthy of ultimate fear — is the foundation of NT awe before the risen Christ. Revelation 6:15-17: "Then the kings of the earth, the princes, the generals, the rich, the mighty, and everyone else, both slave and free, hid in caves... 'Fall on us and hide us from the face of him who sits on the throne and from the wrath of the Lamb! For the great day of their wrath has come, and who can withstand it?'" The "who can stand?" of Psalm 76:7 is the question of Revelation's judgment: none can stand before the wrath of the Lamb.
76.8: A revelation of God: "From heaven you pronounced judgment; the earth feared and stood still." The heavenly origin of divine judgment — and the earth's arrested response — is the imagery of the last judgment. Matthew 24:30: "Then will appear the sign of the Son of Man in heaven. And then all the peoples of the earth will mourn when they see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven, with power and great glory." The earth that "feared and stood still" at YHWH's heavenly judgment in Psalm 76:8 is the earth that mourns and stands still at the Parousia of Christ — the divine judgment from heaven enacted through the Son of Man.
76.9: A revelation of God: "when God arose to judge, to save all the humble of the earth." The divine arising — YHWH rousing himself to act — for the dual purpose of judgment and salvation is the resurrection's shape. The resurrection is the divine "arising" that simultaneously judges (vindicating the innocent, condemning the conspirators) and saves (the humble who trust in the risen one). Luke 18:14: "everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and he who humbles himself will be exalted." The "humble of the earth" who are saved when God arises are those who entrust themselves to the one God raised — the humble who inherit the eschatological salvation.
76.10: A revelation of God: "Surely human fury will bring you praise; whatever wrath remains you will restrain." The divine sovereignty over human anger — even wrath brings YHWH praise, even the remaining wrath is restrained — is the paradox of the cross. Acts 4:27-28: "they did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." The human fury of the Passion — Judas's betrayal, the Sanhedrin's conspiracy, Pilate's capitulation, the crowd's violence — all brought YHWH praise through the resurrection. The wrath of human beings against the Messiah became the instrument of their salvation (1 Cor 2:8: "if they had known, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory").
76.11: A revelation of God: "Make vows to the LORD your God and keep them; let all the surrounding nations bring tribute to the one who is to be feared." The call for all nations to bring tribute (šay) to YHWH is the universal-worship vision of the Psalter (cf. Pss 2:8; 22:27-28; 72:10-11). Philippians 2:10-11: "that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord." The surrounding nations bringing tribute to the fearsome God of Psalm 76:11 is the eschatological worship of all nations before Christ — the fulfillment of the universal-tribute vision in the risen Lord who bears the divine name.
76.12: A revelation of God: "He cuts off the spirit of princes; he is fearsome to the kings of the earth." The divine authority over all human royalty — "cutting off the spirit" of rulers — is YHWH's absolute sovereignty. Revelation 1:5: "Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth." Christ is not merely subject to kings — he is their ruler. The "fearsome to the kings" of Psalm 76:12 is the eschatological reality: every earthly king stands under the authority of the one who is "ruler of the kings of the earth," whose kingdom "will never end" (Luke 1:33).

77.1: A theme: Psalm 77 is an Asaphite meditation on divine silence in crisis — moving from acute personal lament (vv1-9) to the resolution of remembering the Exodus (vv11-20). "I called out to God with my full voice; I cried to God, and he listened." The opening declares that God heard — but the rest of the Psalm shows the anguish of a time when he does not seem to respond. Hebrews 5:7: "he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission." Christ's crying to God who heard — through the apparent silence of the cross — is the ultimate enactment of Psalm 77:1.
77.2: A theme: "In the day of my trouble I sought the Lord; through the night my hand was outstretched without ceasing; my soul refused to be comforted." The night-long outreach without comfort — the persistent seeking that produces no obvious response — is the Gethsemane pattern. Luke 22:44: "And being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly, and his sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground." Three times Christ prays in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39-44); the "night with hand outstretched without ceasing" is the posture of the one who refuses to stop praying even when the cup is not removed.
77.3: A revelation of God: "I thought of God and I groaned; I poured out my grief until my spirit grew faint." The prayer that produces groaning rather than peace — thinking of God that intensifies rather than resolves the grief — is the honest register of the Psalm. Romans 8:26: "In the same way, the Spirit helps us in our weakness. We do not know what we ought to pray for, but the Spirit himself intercedes for us through wordless groans." The groaning of Psalm 77:3 becomes, in the NT, the Spirit's own intercession: the Spirit who indwells the community takes on the groaning that the Psalmist could not sustain, praying through Christ's community with the same intensity.
77.4: A revelation of God: "You kept my eyes wide open through the night; I was too troubled to speak." The divine-caused sleeplessness — God keeping the sufferer awake in crisis — is the anguish before redemption. In Gethsemane, Jesus asks his disciples to "keep watch" (Matt 26:41) while he prays through his own sleepless night of anguish. The inability to sleep before the redemptive act is the human reality of the incarnate Son: the night before the cross is the night of eyes kept wide open by the weight of what is coming.
77.5: A theme: "I think about the days of old, the years of long ago." The turn to historical memory as the resource for present faith — "days of old," "years of long ago" — is the Psalm's beginning of the turn toward the Exodus. The NT makes a parallel move: Hebrews 11's "hall of faith" surveys the "days of old" (Abraham through the judges) as the foundation for present faith. The Psalm's therapeutic memory ("I think about...") is what the church practices in the Lord's Supper: "Do this in remembrance of me" (Luke 22:19) — the redemptive past summoned as resource for the present.
77.6: A revelation of God: "I recall my song in the night; I ponder in my heart, and my spirit searches deeply." The night-song recalled — praise from an earlier rescue functioning as memory in the present crisis — is the Pauline pattern: "Rejoice always, pray continually, give thanks in all circumstances" (1 Thess 5:16-18). Acts 16:25: "About midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God" — in prison (the literal night), they recall and sing the song of faith. The spirit that "searches deeply" in Psalm 77:6 is the Spirit of Christ who "searches all things, even the deep things of God" (1 Cor 2:10).
77.7: A revelation of God: "Will the Lord reject us forever? Will he never again be pleased with us?" The first of five devastating theodicy-questions (vv7-9) — the apparent permanent rejection of the covenant community — is the question that the cross raises in its starkest form. Romans 8:31: "What, then, shall we say in response to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us?" The resurrection is the definitive "No!" to Psalm 77:7's question: God does not reject forever. The one who seemed most rejected (the crucified Messiah) was raised — the rejection is not permanent.
