Paul identifies the rock explicitly: they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4). The water from the split rock is the life-giving water Christ provides — if anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink (John 7:37). The wilderness rock split is a type of Christ's body broken to release living water.
78.16: type He made streams come out of the rock and caused waters to flow down like rivers. Streams from the rock — flowing into the new covenant: out of his heart will flow rivers of living water (John 7:38 — Jesus applies the imagery to the Spirit given through himself). The rock struck in the wilderness flows abundantly; the pierced side of Christ flows with blood and water (John 19:34).
78.17: type Yet they sinned still more against him, rebelling against the Most High in the desert. The wilderness rebellion as pattern and warning — Paul applies it directly to Christians tempted to repeat Israel's failures: we must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did and were destroyed by serpents (1 Cor 10:9). The rebellion against the Most High is now rebellion against Christ.
78.18: type They tested God in their heart by demanding the food they craved. Testing God by demanding food — the temptation Christ refused in the wilderness: man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from the mouth of God (Matt 4:4). He who is the bread of life (John 6:35) refused to turn stones to bread, overcoming what Israel failed.
78.19: type They spoke against God, saying, Can God spread a table in the wilderness? The question of wilderness provision recurs in John 6:31 — the crowd asks, What sign do you do?... Our fathers ate the manna in the wilderness. They too question whether God can provide — and Jesus answers by declaring himself the true bread from heaven (John 6:32-35). Can God spread a table? Christ is that table.
78.20: type He struck the rock so that water gushed out and streams overflowed. Can he also give bread, or provide meat for his people? The doubting question — answered by the feeding miracles. Christ feeds 5,000 (Matt 14:13-21) and 4,000 (Matt 15:32-38) in the wilderness setting, demonstrating that the one who gave water from the rock can also provide bread and meat, and is himself the bread that satisfies forever (John 6:35).
78.21: type Therefore, when the LORD heard, he was full of wrath; a fire was kindled against Jacob; his anger rose against Israel, Divine wrath in the wilderness — the backdrop for understanding the atonement. Christ bears the wrath that rose against rebellious Israel: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (Gal 3:13). The fire kindled against Jacob was ultimately quenched in Christ.
78.22: type because they did not believe in God and did not trust his saving power. Unbelief as the root of Israel's failure — and the sin Christ came to address. The Johannine refrain: this is the work of God, that you believe in him whom he has sent (John 6:29). Faith in Christ is the antidote to the unbelief that caused Israel to perish in the wilderness (Heb 3:19).
78.23: type Yet he commanded the skies above and opened the doors of heaven, The opened doors of heaven — fulfilled at Christ's baptism: the heavens were opened to him (Matt 3:16). The same opening that released manna in the wilderness is the opening at the Jordan that inaugurates Christ's ministry and declares him the beloved Son.
78.24: direct and he rained down on them manna to eat and gave them the grain of heaven. John 6:31 cites this verse as the crowd's justification for demanding a sign: He gave them bread from heaven to eat. Jesus responds: it was not Moses who gave you the bread from heaven, but my Father gives you the true bread from heaven. For the bread of God is he who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world (John 6:32-33). The manna of Ps 78:24 is the direct type Christ claims to fulfill.
78.25: direct Man ate of the bread of the angels; he sent them food in abundance. Jesus cites the abundance of the manna to make a contrast: Your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died... I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever (John 6:49-51). The bread of angels in abundance is surpassed by the bread of the Son of God who gives not physical life but eternal life.
78.26: type He caused the east wind to blow in the heavens, and by his power he led out the south wind; Divine control over winds — fulfilled in Christ who commands the winds: he rebuked the wind and the raging waves, and they ceased (Luke 8:24). The disciples asked, Who then is this? — the answer is the one who causes east and south winds to blow, the creator of weather now present in a boat.
78.27: type he rained meat on them like dust, winged birds like the sand of the seas; Abundance of provision — the eschatological feast theme. Christ at the feeding miracles provides from apparent scarcity; at the last supper he provides his own body and blood. The birds raining from heaven prefigure the Spirit's abundant gifts poured out through Christ at Pentecost.
78.28: type he let them fall in the midst of their camp, all around their dwellings. God's provision surrounding the camp — the Spirit surrounding the church: the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you (Rom 8:11). God's provision in Christ comes not from outside but from within, dwelling in the midst of his people.
78.29: type And they ate and were well filled, for he gave them what they craved. Satisfaction of craving — but the wilderness generation craved physical food, not the bread of life. Christ gives what the soul truly craves: blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied (Matt 5:6). He satisfies the deepest hunger with himself.
78.30: type But before they had satisfied their craving, while the food was still in their mouths, The insatiability of fleshly craving — the contrast with Christ who truly satisfies: everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again, but whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again (John 4:13-14). The manna could not satisfy permanently; Christ alone does.
78.31: type the anger of God rose against them, and he killed the strongest of them and laid low the young men of Israel. Divine judgment in the wilderness — the death of the strong as warning. Paul applies this pattern to the Corinthian church as a warning to those who presume on grace (1 Cor 10:5-11). The death of the strong in the wilderness is the type; the exhortation to those in Christ is not to fall by the same unbelief.
78.32: type In spite of all this, they sinned still more, and did not believe in his wondrous works. Persistent unbelief despite wonders — the pattern that reaches its climax in those who saw Christ's miracles and did not believe (John 12:37 — though he had done so many signs before them, they still did not believe in him). The hardening foretold in Isaiah (John 12:38-41) is the same pattern Israel displayed in the wilderness.
78.33: type So he made their days vanish like a breath, and their years in terror. The vanishing of days in the wilderness — the generation that perished without entering the land. Hebrews uses this as the supreme warning: take care, brothers, lest there be in any of you an evil, unbelieving heart, leading you to fall away from the living God (Heb 3:12). Christ is the rest the wilderness generation never entered (Heb 4:1-11).
78.34: type When he killed them, they sought him; they repented and sought God earnestly. Repentance under judgment — the pattern of genuine turning. Christ calls for this repentance: unless you repent, you will all likewise perish (Luke 13:3). The seeking under pressure is imperfect; Christ seeks those who turn to him permanently — the seeking shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine (Luke 15:4).
78.35: shadow They remembered that God was their rock, the Most High God their redeemer. Rock and Redeemer — both titles belonging to Christ. The rock is Christ (1 Cor 10:4); the Redeemer is Christ: in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses (Eph 1:7). When they remembered God as rock and redeemer, they were remembering the one who would be incarnate as Jesus of Nazareth.
78.36: type But they flattered him with their mouths; they lied to him with their tongues. Flattery concealing unbelief — the very pattern the religious leaders displayed before Christ. They approached him with empty honor (Matt 15:8 — this people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me), testing him with clever questions while plotting his death. Mouth-honor masking rejection.
78.37: type Their heart was not steadfast toward him; they were not faithful to his covenant. The unfaithful heart — the diagnosis of the old covenant problem that the new covenant addresses: I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you (Ezek 36:26). Christ is the mediator of the new covenant (Heb 9:15) who creates the steadfast heart Israel lacked.
78.38: shadow Yet he, being compassionate, atoned for their iniquity and did not destroy them; he restrained his anger often and did not stir up all his wrath. God's atoning compassion — the impulse that drives the Incarnation. The atonement restrained since Eden finds its full expression in Christ: God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them (2 Cor 5:19). Every act of divine patience in the wilderness was an advance on the cross.
78.39: shadow He remembered that they were but flesh, a wind that passes and comes not again. God's remembering of human frailty is the compassion that drives the Incarnation: since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things (Heb 2:14). Christ became flesh and dwelt among us — God remembering that we are flesh by entering that flesh himself.
78.40: type How often they rebelled against him in the wilderness and grieved him in the desert! Grieving the Spirit — the pattern Paul warns against: do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God (Eph 4:30). The wilderness rebellion that grieved God is the backdrop for understanding how Christ's people must not grieve the Spirit he has sent. The rebellion in the wilderness is now the potential rebellion in the church.
78.41: type They tested God again and again and provoked the Holy One of Israel. Testing God in the wilderness — Paul applies this to testing Christ: we must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did and were destroyed by serpents (1 Cor 10:9). The Holy One of Israel is Christ himself — the one whose name Isaiah declared and John applied to Jesus (John 12:41 — Isaiah saw Christ's glory).
78.42: type They did not remember his power — the day when he redeemed them from the foe, Forgetting the redemption — the failure that Christ's Supper was instituted to prevent: do this in remembrance of me (1 Cor 11:24-25). The Lord's Supper is the covenant meal that keeps the redemption in the community's memory, so that the church does not forget as the wilderness generation did.
78.43: type when he performed his signs in Egypt and his marvels in the fields of Zoan. The signs in Egypt — prefiguring Christ's sign ministry: Jesus did many other signs in the presence of the disciples, which are not written in this book; but these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ (John 20:30-31). John's Gospel is structured around signs that echo the Exodus pattern.
78.44: type He turned their rivers to blood, so that they could not drink of their streams. The plague of blood — a type of the covenant blood of Christ. Where the plague turned water to blood and brought death, Christ turns the water of his covenant to the life-giving blood of the new covenant (Matt 26:28 — my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins).
78.45: type He sent among them swarms of flies, which devoured them, and frogs, which destroyed them. The plagues of flies and frogs — the judgment on Egypt that foreshadows final judgment. Christ speaks of final judgment with equal severity: it is better for you to enter the kingdom of God with one eye than with two eyes to be thrown into hell (Mark 9:47). The plagues of Egypt are the shadow of the wrath Christ absorbs and the judgment he will finally administer.
78.46: type He gave their crops to the destroying locust and the fruit of their labor to the locust. The locust plague destroying the fruit of labor — the judgment on Egypt pointing to the eschatological locusts of Rev 9:1-11. Christ warns of the destruction of the fruitless tree (Matt 3:10; 21:19) and the parable of the vineyard taken from those who bear no fruit (Matt 21:41).
78.47: type He destroyed their vines with hail and their sycamore trees with frost. The hail destroying vines and trees — prefiguring the eschatological hail of Rev 8:7; 16:21. Christ's parable of the vine (John 15:1-8) shows him as the true vine; those not abiding in him are like the vines destroyed by hail — cut off and thrown into the fire (John 15:6).
78.48: type He gave over their cattle to the hail and their flocks to thunderbolts. God giving over — the language of Rom 1:24-28 (God gave them over to their desires). The plagues are the pattern of divine judgment that Paul analyzes theologically. Christ's death is the ultimate giving-over: he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all (Rom 8:32) — a giving-over of love instead of judgment.
78.49: type He let loose on them his burning anger, wrath, indignation, and distress, a company of destroying angels. The burning anger loosed on Egypt — the same categories of wrath that Christ absorbs at the cross (Rom 3:25 — propitiation; Gal 3:13 — bearing the curse). The destroying angels are restrained from the church by the blood of Christ, the Passover Lamb (1 Cor 5:7).
78.50: type He made a path for his anger; he did not spare them from death, but gave their lives over to the plague. Not sparing from death — contrasted with Christ: he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things? (Rom 8:32). God did not spare Egypt; he did not spare his own Son; but in not sparing Christ he now spares all who are in him.
78.51: type He struck down every firstborn in Egypt, the firstfruits of their strength in the tents of Ham. The death of the firstborn — the supreme Passover plague. Christ is the Passover Lamb who prevents the final firstborn-death from striking those sheltered by his blood (1 Cor 5:7). He is himself the firstborn: the firstborn of all creation (Col 1:15), the firstborn from the dead (Col 1:18).
78.52: shadow Then he led out his people like sheep and guided them in the wilderness like a flock. God leading his people as sheep — fulfilled in Christ the Good Shepherd: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep (John 10:11). The shepherd-king who leads Israel through the wilderness is the pattern Christ embodies; he guides his flock through the wilderness of this age.
78.53: shadow He led them in safety, so that they were not afraid, but the sea overwhelmed their enemies. Safe passage through danger as the enemies drown — the pattern of baptism (1 Cor 10:1-2; 1 Pet 3:20-21). Christ leads his people through the waters of death safely; their enemies (sin, death, the devil) are overwhelmed as Pharaoh's army was drowned. Those in Christ need not fear the waters.
78.54: shadow And he brought them to his holy land, the mountain that his right hand had won. The holy land won by God's right hand — the new creation won by Christ's right hand (Heb 1:3 — he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high). The inheritance the Exodus generation entered is the type of the eternal inheritance secured by Christ (Heb 9:15; 1 Pet 1:4).
78.55: type He drove out nations before them; he apportioned them for a possession and settled the tribes of Israel in their tents. The conquest as type — fulfilled eschatologically in Christ's victory over the principalities and powers (Col 2:15) and the ultimate settlement of God's people in the new creation (Rev 21:3 — Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man).
78.56: type Yet they tested and rebelled against the Most High God and did not keep his testimonies, Rebellion after settlement — the pattern of spiritual complacency. Hebrews warns the church against repeating this: see to it that no one fails to obtain the grace of God (Heb 12:15). Christ is the one who never rebelled, whose testimony was always kept — and in whom the rebellious find forgiveness.
78.57: type but turned away and acted treacherously like their fathers; they twisted like a deceitful bow. The twisted, unreliable bow — Israel's inconsistency. Contrasted with Christ who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Heb 13:8). He is the faithful witness (Rev 1:5), the one who does not twist or turn, whose yes is always yes (2 Cor 1:20).
78.58: type For they provoked him to anger with their high places; they moved him to jealousy with their idols. Idolatry provoking divine jealousy — Christ cleanses the temple as the act of divine jealousy for God's house (John 2:17 — zeal for your house has consumed me). The true God tolerates no rival; Christ is the true image of the invisible God (Col 1:15) that replaces all idols.
78.59: type When God heard, he was full of wrath, and he utterly rejected Israel. Divine rejection — not the final word in Israel's story or the church's. Paul wrestles with this: I ask, then, has God rejected his people? By no means! (Rom 11:1). The apparent rejection becomes the occasion for the Gentile mission; the final reconciliation comes through Christ (Rom 11:25-26).
78.60: shadow He forsook his dwelling at Shiloh, the tent where he dwelt among mankind, The forsaking of Shiloh — God departing from the sanctuary. Christ predicts the same departure from Jerusalem: See, your house is left to you desolate (Matt 23:38; Luke 13:35). But he is himself the new sanctuary — destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up (John 2:19). God does not ultimately abandon his dwelling; he moves it into Christ.
78.61: shadow and delivered his power to captivity, his glory to the hand of the foe. The glory delivered to the enemy — the cross as God's apparent defeat. Christ is delivered into the hands of sinners (Matt 26:45); God's power is handed over to crucifixion. But the delivery is the strategy: the weakness of God is stronger than men (1 Cor 1:25). The glory delivered to the foe is the glory that rises from the tomb.
78.62: shadow He gave his people over to the sword and vented his wrath on his heritage. Given over to the sword — the language of the atonement: he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all (Rom 8:32). Christ is the ultimate given over to the sword — the one on whom divine wrath is vented so that his heritage need not face it permanently.
78.63: type Fire devoured his young men, and his young women had no marriage song. The devastation of the youth — the tragedy of judgment. Christ mourns over Jerusalem which faces this judgment (Luke 19:41-44; Luke 23:27-31 — daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and your children). He warns of the coming fire that will devour the faithless city.
78.64: type His priests fell by the sword, and his widows made no lamentation. Priests falling by the sword — the reversal of the priestly order. Christ is the new high priest (Heb 4:14) who does not fall by the sword but through his own willing self-offering, and who replaces the fallen priesthood with himself as the eternal mediator (Heb 7:24 — he holds his priesthood permanently).
78.65: shadow Then the Lord awoke as from sleep, like a strong man shouting because of wine. God awakening — the resurrection as God's waking to decisive action. The sleeping Lord who suddenly rises to act is the image of God raising Christ from the dead: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death (Acts 2:24). The strong man who awakens is the risen Christ, declared to be the Son of God in power (Rom 1:4).
78.66: shadow And he put his adversaries to rout; he put them to everlasting shame. The adversaries put to everlasting shame — the principalities and powers defeated at the cross: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him (Col 2:15). The everlasting shame is the defeat of death, confirmed at the resurrection and made final at the parousia.
78.67: shadow He rejected the tent of Joseph; he did not choose the tribe of Ephraim, The rejection of Ephraim in favor of Judah — the divine election that bypasses the expected and chooses the surprising. God's election operates through unexpected channels, fulfilled ultimately in Christ who comes from Judah, the tribe of the lion: the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered (Rev 5:5).
78.68: shadow but he chose the tribe of Judah, Mount Zion, which he loves. The election of Judah and Zion — Christ comes from Judah (Matt 1:2-3; Heb 7:14 — our Lord was descended from Judah) and is the cornerstone of Zion (1 Pet 2:6, citing Isa 28:16). The love of Zion is the love that sent the Son: God so loved the world that he gave his only Son (John 3:16).
78.69: shadow He built his sanctuary like the high heavens, like the earth, which he has founded forever. The sanctuary built like the heavens — fulfilled in Christ's body as the new temple (John 2:21) and in the heavenly sanctuary he entered with his own blood (Heb 9:12). The eternal founding is the eternal nature of Christ's priestly work: he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever (Heb 7:24).
78.70: shadow He chose David his servant and took him from the sheepfolds; David chosen from the sheepfolds — the pattern Christ fulfills as the true David: Jesus Christ, the son of David (Matt 1:1). The shepherd-king who tends sheep and is chosen to rule Israel is the type of the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep and is exalted as King of kings (Rev 17:14).
78.71: shadow from following the nursing ewes he brought him to shepherd Jacob his people, Israel his inheritance. David brought from following nursing ewes to shepherd Israel — Christ's transition from Nazareth to messianic ministry: the carpenter's son (Matt 13:55) becomes the shepherd of all Israel. His ministry to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (Matt 15:24) is the literal fulfillment before the Gentile mission opens.
78.72: shadow With upright heart he shepherded them and guided them with his skillful hand. The upright shepherd with a skillful hand — the Good Shepherd who tends his flock with perfect integrity. Christ has no guile; he is the righteous shepherd (1 Pet 2:22 — no sin was found in his mouth) who guides with wisdom born of perfect communion with the Father (John 10:15 — I know my own and my own know me).

79.1: shadow O God, the nations have come into your inheritance; they have defiled your holy temple; they have laid Jerusalem in ruins. The defilement of the temple — Christ predicted precisely this: you see all these, do you not? Truly, I say to you, there will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down (Matt 24:2). The destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 is the judgment he foresaw, weeping over the city that would not receive him (Luke 19:41-44).
79.2: shadow They have given the bodies of your servants to the birds of the heavens for food, the flesh of your faithful to the beasts of the earth. The bodies given to birds — the imagery Revelation applies to the great eschatological battle: Come, gather for the great supper of God, to eat the flesh of kings... and the flesh of all men (Rev 19:17-18). The judgment fallen on Jerusalem becomes the type of final judgment on all who oppose the Lamb.
79.3: shadow They have poured out their blood like water all around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury them. Blood poured out around Jerusalem — the blood of the martyrs, and ultimately the blood of Christ shed in Jerusalem: it cannot be that a prophet should perish away from Jerusalem (Luke 13:33). His blood poured out is the blood of the new covenant (Matt 26:28) that gives meaning to all martyrs' blood.
79.4: shadow We have become a taunt to our neighbors, mocked and derided by those around us. Taunt and derision — the experience of Christ and his church. The nations mock the suffering people of God; Christ was mocked supremely (Matt 27:39-44). Peter addresses this: if you are insulted for the name of Christ, you are blessed, because the Spirit of glory and of God rests upon you (1 Pet 4:14).
79.5: shadow How long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever? Will your jealousy burn like fire? The how long of the martyrs — the cry under the altar in Revelation: how long before you will judge and avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth? (Rev 6:10). Christ establishes the answer: will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night? (Luke 18:7). The how long is answered by the parousia.
79.6: shadow Pour out your anger on the nations that do not know you, and on the kingdoms that do not call upon your name! The pouring out of wrath on those who do not know God — Paul's language in 2 Thess 1:8: inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God and on those who do not obey the gospel of our Lord Jesus. The judgment of the nations is now specifically tied to rejecting Christ and his gospel.
79.7: shadow For they have devoured Jacob and laid waste his habitation. The devouring of Jacob — Israel scattered. But Christ promises the ultimate gathering: he will send out his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other (Matt 24:31). The scattered Jacob is gathered by the one who is the true Jacob, the Israel of God (Gal 6:16).
79.8: shadow Do not remember against us our former iniquities; let your compassion come speedily to meet us, for we are brought very low. The plea for compassion to come speedily — the Incarnation itself. God's compassion coming to meet humanity brought very low is the Son of God born in a manger (Luke 2:7), taking on the lowliness of the human condition. He comes speedily to the one brought low; the parousia brings the final relief.
79.9: direct Help us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of your name; deliver us, and atone for our sins, for your name's sake! Atonement for God's name's sake — the motive Paul identifies in Rom 3:25-26: God presented Christ as a propitiation to show his righteousness and to demonstrate that he himself is just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus. The atonement vindicates God's glory and name while delivering sinners.
79.10: shadow Why should the nations say, Where is their God? Let the avenging of the outpoured blood of your servants be known among the nations before our eyes! Where is their God? — the taunt directed at the crucified Jesus: let God deliver him now, if he desires him (Matt 27:43). The resurrection is God's public vindication — the avenging of the righteous one's blood made known among the nations through the apostolic proclamation (Acts 2:32-36).
79.11: shadow Let the groaning of the prisoner come before you; according to your great power, preserve those doomed to die! The groaning prisoner preserved from death — Christ in the tomb, the one doomed to die by the sentence of Pilate. God's great power preserves him not by preventing death but by raising him through it. All who are doomed to die in Adam are preserved in Christ (1 Cor 15:22 — in Christ shall all be made alive).
79.12: shadow Return sevenfold into the lap of our neighbors the taunts with which they have taunted you, O Lord! The sevenfold return of taunts — the divine vindication. Those who taunted God's people (and Christ) face the consequences of their mockery. Rev 18:6 uses similar language of judgment on Babylon: pay her back double for her deeds. The taunts against the crucified one return on those who refuse repentance.
79.13: shadow But we your people, the sheep of your pasture, will give thanks to you forever; from generation to generation we will recount your praise. The sheep of your pasture — Christ's flock, who give thanks forever. The church's unending praise is grounded in the resurrection: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name (Heb 13:15). Generation after generation recounting God's praise is the church's mission until the parousia.

90.1: revelation of God Lord, you have been our dwelling place in all generations. God as dwelling place — fulfilled in Christ who is Immanuel, God-with-us (Matt 1:23). John's Prologue declares the ultimate dwelling: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). The dwelling place that sheltered every generation is now made flesh; in Christ, God becomes the home his people have always needed (John 14:2-3).
90.2: revelation of God Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting you are God. The eternal God — Christ's own claim: before Abraham was, I am (John 8:58). Hebrews opens with this eternity: you, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands (Heb 1:10). The everlasting God is the same Jesus Christ who is the same yesterday and today and forever (Heb 13:8).
90.3: shadow You return man to dust and say, Return, O children of man! God returning man to dust — and Christ reversing the return. To dust you shall return (Gen 3:19) is the consequence Christ absorbs: he is laid in the earth (John 19:41-42) and returns from it in resurrection. His Come forth! to Lazarus (John 11:43) is the divine call that reverses the return to dust.
90.4: shadow For a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday when it is past, or as a watch in the night. Divine timelessness applied to eschatological expectation: with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day (2 Pet 3:8). Peter uses this verse to explain why Christ's coming seems delayed — from God's perspective, no time has passed. The eternal Son inhabits this timelessness; the delay of the parousia is patience, not failure.
90.5: shadow You sweep them away as with a flood; they are like a dream, like grass that is renewed in the morning: Human transience like grass — the Incarnation enters this transience. Christ becomes the mortal, grass-like human being (Isa 40:6-8 — all flesh is grass) and transforms it from within by resurrection. The flood that sweeps away is the very flood he walks through (baptism into death) and rises from.
90.6: shadow in the morning it flourishes and is renewed; in the evening it fades and withers. The grass that flourishes and withers — the pattern of death and resurrection. Christ enters the evening of human existence (Good Friday) and becomes the one who flourishes in the morning of the resurrection (Easter Sunday — early on the first day of the week, John 20:1). His resurrection transforms the withering pattern.
90.7: shadow For we are brought to an end by your anger; by your wrath we are dismayed. God's wrath bringing to an end — the judgment Christ absorbs. The wrath that dismays humanity falls on Christ at the cross: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (Gal 3:13). He is brought to an end by the wrath that humanity deserved, so that those in him face no condemning wrath (Rom 8:1).
90.8: shadow You have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your countenance. Iniquities set before the divine face — the transparency that the cross reveals. God's omniscient knowledge of sin is the foundation of the atonement: him who knew no sin he made to be sin on our behalf (2 Cor 5:21). The secret sins set in God's light were set on Christ so they would not condemn those who believe.
90.9: shadow For all our days pass away under your wrath; we bring our years to an end like a sigh. Days passing under wrath — the universal human condition that Christ entered. He lived under the weight of human mortality and the divine wrath that attended it, made under the law to redeem those who were under the law (Gal 4:4-5). His sighing on the cross (Mark 15:37) is the last sigh of the old order ending.
90.10: shadow The years of our life are seventy, or even by reason of strength eighty; yet their span is but toil and trouble; they are soon gone, and we fly away. The brevity of life — your life is a vapor (James 4:14). Christ entered the brevity of human life (born, living approximately 33 years, dying) and transformed it. I am the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) — he who is eternal life takes on the 70-year span so that those in him receive eternal years.
90.11: shadow Who considers the power of your anger, and your wrath according to the fear of you? The power of divine anger — which only Christ fully considered and bore. He who feared God perfectly (Heb 5:7 — heard because of his reverence) also bore the full weight of divine wrath at the cross. No human fully considers this power; the Son of God alone meets it and absorbs it in his own body.
90.12: shadow So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom. A heart of wisdom — found in Christ: Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God (1 Cor 1:30). To number one's days is the posture Christ models: I must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming when no one can work (John 9:4). The urgency of numbered days becomes the church's mission-posture (Eph 5:15-16).
90.13: shadow Return, O LORD! How long? Have pity on your servants! The how long and return — the church's eschatological prayer: Come, Lord Jesus! (Rev 22:20). Christ taught this urgency: the parable of the persistent widow who cries give me justice (Luke 18:1-8) is the church's cry for the returning Lord. The return is the parousia of the Son.
90.14: revelation of God Satisfy us in the morning with your steadfast love, that we may rejoice and be glad all our days. Morning satisfaction with steadfast love — the resurrection morning. Mary Magdalene at the empty tomb (John 20:1,16 — she turned and said Rabboni!) is the morning satisfaction that launches the church's rejoicing. The steadfast love (chesed) that satisfies is made flesh in Christ (John 1:14 — full of grace and truth).
90.15: shadow Make us glad for as many days as you have afflicted us, and for as many years as we have seen evil. Gladness proportionate to affliction — the eschatological reversal. Paul: for I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us (Rom 8:18). The years of affliction give way to eternal joy; the proportion favors the believer infinitely.
90.16: shadow Let your work be shown to your servants, and your glorious power to their children. God's work shown — the Incarnation is the supreme display of God's work and glory: we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth (John 1:14). The Spirit continues showing this work to each generation's children (John 16:13-15 — he will glorify me).
90.17: shadow Let the favor of the Lord our God be upon us, and establish the work of our hands upon us; yes, establish the work of our hands! The favor of the Lord — the Aaronic blessing fulfilled in Christ (Num 6:25; 2 Cor 4:6). The work of our hands established — the church's labor in Christ is not in vain: be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain (1 Cor 15:58).

91.1: shadow He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty. The shelter of the Most High — Christ is both the one who dwells in perfect shelter (his communion with the Father is absolute) and the shelter itself: in him all the fullness of God was pleased to dwell (Col 1:19). Those who are in Christ (Paul's phrase 164 times) abide in the same shadow that protected the Son.
91.2: shadow I will say to the LORD, My refuge and my fortress, my God, in whom I trust. The confession of trust — Christ's own posture toward the Father throughout his ministry: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit (Luke 23:46). He modeled absolute refuge and trust in God at the moment of greatest vulnerability. The believer's confession of God as refuge is grounded in Christ's prior, perfect trust.
91.3: shadow For he will deliver you from the snare of the fowler and from the deadly pestilence. Deliverance from the snare — the snare of death that Christ escaped through resurrection. Those who laid snares for him (Ps 35:7; John 11:53) failed to trap him permanently. The deadly pestilence that is sin and death he overcame (1 Cor 15:55-57) so that those in him share his deliverance.
91.4: shadow He will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge; his faithfulness is a shield and buckler. The sheltering wings — Christ's own image for his longing over Jerusalem: How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing! (Matt 23:37). The longing to shelter is the Incarnation motive; the cross is where the wings are spread widest.
91.5: shadow You will not fear the terror of the night, nor the arrow that flies by day, Freedom from fear — the gift Christ gives: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your hearts be troubled, neither let them be afraid (John 14:27). The terror of the night and arrows of the day are the fears the resurrection makes manageable, since death itself — the greatest terror — is overcome.
91.6: shadow nor the pestilence that stalks in darkness, nor the destruction that wastes at noonday. The pestilence and destruction — the background radiation of the fallen world. Christ walks through the valley of the shadow of death (Ps 23:4) without being consumed, and promises those in him the same passage. I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live (John 11:25).
91.7: shadow A thousand may fall at your side, ten thousand at your right hand, but it will not come near you. The one who stands while others fall — Christ stands at the right hand of God (Acts 7:56) while all others fall under judgment. Those in him are kept: who shall separate us from the love of Christ? (Rom 8:35). Nothing that destroys others can ultimately destroy those sheltered in the risen Christ.
91.8: shadow You will only look with your eyes and see the recompense of the wicked. Witnessing the recompense — the perspective of those vindicated at the last judgment. The risen Christ sees the recompense of those who opposed him; those in him share in this vindication. The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet (Rom 16:20) — the believer sees the enemy's defeat.
91.9: shadow Because you have made the LORD your dwelling place — the Most High, who is my refuge — The Lord as dwelling place — John 15:4-5: abide in me, and I in you. Christ is the dwelling place; those who make him their refuge are sheltered. The mutual indwelling of Christ and believer (John 17:21-23) is the fulfillment of the shelter promised here — not an abstract God-refuge but union with the incarnate Son.
91.10: shadow no evil shall be allowed to befall you, no plague come near your tent. The protection of the one who dwells in God — not a promise of physical immunity but of ultimate security. Christ's promise: I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand (John 10:28). The plague that cannot touch the believer is the second death (Rev 20:6 — over such the second death has no power).
91.11: direct For he will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways. Satan quotes this verse directly to Jesus during the wilderness temptation (Matt 4:6; Luke 4:10-11), demanding that he throw himself from the temple pinnacle. Jesus refuses — not denying the promise but refusing to weaponize it against the Father's will. Christ is the one to whom this promise supremely applies; he does not presume upon it but trusts the Father's timing.
91.12: direct On their hands they will bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone. Continued from v11 — Satan's second temptation citation (Matt 4:6; Luke 4:11). Christ responds: you shall not put the Lord your God to the test (Matt 4:7, citing Deut 6:16). The promise is real — angels do minister to Christ (Matt 4:11; Luke 22:43) — but never on Satan's terms. Christ trusts the Father to deploy the promise in his own way and time.
91.13: shadow You will tread on the lion and the adder; the young lion and the serpent you will trample underfoot. Christ trampling the ancient serpent — Gen 3:15 (enmity between the woman's seed and the serpent). The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet (Rom 16:20). Christ gave the seventy-two authority: I have given you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions and over all the power of the enemy (Luke 10:19). The trampling is accomplished through the cross (Col 2:15).
91.14: shadow Because he holds fast to me in love, I will deliver him; I will protect him, because he knows my name. God speaking: the one who holds fast in love is delivered. Christ holds fast to the Father in perfect love through every trial — Gethsemane, the cross, the tomb — and is delivered through resurrection. Heb 5:7: he was heard because of his reverence. The resurrection is the divine I will deliver him.
91.15: shadow When he calls to me, I will answer him; I will be with him in trouble; I will rescue him and honor him. God promises to answer, be present, rescue, and honor. All four are fulfilled in Christ: the Father answers Gethsemane's cry (Heb 5:7), is present through the Spirit in the Passion (Luke 23:46), rescues through resurrection (Acts 2:24), and honors through exaltation: God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name above every name (Phil 2:9).
91.16: shadow With long life I will satisfy him and show him my salvation. Long life — the resurrection life that has no end: I am the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore (Rev 1:18). Christ is satisfied with the outcome of his suffering: he shall see his offspring; he shall prolong his days; the will of the LORD shall prosper in his hand (Isa 53:10). The salvation God shows him is the salvation he himself is (Luke 2:30 — my eyes have seen your salvation).

92.1: theme It is good to give thanks to the LORD, to sing praises to your name, O Most High; The Sabbath psalm's opening — Christ is Lord of the Sabbath (Matt 12:8), and the rest he provides is the true Sabbath (Heb 4:9-11). Giving thanks through Christ: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God (Heb 13:15). The name Most High is the name Jesus bears (Mark 5:7 — Son of the Most High God).
92.2: theme to declare your steadfast love in the morning, and your faithfulness by night, Morning and night praise — the ceaseless doxology. Christ models this: night prayers on the mountain (Luke 6:12), morning rising to pray (Mark 1:35). His steadfast love (hesed) declared is the gospel itself: God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Rom 5:8).
92.3: theme to the music of the lute and the harp, to the melody of the lyre. Musical praise — the worship of the new covenant community gathered around Christ. Eph 5:19: addressing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody to the Lord with your heart. The Spirit-filled church takes up the instruments of the Psalter and directs them at the risen Christ.
92.4: theme For you, O LORD, have made me glad by your work; at the works of your hands I sing for joy. Gladness in God's works — the works of Christ producing joy: these things I have spoken to you, that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full (John 15:11). The disciples' gladness when they see the risen Lord (John 20:20) is the paradigm — the works of the Lord's hands (the resurrection) are the source of inexpressible joy.
92.5: revelation of God How great are your works, O LORD! Your thoughts are very deep! The greatness of God's works and the depth of his thoughts — Paul erupts in the same doxology after explaining the mystery of Christ and the church: Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how inscrutable his ways! (Rom 11:33). The deep thoughts of God are the wisdom that is Christ himself (1 Cor 1:24).
92.6: theme The stupid man cannot know; the fool cannot understand this: The fool who cannot understand — the natural man who cannot receive the things of the Spirit of God (1 Cor 2:14). The wisdom of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing (1 Cor 1:18) — the depth of God's works in Christ is hidden from the foolish and revealed to babes (Matt 11:25).
92.7: theme that though the wicked sprout like grass and all evildoers flourish, they are doomed to destruction forever, The flourishing wicked doomed to destruction — the eschatological reversal. Christ's parable of wheat and tares (Matt 13:24-30): the tares flourish alongside the wheat until the harvest, when they are gathered for burning. The apparent prosperity of the wicked is temporary; the doom is certain and forever.
92.8: revelation of God but you, O LORD, are on high forever. The eternal height of God — fulfilled in the Ascension of Christ: he ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things (Eph 4:10). The one who is on high forever is the same Christ who descended to earth's depths and rose to fill all things. The eternal highness is the highness of the crucified and risen Lord.
92.9: shadow For behold, your enemies, O LORD, for behold, your enemies shall perish; all evildoers shall be scattered. The perishing of God's enemies — fulfilled ultimately at the parousia. He must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. The last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Cor 15:25-26). The scattering of evildoers begins at the cross (the powers disarmed, Col 2:15) and completes at the last day.
92.10: shadow But you have exalted my horn like that of the wild ox; you have poured over me fresh oil. Exalted horn and fresh oil — the anointing of the Messiah. Christ = Anointed One; the Spirit descends on him like oil at his baptism (Matt 3:16; Isa 61:1 — the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me). God exalts his horn through the resurrection and enthronement (Phil 2:9-11).
92.11: shadow My eyes have seen the downfall of my enemies; my ears have heard the doom of my evil assailants. Seeing the downfall of enemies — the vindicated righteous one's perspective. Christ sees the defeat of his enemies from his enthronement at the Father's right hand: the Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool (Ps 110:1; Matt 22:44). He waits from the position of victory already secured.
92.12: shadow The righteous flourish like the palm tree and grow like a cedar in Lebanon. The righteous flourishing — the fruitful branches abiding in Christ: I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit (John 15:5). The palm and cedar are the imagery of permanence and dignity; those in Christ grow with the stability of deep-rooted trees.
92.13: shadow They are planted in the house of the LORD; they flourish in the courts of our God. Planted in God's house — rooted and grounded in love (Eph 3:17); planted in Christ who is the true temple (John 2:21). The courts of God are now the gathering of the church around the risen Christ, in whom the fullness of the Godhead dwells bodily (Col 2:9).
92.14: shadow They still bear fruit in old age; they are ever full of sap and green, Fruitful in old age — the eternal fruitfulness of those in Christ who drink from the living water: the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). The green, sap-full tree is the believer permanently rooted in Christ, bearing fruit that remains (John 15:16).
92.15: shadow to declare that the LORD is upright; he is my rock, and there is no unrighteousness in him. The upright rock with no unrighteousness — Christ: in him there is no sin (1 John 3:5); no deceit was found in his mouth (1 Pet 2:22; Isa 53:9). The rock is Christ (1 Cor 10:4). The declaration of God's uprightness is the declaration of Christ's righteousness — the foundation on which the justified sinner stands.

93.1: direct The LORD reigns; he is robed in majesty; the LORD is robed; he has put on strength as his belt. Yes, the world is established; it shall never be moved. The LORD who reigns is Christ enthroned: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matt 28:18). Revelation applies this throne imagery to Christ throughout (Rev 4-5; 19:6 — the Lord our God the Almighty reigns). His majesty is the Ascension glory; his belt of strength is the power of the resurrection.
93.2: direct Your throne is established from of old; you are from everlasting. The eternal throne — Hebrews 1:8 applies Ps 45:6 to Christ: Your throne, O God, is forever and ever. The one from everlasting is the Christ who pre-exists all creation (John 1:1 — in the beginning was the Word; Col 1:17 — he is before all things). The ancient throne is the throne of the Son of God.
93.3: shadow The floods have lifted up, O LORD, the floods have lifted up their voice; the floods lift up their roaring. Roaring floods — the chaos waters that threaten. Christ commands the chaos: Peace! Be still! (Mark 4:39) — the disciples ask, Who then is this, that even the wind and the sea obey him? The one mightier than the roaring floods is the LORD of Ps 93, present in the boat.
93.4: shadow Mightier than the thunders of many waters, mightier than the waves of the sea, the LORD on high is mighty! Mightier than the waves — the LORD who stills the storm is Christ. His voice of authority over nature (Matt 8:26; Mark 4:39; Luke 8:24) demonstrates his identity as the creator-God who is mightier than every force of chaos. The voice like many waters in Rev 1:15 is the voice of the risen Christ.
93.5: shadow Your decrees are very trustworthy; holiness befits your house, O LORD, forevermore. Holiness befitting God's house — Christ cleanses the temple as an act of divine holiness (John 2:13-17). But he is himself the new house where holiness dwells: for in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily (Col 2:9). The trustworthy decrees are the words of Christ: heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will not pass away (Matt 24:35).

94.1: shadow O LORD, God of vengeance, O God of vengeance, shine forth! God of vengeance shining forth — the parousia of Christ: when the Lord Jesus is revealed from heaven with his mighty angels in flaming fire, inflicting vengeance on those who do not know God (2 Thess 1:7-8). The shining forth is the glory of Christ's return; the vengeance is the just judgment committed to the Son (John 5:22).
94.2: shadow Rise up, O judge of the earth; repay to the proud what they deserve! The Judge of the earth rising — Christ the appointed judge: he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead (Acts 17:31). The resurrection is the credential of the Judge; the proud are repaid at the last day.
94.3: shadow O LORD, how long shall the wicked, how long shall the wicked exult? The how long of the martyrs — how long before you will judge and avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth? (Rev 6:10). Christ's answer is the promise of vindication at the set time. The wicked exulting at the cross seemed victorious; the resurrection was the first answer to how long; the parousia is the final one.
94.4: theme They pour out their arrogant words; all the evildoers boast. Arrogant words against God — the speech of those who mocked Christ on the cross (Matt 27:39-44) and those who blaspheme throughout history. Whoever speaks against the Son of Man will be forgiven, but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven (Matt 12:32). The boasting of evildoers faces the silencing judgment of the one who speaks with divine authority.
94.5: shadow They crush your people, O LORD, and afflict your heritage. The crushing of God's people — the suffering of believers patterned on Christ's own suffering: a servant is not greater than his master. If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you (John 15:20). Christ identifies with the crushed: as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me (Matt 25:40).
94.6: shadow They kill the widow and the sojourner, and murder the fatherless; The murder of the vulnerable — the oppression Christ came to address (Luke 4:18 — good news to the poor; release to the captives). His identification with the least (Matt 25:40) means every murdered widow and orphan is an offense against Christ himself. The Judge who rises to defend them is the one who cared for them in his earthly ministry.
94.7: shadow and they say, The LORD does not see; the God of Jacob does not perceive. God does not see — the arrogance of those who sinned in the dark, thinking God absent. Christ exposes this: nothing is hidden that will not be revealed, or secret that will not be known (Matt 10:26). Before him all things are naked and exposed (Heb 4:13); his eyes like flames of fire see everything (Rev 1:14).
94.8: theme Understand, O dullest of the people! Fools, when will you be wise? The call to wisdom — Christ calls the foolish to wisdom: come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... learn from me (Matt 11:28-29). The wisdom that the fool lacks is the wisdom from above (James 3:17); it is found in Christ who became to us wisdom from God (1 Cor 1:30).
94.9: shadow He who planted the ear, does he not hear? He who formed the eye, does he not see? The Creator who hears and sees — Christ demonstrates this in John 9: he sees the blind man (John 9:1), heals his eyes (John 9:6-7), and then announces: For judgment I came into this world, that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind (John 9:39). The one who formed the eye comes to restore sight.
94.10: shadow He who disciplines nations, does he not rebuke? He who teaches man knowledge — God who disciplines nations and teaches knowledge — Christ rebukes and teaches with divine authority (Matt 7:29 — he taught them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes). His rebukes to the Pharisees (Matt 23) and his teaching of the nations (Matt 28:20 — teaching them to observe all that I have commanded) fulfill this verse.
94.11: direct the LORD — knows the thoughts of man, that they are but a breath. 1 Corinthians 3:20 quotes this verse: The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise, that they are futile. Paul uses it to demolish human wisdom-boasting and ground all confidence in Christ crucified. The God who knows human thoughts as futile — including the thoughts of those who crucified his Son — is the one who raises the foolish things of the world to shame the wise.
94.12: shadow Blessed is the man whom you discipline, O LORD, and whom you teach out of your law, The discipline of the LORD — applied to believers in Hebrews 12:5-11 (citing Prov 3:11-12): the Lord disciplines the one he loves. Christ himself learned obedience through what he suffered (Heb 5:8) — the ultimate example of divine discipline producing the fruit of righteousness. His disciples are discipled (the same root) by following him through suffering.
94.13: shadow to give him rest from days of trouble, until a pit is dug for the wicked. Rest from trouble — the rest Christ provides: Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest (Matt 11:28). The ultimate rest is the sabbath rest that remains for the people of God (Heb 4:9-11), entered through faith in Christ. The pit dug for the wicked is the eschatological judgment of those who rejected him.
94.14: shadow For the LORD will not forsake his people; he will not abandon his heritage. The LORD will not forsake — Christ's great promise: I am with you always, to the end of the age (Matt 28:20). Paul's certainty: I am sure that neither death nor life... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord (Rom 8:38-39). The never-forsaking God is the Christ who went through abandonment on the cross so he might be the unforsaking Shepherd forever.
94.15: shadow for justice will return to the righteous, and all the upright in heart will follow it. Justice returning to the righteous — the eschatological reversal. Christ is the righteous one (Acts 3:14 — you denied the Holy and Righteous One) to whom justice returns at the resurrection. All who are upright in heart — those clothed in his righteousness (Phil 3:9) — follow in his vindication.
94.16: shadow Who rises up for me against the wicked? Who stands up for me against evildoers? Who rises up? — Christ rises up for the oppressed. Acts 7:55-56: Stephen sees Jesus standing at the right hand of God — he rises to welcome his martyr. The one who rose from the dead rises for those who are persecuted. He stands as advocate (1 John 2:1 — we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous).
94.17: shadow If the LORD had not been my help, my soul would soon have lived in the land of silence. The LORD as help — Heb 13:6 applies a similar promise directly: I will never leave you nor forsake you, leading to the declaration The Lord is my helper; I will not fear. The land of silence is death — from which Christ rescues those who trust him. He himself passed through the land of silence (the tomb) and came out, ensuring none of his own stay there.
94.18: shadow When I thought, My foot slips, your steadfast love, O LORD, held me up. Steadfast love holding up the slipping foot — the preserving grace of Christ. Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling and to present you blameless before the presence of his glory with great joy (Jude 24). The Shepherd who prays for Peter (Luke 22:32 — I have prayed for you that your faith may not fail) holds up the slipping foot.
94.19: shadow When the cares of my heart are many, your consolations cheer my soul. Divine consolation — the Spirit as Comforter (John 14:16-17 — I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper). The consolations that cheer the soul are the consolations of Christ given through the Spirit: if there is any comfort in Christ... any participation in the Spirit... complete my joy (Phil 2:1-2).
94.20: shadow Can wicked rulers be allied with you, who frame injustice by statute? Unjust rulers framing injustice by statute — the Roman and Jewish authorities who condemned Christ through legal process while he was innocent. Pilate found no fault (Luke 23:4), yet sentenced him; the Sanhedrin condemned him on charges of blasphemy against their own manipulated law. Injustice by statute is the mechanism of the Passion.
94.21: shadow They band together against the life of the righteous and condemn the innocent to death. Banding against the righteous to condemn the innocent — Acts 4:27: for truly in this city there were gathered together against your holy servant Jesus, whom you anointed, both Herod and Pontius Pilate, along with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel. The banding together against Christ is the supreme fulfillment of this verse.
94.22: shadow But the LORD has become my stronghold, and my God the rock of my refuge. The LORD as stronghold and rock — Christ the rock of refuge (1 Cor 10:4). The vindicated righteous one finds refuge in the God who raised him; those condemned by the same injustice find in Christ their stronghold. The rock that was struck to bring forth water (Exod 17) is the same rock that is the refuge from every storm.
94.23: shadow He will bring back on them their iniquity and wipe them out for their evil; the LORD our God will wipe them out. Iniquity brought back — the principle of divine justice at the last judgment. Christ will judge: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done (Rev 22:12). The wiping out of evildoers is the work of the one who first wiped out the sin of those who trust him (Col 2:14 — canceling the record of debt).

95.1: shadow Oh come, let us sing to the LORD; let us make a joyful noise to the rock of our salvation! The rock of our salvation — Christ: the Rock was Christ (1 Cor 10:4); he is my rock and my salvation (Ps 62:2). The joyful noise is the church's praise directed at the risen Lord (Heb 13:15 — through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise). The singing to the LORD is the singing of the church to Christ.
95.2: theme Let us come into his presence with thanksgiving; let us make a joyful noise to him with songs of praise! Coming into his presence with thanksgiving — the church's gathered worship. Eph 5:19-20: singing and making melody to the Lord with your heart, giving thanks always and for everything to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. Presence, thanksgiving, and song are constitutive elements of Christ-centered worship.
95.3: direct For the LORD is a great God, and a great King above all gods. The great King above all gods — Christ: King of kings and Lord of lords (Rev 17:14; 19:16). Every earthly power, every spiritual principality is beneath the authority of the risen Christ who is given all authority in heaven and on earth (Matt 28:18). The great God is the one who became flesh and dwelt among us.
95.4: revelation of God In his hand are the depths of the earth; the heights of the mountains are his also. Christ holds the depths and heights — he ascended far above all the heavens, that he might fill all things (Eph 4:10). The one who descended to the depths of the earth (death) and ascended to the heights holds all creation in his hand. In him all things hold together (Col 1:17).
95.5: revelation of God The sea is his, for he made it, and his hands formed the dry land. The Creator of sea and land — the same hands that formed dry land stilled the storm on Galilee (Mark 4:39). Christ as creator (John 1:3; Col 1:16 — all things were created through him and for him) is the LORD of Ps 95 who owns the sea and formed the land. His command over creation reveals his identity.
95.6: shadow Oh come, let us worship and bow down; let us kneel before the LORD, our Maker! Bowing and kneeling before the LORD — the posture of Phil 2:10: at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth. The worship called for in Ps 95:6 is the worship every creature ultimately gives to Christ — voluntarily now in the church, finally and universally at the parousia.
95.7: direct For he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture, and the sheep of his hand. Today, if you hear his voice, Today, if you hear his voice — Heb 3:7-8 quotes this verse as a direct word of the Holy Spirit: Therefore, as the Holy Spirit says, Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. Hebrews applies the warning to the church hearing Christ's voice in the present moment — the today of Christ's call is the present moment of every hearer.
95.8: direct do not harden your hearts, as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness, Hebrews 3:8 continues the citation: do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion, on the day of testing in the wilderness. The wilderness hardening becomes the anti-pattern for those who have heard the gospel of Christ. The testing at Meribah (water from the rock = Christ, 1 Cor 10:4) is now the testing of whether one trusts Christ in the present.
95.9: direct when your fathers put me to the test and put me to the proof, though they had seen my work. Hebrews 3:9 continues: the Israelites who saw God's work and still tested him are the warning to those who have seen Christ's works (John 12:37) and still refuse to believe. The testing of God in the wilderness is the same as testing Christ (1 Cor 10:9 — we must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did).
95.10: direct For forty years I loathed that generation and said, They are a people who go astray in their heart, and they have not known my ways. Hebrews 3:10 quotes this exactly: I was provoked with that generation, and said, They always go astray in their heart; they have not known my ways. The forty years of wandering becomes the image for a generation that refuses Christ — going astray in the heart is the refusal to enter the rest that Christ provides.
95.11: direct Therefore I swore in my wrath, They shall not enter my rest. Hebrews 3:11 cites this directly, then expands it throughout chapters 3-4: the rest the wilderness generation failed to enter is the Sabbath rest that remains for the people of God (Heb 4:9). Let us therefore strive to enter that rest (Heb 4:11) — the rest entered through faith in Christ, who is himself the fulfillment of the Sabbath (Matt 11:28-29 — I will give you rest).

80.1: The cry to the Shepherd of Israel enthroned above the cherubim reaches its fulfillment in the one who came as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) — the very YHWH who led Joseph like a flock now takes on flesh to seek the lost sheep of Israel. The throne above the cherubim that the psalmist addresses is the one from which the Son descends (John 3:13; Heb 1:3).
80.2: The summons to stir up your might before Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh — tribes of the Rachel-Joseph line — is a plea for the powerful showing-up of God. The disciples who witnessed Christ transfigured and resurrected saw precisely this: the divine might displayed not in military power but in the power of an indestructible life (Heb 7:16).
80.3: The refrain — shuvennu Elohim haer panekha venivvashea — "Restore us, O God; make your face shine, so that we may be saved" — is the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25-26) transformed into desperate petition. In Christ, the shining face of God is given permanently: "God... has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of God's glory in the face of Jesus Christ" (2 Cor 4:6). The shining that the psalmist begs for is the very face the disciples beheld (John 1:14).
80.4: "How long, O LORD God of hosts, will you be angry with your people's prayers?" — the smoke of divine anger that seems to consume prayer itself. Christ on the cross cries "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matt 27:46), identifying fully with the community whose prayers seem to go nowhere; and then, risen, he opens the way to the Father so that prayers are no longer met with smoke but with the Spirit's intercession (Rom 8:26-27).
80.5: "You have fed them with the bread of tears and given them tears to drink in full measure" — the divine feeding with sorrow. Christ, the Bread of Life (John 6:35), entered into this very experience: "Man of sorrows and acquainted with grief" (Isa 53:3). The one who feeds with bread of tears is the same one who becomes the bread that ends the tears (Rev 21:4: "He will wipe away every tear").
80.6: "You make us an object of contention for our neighbors, and our enemies laugh among themselves" — the scorn of surrounding nations. Jesus bore this scorn explicitly: he was mocked by Herod's soldiers (Luke 23:11), the crowd wagged their heads (Matt 27:39), and the rulers "scoffed" (Luke 23:35). The shame borne by Israel that the psalmist laments is the shame Christ absorbed as Israel's representative head.
80.7: The refrain recurs: "Restore us, O God of hosts; make your face shine, so that we may be saved." The three-fold repetition of this refrain across the psalm (vv3, 7, 19) mirrors the threefold divine name escalation: first "God" (Elohim), then "God of hosts" (Elohei Tsevat), finally "LORD God of hosts" (YHWH Elohei Tsevat) — an intensification that finds its fullest expression in Christ who is "Emmanuel, God with us" (Matt 1:23), the complete shining of the divine face.
80.8: "You brought a vine out of Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it" — the great vine-transplant of the Exodus. Jesus said "I am the true vine" (John 15:1), explicitly claiming to be what Israel the historic vine failed to be. The vine-from-Egypt that should have filled the land is now the true vine from which the branches draw life — the Exodus-vine history is not abandoned but fulfilled and surpassed.
80.9: "You cleared the ground for it; it took deep root and filled the land" — the flourishing of the planted vine. The true vine planted in the land, Jesus of Nazareth, took root in the soil of Jewish history (Matt 1:1-17), bore fruit through death and resurrection, and now fills the earth as his people spread to every nation (Acts 1:8; Rev 7:9). The geographical filling of the land points toward the eschatological filling of the earth.
80.10: "The mountains were covered with its shade, the mighty cedars with its branches" — the vine's cosmic reach. The mustard seed parable (Matt 13:31-32) uses the same image: a tiny plant that grows until "the birds of the air come and perch in its branches." Christ's kingdom begins as the most modest of plantings in Galilee and expands to fill the earth; the vine of Psalm 80:10 is the mustard-tree of the Gospels.
80.11: "It sent out its branches to the sea, and its shoots to the River" — the vine spanning from Mediterranean to Euphrates, the full extent of the Davidic promise (Gen 15:18; 1 Kgs 4:21). Ephesians 1:10 speaks of God's plan to bring all things together under Christ — the vine whose branches reach from sea to river becomes the cosmic Christ who fills all in all (Eph 1:23).
80.12: "Why then have you broken down its walls, so that all who pass along the way pluck its fruit?" — the ruined vineyard, open to plunder. Isaiah 5:1-7 uses the same image of the unprotected vineyard to speak of divine judgment. Christ quotes the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:33-44) over this same vineyard — the story of Israel's failure is one Jesus knows from the inside, as both the rejected cornerstone and the true Son sent last.
80.13: "A boar from the forest ravages it, and all that move in the field feed on it" — the wild beast tearing apart the vine. Christ came to destroy the works of the devil (1 John 3:8), to bind the strong man and plunder his house (Matt 12:29). The vine that was ravaged by the enemy is defended by the Lion of Judah (Rev 5:5).
80.14: "Turn again, O God of hosts! Look down from heaven, and see; have regard for this vine" — the prayer that God would look at the vine. The Incarnation is the divine response to this prayer: God does not merely look down from heaven but descends to the vine in the person of his Son. "The LORD looked down from his holy height" (Ps 102:19) finds its ultimate expression in the One who stooped from highest heaven to the manger (Phil 2:6-7).
80.15: "The stock that your right hand planted, and the son that you made strong for yourself" — the vine personalized as a son. Here is the Christological hinge: the vine is simultaneously a son. Jesus is the True Vine (John 15:1) and the Son of God — the personification of Israel-vine in a single person. "This is my beloved Son" (Matt 3:17) is the divine recognition of the stock planted by the right hand.
80.16: "They have burned it with fire, they have cut it down; may they perish at the rebuke of your face!" — the vine cut down and burned. This images Christ's death: the true vine is cut down at Calvary, burned in the fire of divine wrath (Gal 3:13: "cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree"). But the cut vine rises — the burned branch is not consumed but resurrected; the rebuke of God's face falls on Christ so that the divine face may shine on his people (refrain, v19).
80.17: "Let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man whom you have made strong for yourself" — one of the most direct Messianic moments in the Psalter. "Ben-adam" (son of man) seated at the right hand of power, strengthened by God himself. Daniel 7:13 sees "one like a son of man" coming with the clouds to receive all authority. Jesus explicitly takes this title as his own self-designation (Matt 8:20; 26:64: "you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power"). The man at YHWH's right hand of Psalm 80:17 is the ascended Christ (Acts 2:33; Heb 1:13).
80.18: "Then we shall not turn back from you; give us life, and we will call upon your name!" — the vow of faithfulness and the cry for life (hayyenu). Christ's resurrection answers this cry perfectly: it is the giving of life that makes calling on the name possible. "Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved" (Rom 10:13, quoting Joel 2:32) — the life-cry of Psalm 80:18 is answered in the resurrection and the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost.
80.19: The final refrain in its fullest form: "Restore us, O YHWH God of hosts; make your face shine, so that we may be saved!" — the most complete divine name combined with the prayer for the shining face. Second Corinthians 4:6: the face of Jesus Christ is the shining face the psalm seeks. The escalating divine name through the psalm's three refrains traces the journey from lament to the full light of God's glory in Christ.

81.1: "Sing aloud to God our strength; shout for joy to the God of Jacob!" — the festal call to worship. In Christ, the God of Jacob is recognized as the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ (Eph 1:3) — the tribal patriarch-God who comes to be the God of all nations through the seed of Abraham/Jacob (Gal 3:16). The shouting of the assembly gathered at the feast finds its fullest echo in the church's gathered worship (Heb 12:22-24).
81.2: "Raise a song; sound the tambourine, the sweet lyre with the harp!" — the liturgical instruments of praise. In the new creation, the song of the redeemed fills heaven: "They sang a new song" (Rev 5:9) before the Lamb. The festal musicality of Psalm 81 points toward the eternal worship of the Lamb, the one toward whom all celebration ultimately moves.
81.3: "Blow the trumpet at the new moon, at the full moon, on our feast day" — the lunar calendar marking the sacred assembly. Paul writes that the Sabbaths, new moons, and festivals "are a shadow of the things to come, but the substance belongs to Christ" (Col 2:16-17). The trumpet-call to the feast is the type; Christ is the substance whom the feast days commemorate and anticipate.
81.4: "For it is a statute for Israel, a decree of the God of Jacob" — the divine ordinance ordering the festal assembly. Christ "came not to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them" (Matt 5:17). The statutes and decrees of the covenant are fulfilled in him — the festal calendar finds its fulfillment in the One toward whom every feast pointed: the Passover Lamb, the Firstfruits from the dead (1 Cor 15:20), the Bread of heaven.
81.5: "He made it a decree in Joseph, when he went out over the land of Egypt. I hear a language I had not known" — the Exodus origin of the ordinance, and the divine oracle in a form not previously known. Hebrews 1:1-2: "In the past God spoke... at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son." The unknown language becomes the known Word made flesh — the radically new mode of divine speech that the Incarnation represents.
81.6: "I relieved your shoulder of the burden; your hands were freed from the basket" — the liberation from Egyptian slave-labor. Jesus explicitly takes this image: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... my yoke is easy, and my burden is light" (Matt 11:28-30). The liberation from the brick-basket of Egypt is the type; the freedom Christ gives from sin's burden and the law's condemnation is the antitype. The shoulder freed in Exodus points to the yoke removed by Christ.
81.7: "In distress you called, and I delivered you; I answered you in the secret place of thunder; I tested you at the waters of Meribah" — divine response from hidden thunder. John 12:28-29: when a voice came from heaven after Jesus' prayer, "the crowd that stood there and heard it said that it had thundered." The divine voice from the thunder-cloud over Israel's wilderness is the same voice that confirms the Son at his hour of glory. The waters of Meribah-testing prepare for the one who is himself the living water (John 4:14; 7:37-38).
81.8: "Hear, O my people, while I admonish you! O Israel, if you would but listen to me!" — the divine call to hear. Jesus stands in this tradition and intensifies it: "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" (Matt 13:9). Revelation 2-3 repeats "He who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the churches" — the listening that Israel failed to do is now called for by the risen Christ through the Spirit. The divine appeal of Psalm 81:8 echoes through every page of the Gospels.
81.9: "There shall be no strange god among you; you shall not bow down to a foreign god" — the first commandment's heart. Christ himself is the fulfillment of this exclusivity: "I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The demand for exclusive devotion to YHWH alone is concentrated in the person of the Son, who is YHWH's definitive self-revelation (John 10:30; Col 2:9).
81.10: "I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it" — the Exodus formula of divine self-identification combined with a promise of provision. Jesus takes both elements: he is the one who brings the new Exodus (Luke 9:31), and he is the Bread that fills the open mouth — "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst" (John 6:35). The Sinai self-identification of YHWH is fulfilled in the "I am" declarations of Jesus.
81.11: "But my people did not listen to my voice; Israel would not submit to me" — the terrible refusal. This is the pattern that runs through the OT prophets and culminates in Stephen's speech (Acts 7:51-53: "you always resist the Holy Spirit; as your fathers did, so do you"). Christ came to the very ones who would not listen and was rejected: "He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him" (John 1:11). The non-listening of Psalm 81:11 reaches its climax at the cross.
81.12: "So I gave them over to their stubborn hearts, to follow their own counsels" — the divine judicial abandonment. Paul uses this exact pattern in Romans 1:24-28: "God gave them over (paredoken) in the sinful desires of their hearts... God gave them over to shameful lusts... God gave them over to a depraved mind." The threefold giving-over of Romans 1 echoes the giving-over of Psalm 81:12. Christ is the answer: in him the Father does not give over but gives up the Son for us all (Rom 8:32), so that the pattern of judicial abandonment is reversed through substitutionary love.
81.13: "Oh, that my people would listen to me, that Israel would walk in my ways!" — the divine lament-wish. This holy longing is taken up by Jesus over Jerusalem: "O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... how often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!" (Matt 23:37). The divine wish-oracle of Psalm 81:13 is Jesus weeping over the city. God's desire for his people's flourishing is not cold decree but aching love.
