The celebration of those who hate him is the Friday celebration that Sunday overturns.
89.43: "You have blunted his sword in battle and failed to hold him up when he fought." The failure of the anointed king's weapons and the divine non-upholding in battle. At Gethsemane, Christ says "put your sword away" (John 18:11) — the literal blunting of the weapon. In the trial, the theological defense is met with silence: Matthew 27:14 ("he gave no answer, not even to a single charge — to the great amazement of the governor"). The non-upholding is the withdrawal of the divine intervention that could have prevented the cross — the Father "failed to hold him up" not through abandonment but through the purposive allowing of the death (Acts 2:23).
89.44: "You have stripped away his glory and hurled his throne down to the ground." The glory stripped away — the kenosis of Philippians 2:7 ("he emptied himself, taking the form of a servant") — and the throne hurled down are the incarnation and the cross together. The Transfiguration briefly showed the glory beneath the humiliation (Matt 17:2); the cross is the moment when the glory appears entirely gone, the throne apparently destroyed. Hebrews 12:2 ("for the joy set before him he endured the cross, scorning its shame") identifies the cross as the place of the scorned throne — the kingship exercised in the most shameful possible form, only to be revealed as the path to the eternal throne (Heb 1:8).
89.45: "You have shortened the days of his prime and wrapped him in shame." The cut-short days and shame-wrapping are the passion-language of humiliation and premature death. Hebrews 12:2 ("scorning its shame") and Philippians 2:8 ("death on a cross" — the shameful execution reserved for criminals and enemies of Rome) give the NT form. Jesus dies at approximately thirty-three years old, at the height of a human life. Yet Revelation 1:17-18 ("I am the Living One; I was dead, and now look, I am alive forever and ever") reverses the shortening: the risen Christ has not shortened days but endless days — the apparent cutting-short is the beginning of the endless.
89.46: "How long, O LORD? Will you hide yourself away forever? How long will your anger blaze like fire?" The how-long cry — the lament question about divine hiddenness — is the cry of the interval between the cross and the second coming. The disciples lived this question in the three days between crucifixion and resurrection. The church lives it in the age between Pentecost and parousia. Revelation 6:10 ("how long, Sovereign Lord, holy and true, until you judge the inhabitants of the earth and avenge our blood?") is the eschatological form. The fire-like anger that blazes is the wrath absorbed by Christ at the cross, whose "how long" is answered by the resurrection's declaration that it is finished.
89.47: "Remember how short my span of life is! For what emptiness you have made all the children of man!" The brevity of human life and the vanity that death exposes — the existential crisis that underlies the covenant lament. Hebrews 2:14-15 ("since the children have flesh and blood, he too shared in their humanity so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil — and free those who all their lives were held in slavery by their fear of death") gives the specific reason Christ takes on the short span: to break the death-power from within human mortality. The emptiness made of all the children of man is precisely what the resurrection overcomes.
89.48: "What man can go on living and never see death? Who can rescue his life from the grip of the grave?" The rhetorical questions that expect "no one" — the universal mortality of every human. But Christ is the exception who makes the questions answerable. John 11:26 ("whoever lives by believing in me will never die — do you believe this?") and 1 Corinthians 15:54-55 ("death has been swallowed up in victory. 'Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?'") are the NT answers. Christ rescues his life from the grip of the grave (Acts 2:24: "it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him") and thereby makes possible the rescue of all who are in him.
89.49: "Lord, where is your steadfast love of old, which in your faithfulness you swore to David?" The covenant question — the lament that the sworn promise appears unfulfilled. This is the cry that resurrection answers definitively. Acts 13:32-34 (Paul in Antioch: "we tell you the good news: what God promised our ancestors he has fulfilled for us, their children, by raising up Jesus... 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings promised to David'") is the explicit citation of the Davidic promise as fulfilled in the resurrection. The "where is your hesed" of v49 receives the answer: it is in the resurrection of Jesus, the fulfillment of what was sworn to David.
89.50: "Remember, Lord, the mocking your servants endure — how I bear in my heart the taunts of all those nations." The internalized mockery — the taunts of the nations carried in the heart of the suffering servant — is the burden of shame borne by Christ and shared with his disciples. Hebrews 11:26 (Moses "regarded disgrace for the sake of Christ as of greater value than the treasures of Egypt") and Hebrews 13:13 ("let us go to him outside the camp, bearing the disgrace he bore") make the bearing of mockery a participation in Christ's own experience. The taunts of the nations that the psalmist carries in his heart are the taunts that Christ carried through the passion and that his witnesses bear after him.
89.51: "with which your enemies, LORD, have taunted, mocking every step of your anointed king." The mocking of every step of the anointed's journey — the sustained ridicule of the Messianic claim. Luke 23:35-39 describes successive layers of mocking at the cross: the rulers sneered, the soldiers mocked, even one of the crucified criminals hurled insults. The mocking of "every step" — from entry into Jerusalem through the trials and crucifixion — is the sustained taunt that the anointed king endures. John 19:14-15 (Pilate: "here is your king!"; crowd: "take him away! Crucify him!") is the specific mocking of the kingship claim at its climax.
89.52: "Blessed be the LORD forever! Amen and Amen." The doxology that closes Book III of the Psalter — not a conclusion to the lament of Psalm 89 but an editorial seam marking the end of the third book. Yet placed here, after the profound lament of the Davidic covenant's apparent collapse, this doxology has the quality of faith that persists beyond what can be seen. Hebrews 11:1 ("faith is confidence in what we hope for and assurance about what we do not see") describes exactly this: blessing YHWH in the face of the covenant's apparent failure. The resurrection is the answer that vindicates the doxology: Revelation 5:13 ("to him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be praise and honor and glory and power, for ever and ever") is the eschatological Amen and Amen.

103.1: theme Bless the LORD, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless his holy name! The whole-person blessing of God — realized in Christ who loves the Lord with all his heart, soul, mind, and strength (Mark 12:30), perfectly fulfilling what Israel could only approximate. The holy name blessed here is the name Jesus declares to his Father: I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me (John 17:6).
103.2: theme Bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits, Forgetting none of God's benefits — the Eucharist as the church's act of remembrance: do this in remembrance of me (1 Cor 11:24-25). Christ institutes the meal precisely so his benefits — forgiveness, body broken, blood poured — are not forgotten. Every celebration of the Lord's Supper is the soul blessing God and forgetting none of his benefits.
103.3: direct who forgives all your iniquity, who heals all your diseases, Forgiveness and healing united — the signature of Christ's ministry. Matthew 9:2-7: Jesus forgives the paralytic's sins, and when challenged asks, which is easier, to say your sins are forgiven, or to say rise and walk? — then heals him to prove the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins. The conjunction of forgiveness and healing in Ps 103:3 is precisely enacted in Christ.
103.4: shadow who redeems your life from the pit, who crowns you with steadfast love and mercy, Redemption from the pit — the resurrection as the ultimate pit-redemption. Christ descended into death (Eph 4:9) and was raised out of the pit: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it (Acts 2:24). Those in him are crowned with the steadfast love and mercy Christ absorbed — through him we have obtained access by faith into this grace (Rom 5:2).
103.5: shadow who satisfies you with good so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's. Youth renewed like the eagle — the resurrection transformation. If the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, he who raised Christ Jesus from the dead will also give life to your mortal bodies through his Spirit who dwells in you (Rom 8:11). The renewal of youth is not cosmetic but ontological — the new creation body inaugurated in Christ's resurrection.
103.6: shadow The LORD works righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed. Righteousness and justice for the oppressed — the Nazareth manifesto: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to set at liberty those who are oppressed (Luke 4:18, citing Isa 61:1). Christ is the LORD working righteousness for the oppressed, fulfilling the prophetic vision of Ps 103:6 in his own person and ministry.
103.7: type He made known his ways to Moses, his acts to the people of Israel. God's ways known to Moses — fulfilled and surpassed in Christ: the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ (John 1:17). The mediator of the law who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11) is superseded by the one who is the face of God (2 Cor 4:6) and the mediator of the new covenant (Heb 9:15).
103.8: revelation of God The LORD is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love. The covenant name of God (Exod 34:6-7) — which Christ makes visible. God, being rich in mercy, because of the great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in our trespasses, made us alive together with Christ (Eph 2:4-5). The divine character defined in Ps 103:8 is the character displayed at the cross and in the resurrection.
103.9: shadow He will not always chide, nor will he keep his anger forever. The anger that does not last forever — the atonement makes this possible without compromising justice. God's wrath is satisfied in Christ (Rom 3:25 — propitiation) so that he does not chide forever. In overflowing anger for a moment I hid my face from you, but with everlasting love I will have compassion on you (Isa 54:8) — the moment of the cross gives way to everlasting compassion.
103.10: shadow He does not deal with us according to our sins, nor repay us according to our iniquities. Grace exceeding deserved punishment — the doctrine of justification. For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God (2 Cor 5:21). God does not deal with believers according to their sins because Christ was dealt with according to those sins on their behalf. The not-dealing is grounded in the substitution.
103.11: revelation of God For as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his steadfast love toward those who fear him; The immeasurable love — Paul reaches for the same cosmic scale: I pray that you, being rooted and grounded in love, may have strength to comprehend with all the saints what is the breadth and length and height and depth, and to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge (Eph 3:17-19). The heavens-above-earth image is the inadequate metaphor Paul tries to exceed.
103.12: shadow as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us. Total removal of transgressions — the irreversible forgiveness grounded in Christ's atonement. Paul's declaration: there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Rom 8:1). The infinite east-west distance is the distance the cross places between the believer and their sin. He has delivered us from the domain of darkness (Col 1:13) — a complete removal.
103.13: direct As a father shows compassion to his children, so the LORD shows compassion to those who fear him; The fatherly compassion enacted in Christ's parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:20): But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him and felt compassion, and ran and embraced him and kissed him. Jesus gives the parable to reveal the Father's heart — the running father of Luke 15 is the LORD of Ps 103:13 made visible in a story.
103.14: shadow For he knows our frame; he remembers that we are dust. God knowing human frailty — the Incarnation is God not merely remembering but entering: since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things (Heb 2:14). Christ knows our frame from the inside — he grew tired (John 4:6), wept (John 11:35), hungered (Matt 4:2), and died. His knowing is not theoretical but experiential.
103.15: shadow As for man, his days are like grass; he flourishes like a flower of the field; Human transience like grass — 1 Pet 1:24 applies Isa 40:6-8 (same image) to the contrast between the fading flesh and the enduring word of God. Christ enters the transience of grass-like humanity and transforms it: his resurrection body is the firstfruits of the imperishable (1 Cor 15:42-44), the flowering of humanity that does not fade.
103.16: shadow for the wind passes over it, and it is gone, and its place knows it no more. The passing wind and vanishing life — the transience that Christ redeems. Your life is a vapor that appears for a little time and then vanishes (James 4:14). But those in Christ do not merely vanish: For this corruptible must put on the incorruptible, and this mortal must put on immortality (1 Cor 15:53). The wind that blew Jesus into the tomb also came as the breath of the resurrection morning.
103.17: revelation of God But the steadfast love of the LORD is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear him, and his righteousness to children's children, Everlasting steadfast love — the love of God from which nothing can separate: I am sure that neither death nor life... nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord (Rom 8:38-39). The from-everlasting-to-everlasting love is the love that sent the Son (John 3:16) and keeps all who are in him.
103.18: shadow to those who keep his covenant and remember to do his commandments. Covenant-keeping as the condition of receiving steadfast love — fulfilled in Christ the perfect covenant-keeper. He kept every commandment (John 8:29 — I always do the things that are pleasing to him) and his obedience is imputed to those who are in him (Rom 5:19 — by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous).
103.19: direct The LORD has established his throne in the heavens, and his kingdom rules over all. The heavenly throne and universal kingdom — Christ's enthronement: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matt 28:18). Revelation places Christ on the heavenly throne (Rev 3:21; 5:6-14) alongside the Father. The kingdom that rules over all is now the kingdom of the Son (Col 1:13 — the kingdom of his beloved Son).
103.20: shadow Bless the LORD, O you his angels, you mighty ones who do his word, obeying the voice of his word! Angels obeying the voice of God's word — Christ is the Word (John 1:1). At his birth the angels proclaimed him (Luke 2:13-14); at the temptation they ministered to him (Matt 4:11); at the resurrection they announced him (Matt 28:2-7). The mighty angels who obey the voice of his word now obey the voice of the incarnate Word.
103.21: shadow Bless the LORD, all his hosts, his ministers who do his will! All the heavenly hosts blessing and serving — the throne-room of Revelation: ten thousand times ten thousand and thousands of thousands, saying with a loud voice, Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing! (Rev 5:11-12). The hosts who bless God bless him through the Lamb.
103.22: theme Bless the LORD, all his works, in all places of his dominion. Bless the LORD, O my soul! All creation blessing the LORD — the eschatological worship of Rev 5:13: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! The Psalm's universal praise reaches its target: Christ and the Father, receiving the blessing of all creation.

104.1: revelation of God Bless the LORD, O my soul! O LORD my God, you are very great! You are clothed with splendor and majesty, God clothed in splendor and majesty — the Transfiguration discloses this glory: his face shone like the sun, and his clothes became white as light (Matt 17:2). The resurrection appearance radiates the same divine splendor: his appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow (Matt 28:3). The great God clothed in majesty is the Christ who was clothed in human flesh and then revealed his hidden glory.
104.2: revelation of God covering yourself with light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent. God clothed in light — God is light, and in him is no darkness at all (1 John 1:5). Christ: I am the light of the world (John 8:12). The stretching out of the heavens is the creative act of the Word: in the beginning was the Word... all things were made through him (John 1:1,3). The tent stretched out is the tabernacle of the Incarnation — he pitched his tent among us (John 1:14).
104.3: shadow He lays the beams of his chambers on the waters; he makes the clouds his chariot; he rides on the wings of the wind; God riding clouds and wind — Christ coming on the clouds: they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory (Matt 24:30; Rev 1:7 — behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him). The cloud-rider of Ps 104 is identified in the NT as the returning Christ.
104.4: direct he makes his messengers winds, his ministers a flame of fire. Hebrews 1:7 quotes this verse directly to contrast angels with the Son: Of the angels he says, He makes his angels winds, and his ministers a flame of fire. But of the Son he says, Your throne, O God, is forever and ever. The angels who are like wind and fire are servants; the Son is the eternal God enthroned. Ps 104:4 establishes the angel-servant status that proves the Son's superiority.
104.5: shadow He set the earth on its foundations, so that it should never be moved. The earth's foundations set by Christ the creator: by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible (Col 1:16). Heb 1:10-12 quotes Ps 102:25-27 similarly — you, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning — applying it to the Son. The one who set the earth's foundation now holds all things together (Col 1:17).
104.6: shadow You covered it with the deep as with a garment; the waters stood above the mountains. The primordial waters — Genesis creation imagery that John echoes in the new creation. The Spirit hovering over the waters (Gen 1:2) is the Spirit Christ sends (John 20:22). The deep covered by waters becomes the deep that Christ commands (Mark 4:39) and the deep of death he descends into and rises from (Rom 10:7).
104.7: shadow At your rebuke they fled; at the sound of your thunder they took to flight. Waters fleeing at God's rebuke — Christ rebuking the storm: he rebuked the wind and the raging waves, and they ceased, and there was a great calm (Luke 8:24). The waters of chaos that fled at creation's command flee again at the voice of the incarnate Creator. The disciples' question — who then is this? — is answered by Psalm 104.
104.8: shadow The mountains rose, the valleys sank down to the place that you appointed for them. Mountains rising and valleys sinking — the ordering of creation by the Word. The eschatological reversal: every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be made low (Luke 3:5, citing Isa 40:4) — the preparation for the coming of the Lord. The creator who raised mountains will level them for his own appearing.
104.9: shadow You set a boundary that they may not pass, so that they might not again cover the earth. The boundary set for the waters — the covenant with creation (Gen 9:11). Christ fulfills the Noah sign: the rainbow covenant (Gen 9:13-17) is the type of the new covenant in his blood. He who set the boundary for the seas is the one who says of the church: the gates of hell shall not prevail against it (Matt 16:18) — another boundary that holds.
104.10: revelation of God You make springs gush forth in the valleys; they flow between the hills; Springs in the valleys — the living water Christ gives: the water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). The creator God who makes springs gush in the valley is the same Lord who offers the Samaritan woman water that springs up to eternal life. Creation's provision is a parable of the gospel.
104.11: shadow they give drink to every beast of the field; the wild donkeys quench their thirst. Every creature given drink by God — the providential care that points to Christ's universal provision. Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them (Matt 6:26). The God who quenches the wild donkey's thirst is the Father Christ reveals, who cares for every creature.
104.12: shadow Beside them the birds of the heavens dwell; they sing among the branches. Birds singing in the branches — the imagery Christ uses for the kingdom: the kingdom of heaven is like a grain of mustard seed... it becomes a tree, so that the birds of the air come and make nests in its branches (Matt 13:31-32). The birds singing in creation's branches are the type of the nations finding rest in the kingdom of God.
104.13: shadow From your lofty abode you water the mountains; the earth is satisfied with the fruit of your work. Earth satisfied by God's fruit — the spiritual fruitfulness Christ produces: I chose you and appointed you that you should go and bear fruit and that your fruit should abide (John 15:16). The satisfied earth of Ps 104 is the type of the new creation where Christ's work produces permanent fruit that abides forever.
104.14: shadow You cause the grass to grow for the livestock and plants for man to cultivate, that he may bring forth food from the earth God providing food through creation — fulfilled in Christ the bread of life. The grass-and-grain provision of Ps 104 is good; Christ surpasses it: your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died... I am the living bread that came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever (John 6:49-51).
104.15: shadow and wine to gladden the heart of man, oil to make his face shine and bread to strengthen man's heart. Wine, oil, and bread — the eucharistic elements. Christ transforms ordinary wine (John 2:1-11 — the first miracle) and bread (the feeding miracles; the Last Supper) into the sacramental signs of the new covenant. The oil suggests anointing; Christ the Anointed One (Messiah) is himself the oil that makes the face shine (Ps 45:7 — anointed with the oil of gladness).
104.16: shadow The trees of the LORD are watered abundantly, the cedars of Lebanon that he planted. Trees planted and watered by the LORD — the righteous flourishing like cedars (Ps 92:12). Christ plants the church (John 15:1-5 — I am the vine, you are the branches) and waters it through the Spirit. The tree planted by the LORD is the image of the disciple rooted in Christ, bearing fruit in season (Ps 1:3; John 15:16).
104.17: shadow In them the birds build their nests; the stork has her home in the fir trees. Nesting birds finding home in God's trees — the kingdom parable again (Matt 13:31-32). Those who find their home in Christ find shelter in the one who is simultaneously the tree and the Creator who planted the tree. The nesting place is the abiding of John 15:4-5.
104.18: shadow The high mountains are for the wild goats; the rocks are a refuge for the rock badgers. Rocky refuge for small creatures — God's providential care for the vulnerable. Christ: not one sparrow will fall to the ground apart from your Father (Matt 10:29). Every creature finding its refuge in the created order is a parable of every soul finding refuge in Christ the Rock (1 Cor 10:4).
104.19: shadow He made the moon to mark the seasons; the sun knows its time for setting. Seasons and times ordered by God — the eschatological times ordered by Christ. It is not for you to know times or seasons that the Father has fixed by his own authority (Acts 1:7). The creator who set the moon and sun's times has fixed the day of Christ's return; as the sun knows its time, so the Son knows when the Father will send him again.
104.20: shadow You make darkness, and it is night, when all the beasts of the forest creep about. Darkness and night — the realm of the enemy that Christ invaded. The night is coming, when no one can work (John 9:4); the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it (John 1:5). Christ is the light that came into the world's night; the beasts that creep in darkness are routed by his presence.
104.21: shadow The young lions roar for their prey, seeking their food from God. Even predators seeking food from God — the providential care extended even to the violent. Christ: he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust (Matt 5:45). The common grace that feeds even lions is the goodness of the God Christ reveals — who sends rain on the fields of those who crucified him.
104.22: shadow When the sun rises, they steal away and lie down in their dens. Darkness and light alternating — the rhythm of creation that Christ transforms. He is the light that makes the darkness retreat permanently: the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb (Rev 21:23). The day-and-night rhythm ends in the new creation where there is no night.
104.23: shadow Man goes out to his work and to his labor until the evening. Human labor until evening — the workday Christ inhabited. He was a carpenter (Mark 6:3) who worked with his hands; in his ministry he said I must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming, when no one can work (John 9:4). The daily labor of the human being is the creation order Christ dignified by entering it.
104.24: revelation of God O LORD, how manifold are your works! In wisdom you have made them all; the earth is full of your creatures. Creation made in wisdom — Christ is the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24; Col 2:3 — in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge). Paul: for by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible (Col 1:16). The manifold works of God are the works of the Son through whom and for whom all things exist (Col 1:16-17).
104.25: shadow Here is the sea, great and wide, which teems with creatures innumerable, living things both small and great. The teeming sea — the harvest field of Christ's disciples. Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men (Matt 4:19). The sea teeming with creatures is the world teeming with people for whom Christ died; the net of the kingdom (Matt 13:47-50) gathers from this sea.
104.26: shadow There go the ships, and Leviathan, which you formed to play in it. Leviathan playing in the sea — the chaos monster tamed and made a plaything of God. Christ conquers the ultimate Leviathan: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him (Col 2:15). The serpent-dragon of Revelation (Rev 12:9; 20:2 — the ancient serpent, who is the devil) is defeated by the Lamb.
104.27: shadow These all look to you, to give them their food in due season. All creation looking to God for provision — the posture Christ taught: look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not of more value than they? (Matt 6:26). The looking of every creature to God is the posture of faith Christ calls humans to adopt toward the Father.
104.28: shadow When you give it to them, they gather it up; when you open your hand, they are filled with good things. God opening his hand to fill — the feeding miracles of Christ. He opens his hands in the wilderness (Matt 14:19 — taking the loaves, looking to heaven, blessing and breaking) and fills five thousand. He opens his hands on the cross to fill all who come to him: Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters (Isa 55:1).
104.29: shadow When you hide your face, they are dismayed; when you take away their breath, they die and return to their dust. The hidden face causing death — Christ's experience at the cross: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matt 27:46). The hiding of the divine face and the withdrawal of breath are the depths Christ entered on behalf of all who face the dismay of dying. His death exhausts the dismay so that his people face death with the Spirit's breath within them.
104.30: direct When you send forth your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the face of the ground. The Spirit sent to create and renew — Pentecost and the new creation. Christ breathes the Spirit on his disciples (John 20:22 — he breathed on them and said, Receive the Holy Spirit). The Spirit sent at Pentecost renews the face of the earth by creating the new covenant community: if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation (2 Cor 5:17). The renewing Spirit of Ps 104:30 is the Spirit of Christ.
104.31: shadow May the glory of the LORD endure forever; may the LORD rejoice in his works, The enduring glory of the LORD — Christ's glory that endures: the glory that you have given me I have given to them (John 17:22). The LORD who rejoices in his works rejoices in the new creation accomplished through Christ — the joy that was set before him (Heb 12:2) for which Christ endured the cross. Creation's joy and God's joy meet in the resurrection of the Son.
104.32: revelation of God who looks on the earth and it trembles, who touches the mountains and they smoke! The earth trembling at God's look — the earthquake and darkness at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51-54), the earthquake at the resurrection (Matt 28:2). The earth recognizes its Creator even when humanity does not. The centurion's response — truly this was the Son of God! — is the human echo of the earth's trembling recognition.
104.33: theme I will sing to the LORD as long as I live; I will sing praise to my God while I have being. Singing to God as long as I live — the eternal praise of those whose life is Christ: to live is Christ, and to die is gain (Phil 1:21). The praise that lasts as long as life lasts becomes, in Christ, eternal — for I am the resurrection and the life... whoever lives and believes in me shall never die (John 11:25-26). Endless living means endless singing.
104.34: theme May my meditation be pleasing to him, for I rejoice in the LORD. Meditation pleasing to God — the prayer Christ taught: meditation on God's word and works, grounded in the one who is himself the Word (John 1:1). Whatever is true... honorable... just... pure... lovely... commendable... think about these things (Phil 4:8). The meditation pleasing to God has Christ as its ultimate content and source.
104.35: shadow Let sinners be consumed from the earth, and let the wicked be no more! Bless the LORD, O my soul! Praise the LORD! Sinners consumed and the wicked no more — the eschatological cleansing of the new creation: nothing unclean will ever enter it, nor anyone who does what is detestable or false, but only those who are written in the Lamb's book of life (Rev 21:27). The final Praise the LORD! (Hallelujah) is the word of Revelation 19:1-6 at the consummation — all creation joining the Hallelujah! of Ps 104's closing word.

118.1: "Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever." The opening refrain of Psalm 118 is also its close (v29) — the psalm is framed by this liturgical declaration. In Jewish practice, Psalm 118 was the final psalm of the Egyptian Hallel (Ps 113-118), sung at Passover. Matthew 26:30 ("when they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives") — the hymn sung after the Last Supper and before Gethsemane was Psalm 118. Christ sang this psalm of rejected-stone-and-resurrection the night before his death. The "steadfast love endures forever" (ki le'olam hasdo) is the ground of the thanksgiving: what will be demonstrated in the next 24 hours is that the hesed that sent the Son into death will bring him through it.
118.2: "Let Israel declare: 'His steadfast love endures forever.'" The communal proclamation of enduring hesed — Israel's corporate testimony. In the NT, the Israel that declares this is redefined: Romans 9:6 ("not all who are descended from Israel are Israel") and Galatians 6:16 ("the Israel of God") describe the new covenant community as the heirs of Israel's worship tradition. The church that sings Psalm 118's refrain — "his steadfast love endures forever" — is the true Israel making the declaration that the psalm calls for. The body of Christ is the community that testifies to the resurrection as the ultimate act of enduring hesed.
118.3: "Let the house of Aaron declare: 'His steadfast love endures forever.'" The priestly witness to enduring hesed. In Christ, the Aaronic priesthood is superseded and fulfilled: Hebrews 7:11-19 argues that the Levitical/Aaronic priesthood was incomplete and that Christ as the priest "in the order of Melchizedek" (Heb 7:17) is the eternal high priest whose priesthood does not end with death. The "house of Aaron" that declares hesed endures forever finds its ultimate form in Christ, the eternal priest whose intercession never ceases (Heb 7:25: "he always lives to intercede for them").
118.4: "Let all who fear the LORD declare: 'His steadfast love endures forever.'" The expansion of the proclamation to all who fear YHWH — beyond Israel and the priesthood to the wider community of the faithful. Acts 10:35 ("in every nation the one who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him") and Revelation 15:4 ("all nations will come and worship before you") describe the universal community of hesed-declarers that the psalm's expansion envisions. In Christ, the wall dividing "Israel," "the house of Aaron," and "all who fear the LORD" is removed (Eph 2:14) — all become one body declaring the same enduring love.
118.5: "From the depths of my distress I called to the LORD; the LORD answered me and gave me room to breathe." The distress-call and the spacious answer — the pattern of Gethsemane and the resurrection. Hebrews 5:7 ("during the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent crying and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission") describes the distress-call of v5. The "room to breathe" (bamercav, a spacious place) is the resurrection's answer: the constriction of death is replaced by the spaciousness of risen life. What the psalmist experiences as temporary relief from distress, Christ experiences as the definitive escape from death's constriction.
118.6: "The LORD is on my side; I will not be afraid. What can any person do to me?" A direct citation: Hebrews 13:6 quotes this verse verbatim as a promise for believers: "So we say with confidence: 'The Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can mere mortals do to me?'" (Heb 13:6, citing Ps 118:6). The citation appears in the context of Hebrews 13:5 ("I will never leave you; never will I forsake you") — the two promises together constitute the ground of Christian fearlessness. Christ himself lived this verse: before Pilate (who could only kill the body, Matt 10:28), before the cross (which he endured without fear of the human agents, Heb 12:2), and in the resurrection (which proved that what persons did to him was not the final word).
118.7: "The LORD stands on my side as my helper; I will see my enemies brought to shame." The divine helper-role and the eventual shame of enemies — the pattern the resurrection enacts. Romans 8:31 ("if God is for us, who can be against us?") is the theological form of v7. The enemies brought to shame are not gloated over but are the negative of vindication: Acts 2:36 ("God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah") is the moment when those who put Christ to death see that their actions were the opposite of what they intended. The shame of Christ's enemies is the reverse side of the resurrection's vindication.
118.8: "It is better to take shelter in the LORD than to put your confidence in any person." The comparative trust claim — YHWH as shelter surpassing any human protection — is foundational to the NT's understanding of faith. John 2:24-25 ("Jesus did not entrust himself to them, for he knew all people... he knew what was in each person") shows Christ himself practicing v8: he did not put confidence in persons but in the Father alone. This is not misanthropy but theological realism — the only shelter that endures death is the divine shelter. The cross confirms it: every human protection failed (the disciples fled, Pilate capitulated, the crowds turned) but the divine shelter held.
118.9: "It is better to take shelter in the LORD than to put your confidence in rulers." The second comparative — rulers (nedivim, nobles/princes) as inferior to the divine shelter. Acts 4:12 ("salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved") is the NT equivalent: the rulers (Herod, Pilate, Caesar) cannot provide the salvation that YHWH in Christ provides. Jesus refused the political Messianism that would have made him a military or political leader (John 6:15: "knowing that they intended to come and make him king by force, Jesus withdrew"). The better shelter is not political power but the divine presence.
118.10: "All the nations closed in around me, but in the name of the LORD I routed them." The nations surrounding the anointed — the international hostility that Psalm 2 also describes ("the nations rage... against the LORD and against his anointed"). Acts 4:25-27 quotes Psalm 2 in response to the trial of Peter and John: "the nations raged, the peoples plotted in vain... Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel in this city to conspire against your holy servant Jesus." The nations of Ps 118:10 are the coalition of Ps 2:1-2, now identified specifically as the parties to the crucifixion. "In the name of the LORD I routed them" — the resurrection.
118.11: "They surrounded me — closed in from every side — but in the name of the LORD I cut them off." The encirclement intensified — surrounding from every direction. The passion-week encirclement: the chief priests surrounding Jesus with questions (Matt 21:23; 22:15-46), the soldiers surrounding the garden (John 18:3-12), the crowd surrounding the cross (Luke 23:35). Each act of encirclement intends to trap or destroy; each is answered by the name of YHWH. The name invoked against death is the resurrection name — the God who raises the dead breaks every encirclement that death constructs around the living.
118.12: "They swarmed around me like bees, but blazed out as quickly as a brush fire; in the name of the LORD I cut them off." The bee-swarm image of hostile attack — quick, multiple, painful — against the single figure who invokes the divine name. The brush fire that blazes up and quickly dies: the opposition that appeared overwhelming is ultimately transient. James 4:14 ("you are a mist that appears for a little while and then vanishes") and 1 Corinthians 15:55 ("where, O death, is your sting?") describe the transience of even the most overwhelming opposition. The bee's sting — death — is removed by the resurrection. The name invoked is the resurrection name.
118.13: "I was shoved to the point of falling, but the LORD was there to help me." The near-fall — the moment of maximum precarity before divine intervention. "Shoved to the point of falling" is the crucifixion language: pushed to the very edge of death, driven to the ground by the weight of the cross, nailed to the wood. Luke 23:26 ("as the soldiers led him away, they seized Simon from Cyrene... and put the cross on him to carry it behind Jesus") shows Jesus at the point of physical collapse under the cross's weight. "But the LORD was there to help" — the resurrection is the help that arrives at the moment of maximum apparent defeat. Psalm 22:24 makes the same claim: "he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one... but has listened to his cry for help."
118.14: "The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation." A citation from Exodus 15:2 — the song of Moses at the Red Sea after the crossing. The same words appear in Isaiah 12:2 (the eschatological thanksgiving for the new Exodus). The NT reads the resurrection as the new Exodus: 1 Corinthians 10:1-4 (the Exodus as a type of baptism and the spiritual food and drink = Christ). Luke 9:31 calls the cross and resurrection an "exodus" (exodon) that Jesus was about to accomplish in Jerusalem. Christ is himself the strength, song, and salvation of the new Exodus — the one who leads through the sea of death and brings his people to the other side.
118.15: "Shouts of joy and victory echo in the tents of the righteous: 'The right hand of the LORD does mighty things!'" The victory shout in the community of the righteous — the resurrection announcement reverberating through those who belong to Christ. Luke 24:33-35 (the two from Emmaus returning to the eleven: "the Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon!") is the specific historical moment of the victory shout echoing in the tents of the righteous. The "right hand of the LORD" that does mighty things is the right hand of Acts 2:33 ("exalted to the right hand of God, he has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit and has poured out what you now see and hear").
118.16: "The right hand of the LORD is raised high; the right hand of the LORD does mighty things!" The doubled acclamation of the divine right hand — the repeated insistence on this single phrase marks it as the theological center of the victory song. Acts 2:33 ("exalted to the right hand of God") and Hebrews 1:3 ("he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty in heaven") give the exaltation-form of the raised right hand. Revelation 1:16 ("in his right hand he held seven stars") shows the risen Christ with the right hand still active in his exalted state. The right hand raised high is the universal sovereignty displayed in the resurrection and ascension.
118.17: "I will not die — I will live, and I will tell of what the LORD has done." The resurrection declaration. This verse is the center of the psalm's Christological meaning. The one who was shoved to the point of falling (v13), who was disciplined hard (v18), does not remain in death. John 10:17-18 ("I lay down my life — only to take it up again... no one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord") is the self-determination that v17 asserts: the declaration "I will live" is not wishful thinking but the sovereign announcement of the one who holds the keys of death and Hades (Rev 1:18). "I will tell of what the LORD has done" — the resurrection testimony: Luke 24:46-47; Acts 1:3 ("appearing to them over a period of forty days and speaking about the kingdom of God").
118.18: "The LORD has disciplined me hard, but he has not let death have me." The hard discipline and the limit on death — what Hebrews 5:8 ("although he was a son, he learned obedience from what he suffered") and Hebrews 12:6 ("the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and he chastens everyone he accepts as his son") explicate. The discipline is real and severe (the cross), but it has a boundary: "he has not let death have me." Acts 2:24 ("it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him") is the NT statement of this boundary. The hard discipline of the cross is the instrument of salvation; death is the doorway, not the terminus. The disciplined-but-not-destroyed pattern is then shared with believers (2 Cor 4:9: "struck down, but not destroyed").
118.19: "Open the righteous gates for me so I can go in and give thanks to the LORD." The entry through the righteous gates — the approach to the divine presence for worship. John 10:9 ("I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved") is the inverse application: Christ is not the one asking for the gate to be opened but is himself the gate through which others enter. Hebrews 10:19-20 ("we have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place by the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way opened for us through the curtain, that is, his body") is the entry-through-Christ that v19 anticipates. The opening of the temple veil at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51) is the moment when the righteous gate is opened through the death of Christ.
118.20: "This is the LORD's own gate; only the righteous may enter through it." The divine ownership of the gate and the righteousness requirement. John 14:6 ("I am the way and the truth and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me") is the exclusivity of v20 fulfilled in Christ — the one gate through whom the righteous enter is the personal Christ. Romans 3:22 ("righteousness is given through faith in Jesus Christ to all who believe") solves the problem of who qualifies as "the righteous": those who enter are those made righteous in Christ, not those who achieve righteousness by their own effort. The gate that admits only the righteous becomes accessible to all who trust the one who has become their righteousness (1 Cor 1:30).
118.21: "I praise you because you answered me and have become my salvation." The personal thanksgiving that follows the entry through the gate — the individual voice within the communal psalm. This is the voice that Christ lifts in the resurrection: the Father answered his prayer (Heb 5:7), becoming his salvation through the resurrection. In John 11:41-42 ("Father, I thank you that you have heard me... I knew that you always hear me"), Christ models the personal thanksgiving of v21 — the assurance that the Father's answer is certain, spoken before the visible evidence arrives. V21 is the personal doxology of the one who has come through the shoved-to-the-point-of-falling (v13) and the hard discipline (v18) to the thanksgiving side of death.
118.22: "The stone that the builders threw aside has become the cornerstone." The most widely cited verse from the Psalms in the NT. Christ applies it to himself immediately after the parable of the wicked tenants (Matt 21:42: "have you never read in the Scriptures: 'The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone'?"). Peter cites it in the Jerusalem council (Acts 4:11: "Jesus is 'the stone you builders rejected, which has become the cornerstone'"). 1 Peter 2:7 cites it for believers: "the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone" — and then continues to Isa 8:14 (a stone of stumbling for those who reject). Ephesians 2:20 ("with Christ Jesus himself as the chief cornerstone") is the ecclesial application. The rejected-and-exalted stone is Jesus's self-understanding of his death and resurrection: rejected by the builders of Israel's temple establishment, raised by the Father to the place of maximum structural importance.
118.23: "This is what the LORD has done; it is wonderful in our sight." Cited together with v22 in Matthew 21:42, the wonderfulness of the rejected-stone-become-cornerstone is the astonishment of the resurrection. Acts 2:12 ("amazed and perplexed, they asked one another, 'What does this mean?'") and Acts 3:10 ("they were filled with wonder and amazement") capture the niphla'ot (wonderful) that v23 announces. "In our sight" (be'einenu) — the resurrection is a public event witnessed by many (1 Cor 15:5-8: "more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at the same time, most of whom are still living"). The wonder is not private mystical experience but public divine act.
118.24: "This is the day the LORD has made; let us celebrate and be glad in it." The resurrection day — the Lord's Day. The early church met on "the first day of the week" (Acts 20:7; 1 Cor 16:2; Rev 1:10: "on the Lord's Day") specifically because the resurrection occurred on the first day (Matt 28:1; John 20:1). Psalm 118:24 is the OT ground of the Lord's Day theology: the day YHWH has made — not made in the sense of creating it in time, but made in the sense of investing it with unique divine significance through the resurrection act. The celebration and gladness are the liturgical form of Easter faith: Luke 24:41 ("they still did not believe it because of joy and amazement") is the first Easter response.
118.25: "LORD, save us, we pray! LORD, grant us success, we pray!" The hoshi'ah-na (Hosanna — save now!) and the anna YHWH hatzlichah na (grant success now). Matthew 21:9 records the crowd's cry at the triumphal entry as "Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD! Hosanna in the highest heaven!" — taking both v25 and v26 and directing them to Jesus. Mark 11:9 ("Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD!") similarly. The Palm Sunday cry is the Passover hallel psalm applied to Jesus as the arriving Davidic king: those crying "Hosanna" were singing the psalm they sang annually and now applying it to the one who had come to fulfill it.
118.26: "May God bless the one who comes in the name of the LORD! We bless you from the LORD's house." A direct citation: Matthew 21:9 (Palm Sunday); Matthew 23:39 ("you will not see me again until you say, 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord'") — Christ applies this verse to his own second coming. Luke 13:35 (same citation). John 12:13 (the crowd at the triumphal entry: "blessed is the king of Israel!"). The "one who comes" (haba) is a Messianic title: Luke 7:19-20 (John the Baptist's question: "are you the one who is to come?"). The blessing from "the LORD's house" is the temple establishment's endorsement — ironically, the temple leadership's rejection of Jesus fulfills the rejected-stone of v22, while the crowd's Palm Sunday acclamation fulfills v26.
118.27: "The LORD is God, and his light has shone on us. Bind the sacrifice with cords and bring it all the way to the horns of the altar." The light shining and the sacrifice bound to the altar — two powerful Christological images. John 1:9 ("the true light that gives light to everyone was coming into the world") is the light-shone-on-us of v27. John 8:12 ("I am the light of the world") is the personal claim. The sacrifice bound with cords: at the crucifixion, Christ is bound (John 18:12: "the detachment of soldiers... arrested Jesus; they bound him") and brought to the place of sacrifice. "All the way to the horns of the altar" — the horns of the altar were the place of ultimate refuge (1 Kgs 1:50; 2:28) and of the sacrifice's blood application. Christ is brought all the way to the cross as the altar of his own sacrifice (Heb 13:10-12).
118.28: "You are my God, and I will give you thanks; you are my God, and I will praise you." The personal covenant declaration — "you are my God" — spoken by the one who has passed through the shoved-to-falling (v13) and the hard discipline (v18) to reach the thanksgiving. This is the voice of the risen Christ addressing the Father. John 20:17 (Christ to Mary: "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God") uses the same possessive: "my God" — the risen Christ maintains the personal covenantal relationship with the Father that sustained him through the passion. The double "you are my God" and the double thanksgiving/praise are the emphatic personal testimony of the resurrection's survivor.
118.29: "Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever." The closing refrain — the psalm ends where it began. Having journeyed through distress, encirclement, near-fall, hard discipline, and the rejected stone that becomes the cornerstone, the psalm arrives back at the opening declaration of divine goodness and enduring hesed. The resurrection is the proof: the hesed of YHWH endured through the entire passion and came out the other side unchanged. Revelation 5:9-13 (the four living creatures and the elders and ten thousand angels singing to the Lamb) is the eschatological form of this closing thanksgiving — the entire creation joins the hallel that Psalm 118 invites, because the one who was rejected and raised has been given all honor and glory and power forever.

105.1: A theme: the missional call — "make his deeds known among the nations" — is the OT anticipation of the Great Commission (Matt 28:19-20; Acts 1:8). What the psalm calls Israel to do for YHWH, the NT says Christ accomplishes through his followers. The nations are the audience not of religious information but of the proclaimed deeds of God in history, now centered on the Christ-event.
105.2: A theme: the praise of God expressed in singing is taken up in Christ, who leads his brothers in worship. Heb 2:12 applies Ps 22:22 to Christ: "I will declare your name to my brothers; in the midst of the assembly I will sing your praise." The song that the psalm calls Israel to sing is the song Christ himself sings as the firstborn among many brothers (Rom 8:29).
105.3: A theme: "boast in his holy name" — Phil 2:9-11 names the name above every name as the name of Jesus; Gal 6:14 says "may I never boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ." The holy name in which Israel boasts is ultimately the name of the Son, who perfectly bears and expresses the character of the Father (John 1:18; 17:6).
105.4: A theme: "seek the LORD and his power; seek his presence at all times" — in Christ this seeking is answered definitively. John 14:6 ("I am the way and the truth and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me") and John 1:18 present Christ as the presence and power of God made accessible. To seek God's face is now to encounter the face of Christ (2 Cor 4:6).
105.5: A revelation of God: YHWH is the God who performs signs, wonders, and judgments — his character disclosed through mighty acts. Jesus's signs in John's Gospel stand in this tradition: John 6:14 identifies him as "the Prophet who is to come" after the feeding miracle; the signs reveal that the God of the Exodus is present and acting in Christ. "Wonderful deeds" (niplāʾôt) frames the signs as theological disclosure, not merely miracle.
105.6: A theme: "children of Abraham, his servant" — Gal 3:29 redefines this identity in Christ: "if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's offspring, heirs according to the promise." The covenant lineage is not cancelled but fulfilled — the "children of Abraham" now includes all who share Abraham's faith, united in Christ the true seed (Gal 3:16: "the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed... meaning one person, who is Christ").
105.7: A revelation of God: YHWH is universal judge — "his judgments are in all the earth." The NT presents Christ as the appointed agent of this universal judgment: "the Father judges no one, but has entrusted all judgment to the Son" (John 5:22); "he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed" (Acts 17:31). Universal sovereignty is already implicit in the psalm and made explicit in Christ.
105.8: A direct connection: "he keeps his covenant in memory forever" — Luke 1:54-55 and 1:72-73 say God "remembered his covenant" in the Incarnation. The canticles of Luke deliberately invoke this theological tradition: YHWH's covenant memory, kept "forever," is activated in Christ. The new covenant (Luke 22:20 — "this cup is the new covenant in my blood") is the ultimate expression of covenant-memory fulfilled in blood.
105.9: A type: "the covenant he confirmed to Abraham, and his sworn promise to Isaac" — Paul calls this the proto-Gospel in Gal 3:8 ("the Scripture foresaw that God would justify the Gentiles by faith and announced the gospel in advance to Abraham"). Gal 3:15-17 argues that Christ is the mediator of this covenant; what was made with Abraham was always pointing to Christ as the singular heir through whom the blessing flows to all nations.
105.10: A theme: "he confirmed it to Jacob as a decree, to Israel as an everlasting statute" — Christ's statement "I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it" (Matt 5:17) answers this everlasting statute. The covenant established with the patriarchs is not voided but brought to its intended goal (telos) in Christ (Rom 10:4). The statute is everlasting because the one who fulfills it is eternal.
105.11: A shadow: "to you I will give the land of Canaan as your inheritance" — the land promise is the first layer of a theological theme that expands in the NT. Heb 11:16 says the patriarchs were looking for "a better country — a heavenly one"; Matt 5:5 reinterprets "the meek shall inherit the land" as inheriting the earth; Rev 21:1-4 presents the renewed creation as the fulfillment. What Israel received as a down payment in Canaan, the redeemed receive in full through the Heir of all things (Heb 1:2).
105.12: A revelation of God: God works through the small and vulnerable to accomplish his purposes. The incarnation of Christ into a marginal people under Roman occupation, born in a stable with no room at the inn (Luke 2:7), continues the pattern. Divine election runs counter to human expectations of power and size: "God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong" (1 Cor 1:27).
105.13: A revelation of God: "they wandered from nation to nation, from one kingdom to another people" — the patriarchal sojourn as the condition of covenant belonging. Heb 11:13-16 calls them "strangers and exiles on the earth." Christ himself was a refugee in Egypt (Matt 2:13-15) and had "nowhere to lay his head" (Luke 9:58), sharing the sojourner-condition of the covenant people and validating it as the shape of life under the cross.
105.14: A revelation of God: "he allowed no one to oppress them; he rebuked kings on their behalf" — divine protection of the covenant line through which Christ would come. The same providential protection guards the line of promise from the patriarchs to the flight into Egypt (Matt 2:13-15). God guards the line of promise because that line ultimately produces the Messiah.
105.15: A direct connection: "do not touch my anointed ones; do not harm my prophets" — meshîḥîm (anointed ones) is the plural of meshîḥ (Messiah/Christ). The term here protects the patriarchs but points forward to the Anointed One par excellence. The protected prophets form the chain of testimony culminating in Christ — who alone is ultimately "harmed" precisely so that harm falls on no one else.
105.16: A type: "when he called down famine over the land" — divine sovereign ordering of events to bring Joseph to prominence in Egypt. This launches the Joseph narrative, the most extended OT type of Christ. The famine that creates the crisis God uses to exalt his chosen servant anticipates the circumstances through which Christ's path to glory runs: through the cross to the crown (Phil 2:8-9).
105.17: A type: "he sent a man ahead of them — Joseph, sold as a slave" — Acts 7:9 treats Joseph as a direct antecedent of Jesus, both handed over by those who should have received them. Sold for twenty pieces of silver by his brothers (Gen 37:28) / Jesus sold for thirty by his disciple (Matt 26:15; cf. Zech 11:12-13). The "sending ahead" — prior to the salvation he would accomplish — types Christ sent as the forerunner who prepares the way (John 14:2-3; Heb 6:20).
105.18: A type: "they bruised his feet with shackles, his neck was put in an iron collar" — Joseph's imprisonment and physical suffering precede his exaltation. Phil 2:8 follows the same pattern: "he humbled himself and became obedient to death." The iron collar anticipates the crown of thorns; the shackles anticipate the nails. Humiliation preceding exaltation is the irreducible pattern of both Joseph and Christ.
105.19: A type: "this lasted until what he had foretold came true — all the while the word of the LORD was testing him" — the divine word that predicted Joseph's dreams tested him by delaying fulfillment. In Christ, "when the fullness of time had come" (Gal 4:4) — God acted according to a precise divine timetable. Christ's suffering was the test the word of God worked through before the promise was fulfilled (Heb 5:8: "he learned obedience from what he suffered").
105.20: A type: "the king sent word and freed him; the ruler of the peoples set him at liberty" — Joseph's release from imprisonment to royal authority is one of the OT's most direct resurrection types. Acts 2:24 uses the same logic for Christ: "God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him." The imprisoned servant released by divine intervention to rule is the pattern both embody.
105.21: A type: "he made him master of his household, ruler of all he possessed" — Joseph's exaltation to Pharaoh's right hand (Gen 41:40-43) types Christ's session at the Father's right hand. Ps 110:1 ("sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool") is the explicit messianic application; Eph 1:20-22 applies this to Christ: God "seated him at his right hand... and placed all things under his feet." The Joseph-figure exalted to the ruler's side is the OT's structural preview.
105.22: A type: "to instruct his princes at his pleasure and teach his elders wisdom" — Joseph's authority to instruct the powerful in Egypt anticipates Christ's authority as teacher. Matt 7:29 says Jesus taught "as one who had authority, not as their teachers of the law"; 1 Cor 1:30 calls Christ "our wisdom from God." The exalted servant who teaches the powerful shadows the exalted Christ who is wisdom incarnate.
105.23: A type: "Israel entered Egypt, Jacob sojourned in Ham's territory" — Matt 2:14-15 cites this pattern directly, applying Hos 11:1 ("out of Egypt I called my son") to Jesus's sojourn in Egypt. The evangelist reads the Exodus typologically: Israel-in-Egypt types Christ-in-Egypt; the calling "out of Egypt" fulfilled first in the Exodus is the pattern re-enacted in Christ's return. The descent into Egypt is a type of the incarnation — the Son entering foreign territory before being called home.
105.24: A revelation of God: "the LORD made his people very fruitful" — multiplication of the covenant people despite opposition is YHWH's pattern. John 15:5 ("those who remain in me... will bear much fruit") applies the growth-pattern to Christ and his people. The fruitfulness of Israel in Egypt types the fruitfulness of the Church in an opposed world, both dependent on divine blessing, not human strategy.
105.25: A revelation of God: "he turned their hearts to hate his people" — divine sovereignty ordering even the hostility of Pharaoh. Acts 4:27-28 uses the same logic for the crucifixion: Herod and Pilate, Gentiles and Israel "did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen." Even Christ's enemies serve the purposes of the one who "turned their hearts," making the cross the supreme example of God's sovereignty over hostile human will.
105.26: A type: "he sent Moses his servant, and Aaron whom he had chosen" — Moses the servant-deliverer prefigures Christ in Deut 18:15 ("a prophet like me"), applied to Jesus in Acts 3:22. Aaron prefigures the high priest: Heb 5:4 says Aaron was called by God, not self-appointed; Christ likewise (Heb 5:5-6). Both prophet and high priest are united in Christ, who is simultaneously the prophet who speaks God's word and the priest who mediates through sacrifice.
105.27: A type: "they performed his signs among them, worked wonders in Egypt" — the signs of Moses and Aaron type Jesus's signs. John 6:14 after the feeding miracle: "Surely this is the Prophet who is to come" — the crowd reads the sign against the background of Moses's wonder-working. Where the Mosaic signs pointed to Sinai, the Johannine signs point to the cross and resurrection as the definitive act of divine self-disclosure.
105.28: A shadow: "he sent darkness and made the land dark" — the ninth plague (Exod 10:21-23) anticipates the darkness at the crucifixion (Matt 27:45: "from the sixth hour to the ninth hour darkness came over all the land"). Both are signs of divine judgment; the darkness at Calvary signals that Christ is absorbing the judgment that the Egyptian plagues historically embodied — the plague falls on the Deliverer, not his people.
105.29: A shadow: the plagues are instruments of covenant judgment. Gal 3:13 ("Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us") is the NT logic: the curse-judgments that fell on Egypt in the Exodus are previews of the judgment Christ absorbs at Calvary. His people are delivered through his blood — as Israel was, through the Passover blood on the doorposts — not by the blood of their enemies.
105.30: A shadow: "their land swarmed with frogs, even in the chambers of their kings" — the plague that invaded the centers of worldly power. In Christ the Gospel itself penetrates "even in Caesar's household" (Phil 4:22). The divine advance against human power-structures previewed in the Exodus plagues continues in the spread of Christ's kingdom — no human power, however entrenched, is beyond the reach of God's intervention.
105.31: A revelation of God: "he spoke and there came swarms of flies, gnats throughout their territory" — God accomplishes judgment through his word alone. "He spoke and it came to be" (Ps 33:9). Christ is the Word through whom all things were made and through whom all divine purposes are accomplished (John 1:3; Heb 1:3 — "sustaining all things by his powerful word").
105.32: A shadow: "he gave them hail for rain, lightning through their land" — storm as divine judgment. The seventh plague (Exod 9:23-26) becomes eschatological judgment imagery in Rev 8:7; 11:19; 16:21. Christ's stilling of the storm (Mark 4:39) shows his authority over the elements that bring judgment — he is master of the powers that embody divine wrath, pointing to his authority to face judgment at Calvary and emerge victorious.
105.33: A shadow: "he struck their vines and fig trees, shattered the trees" — the fig tree is a prophetic symbol for Israel (Jer 8:13; Hos 9:10; Joel 1:7). Christ's cursing of the barren fig tree (Mark 11:12-14, 20-21) is a prophetic sign-act echoing the plague-judgments: unfruitful religious structures under divine judgment. The destroyed trees of the plague become the withered fig tree of the Passion week.
105.34: A shadow: "he spoke and the locusts came, grasshoppers without number" — the locust plague (Exod 10:4-19) is the background for Rev 9:3 and the eschatological locust-judgment of Joel 1-2. Christ who averts the plague for those under his blood (1 Cor 5:7 — "our Passover lamb") brings the full harvest-judgment on those who are not. The plague-averted becomes the eschatological judgment only the Lamb can prevent.
105.35: A shadow: "they consumed every plant in their land" — total destruction as covenantal judgment. Heb 12:29 ("our God is a consuming fire") stands in this tradition. Christ who is the vine (John 15:1) warns that fruitless branches are "thrown into the fire and burned" (John 15:6). The consuming judgment of the Exodus plagues reaches its final form in Christ's eschatological judgment at the harvest.
105.36: A type: "he struck down every firstborn in their land" — the Passover judgment. 1 Cor 5:7 makes the typological link explicit: "Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed." The death of Egypt's firstborn is averted for Israel by the lamb's blood; the final judgment is averted for all under the blood of Christ, who is himself "the firstborn from the dead" (Col 1:18; Rev 1:5) — the firstborn who died so that God's firstborn-people might live.
105.37: A type: "he brought his people out loaded with silver and gold; not one person in all their tribes was weak" — the Exodus plunder and the strength of the redeemed. Col 2:15 applies plunder-language to the cross: Christ "disarmed the powers and authorities, making a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them." The Exodus plunder of Egypt types Christ's despoiling of the enemy at Calvary; the redeemed people leave strong because their deliverer has prevailed.
105.38: A revelation of God: "Egypt was glad when they left, for dread of them had fallen on them" — reversal of the oppressor-and-oppressed dynamic. In Christ's resurrection the ultimate reversal occurs: the powers that crucified him are defeated. Acts 2:36 ("God has made this Jesus both Lord and Christ") declares that the executed one is now the feared one — the full reversal the Exodus only previewed.
105.39: A type: "he spread out a cloud as a shelter and gave fire to light the way through the night" — the pillar of cloud and fire is the Shekinah, the localized divine presence with Israel. John 1:14 ("the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us — and we have seen his glory") is the explicit fulfillment. Christ is the cloud and fire — the divine presence made incarnate; John 8:12 ("I am the light of the world") applies the fire-pillar image directly to himself.
105.40: A type: "they asked for food and he brought them quail, satisfying them with bread from heaven" — this text stands directly behind Jesus's feeding miracles and John 6's bread-of-life discourse. John 6:31-35 quotes the manna tradition and Jesus responds: "my Father gives you the true bread from heaven... I am the bread of life." The manna gave temporary physical life; Christ gives life that endures forever (John 6:51). The type is fulfilled in the one who is himself the bread.
105.41: A type: "he split open the rock and water poured out" — 1 Cor 10:4 states the typological connection explicitly: "they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ." The water from the rock (Exod 17:6; Num 20:11) is the NT's most directly named Christ-type. John 7:37-38 applies the same imagery to the Spirit poured out through Christ: "rivers of living water will flow from within them" — water from the rock becomes the Spirit from the crucified side (John 19:34).
105.42: A direct connection: "for he had called to mind his holy promise to Abraham his servant" — Luke 1:72-73 says God "remembered his holy covenant, the oath he swore to our father Abraham." The Exodus is the first fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise; the Incarnation is the ultimate fulfillment. In Christ, God permanently "remembers" Abraham — the promise is not merely kept but fully redeemed in the one who is Abraham's seed (Gal 3:16).
105.43: A type: "he led his people out with rejoicing, his chosen ones with shouts of joy" — the New Exodus motif of Isa 51:11; 55:12 is fulfilled in Christ. 1 Pet 1:8 describes the joy of those redeemed by Christ: "you believe in him and are filled with an inexpressible and glorious joy." The joyful departure from bondage types the eschatological rejoicing of the redeemed, who "come to Mount Zion" with "everlasting joy upon their heads" (Isa 35:10).
105.44: A shadow: "he gave them the lands of other nations, and they took possession of what others had worked for" — Israel's inheritance of Canaan types the inheritance of believers in Christ. Rom 8:17 ("co-heirs with Christ"); Heb 1:2 ("appointed heir of all things"); Rev 21:7 ("the one who is victorious will inherit all this") — the land-gift expands to cosmic scope. What Israel received as a down payment in Canaan, the redeemed receive in full through the Heir of all things.
105.45: A revelation of God: "so that they would keep his decrees and obey his teachings — Praise the LORD!" — the Exodus's stated purpose: covenant-obedience. This goal is fulfilled in Christ through the new covenant: "I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts" (Jer 31:33; Heb 8:10-11). What the Exodus could not achieve by law alone — willing, inward obedience — the Spirit of Christ accomplishes (Ezek 36:26-27; Rom 8:4). The psalm's closing praise is the response to grace-enabled obedience becoming finally possible.

106.1: A revelation of God: "for he is good; his steadfast love endures forever" — the foundational covenant-affirmation. In Eph 2:4-7, Paul's account of salvation begins with precisely this: "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love for us..." The hesed praised in this doxology is the very attribute expressed definitively in Christ's self-giving. The goodness of God toward the undeserving is the theme of both Psalm 106 and the Gospel.
106.2: A theme: "who is able to speak all the LORD's mighty deeds?" — the question implies the answer is no mere human. John 1:18 provides it: "No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known." Christ is the one who definitively declares the Father's mighty deeds because he both performs them and is himself their content (John 5:19-20).
106.3: A theme: "blessed are those who uphold justice and do what is right at every moment" — Matt 5:6 echoes this beatitude form: "blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be satisfied." Christ both perfectly embodies this blessing (Matt 3:15: "fulfill all righteousness") and gives it as a gift: 1 Cor 1:30 says Christ "has become for us... our righteousness." The one who blesses is the one who fulfills what the blessing requires.
106.4: A direct connection: "remember me, O LORD, when you show favor to your people" — the same plea appears on the dying thief's lips: "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom" (Luke 23:42). Christ's response — "today you will be with me in paradise" — is the divine answer. He is the one through whom YHWH "shows favor"; the petitioner who prays to be remembered is answered in Christ who carries the petitioner into his own kingdom and life.
106.5: A theme: "so that I may see the well-being of your chosen ones, share the joy of your nation" — the petition to be included among the covenant people. Eph 1:4-5 says believers were "chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world" and "predestined for adoption as sons through Jesus Christ." The identity that was Israel's by birth becomes available to all through union with Christ — "the Chosen One" (Luke 23:35) who includes others in his election.
106.6: A direct connection: "we have sinned, just as our fathers did" — universal sin across generations. Rom 3:23 ("all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God") and Rom 5:12 expand this generational confession to all humanity. Christ is the one who takes on the full weight of this accumulated sin: "God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God" (2 Cor 5:21). The psalm's confession is precisely what Christ bears.
106.7: A revelation of God: "they did not remember your abundant steadfast love" — the failure of memory that leads to covenant failure. Jesus institutes the Eucharist as the direct remedy: "do this in remembrance of me" (1 Cor 11:24-25). The Last Supper creates a perpetual memory-practice for the covenant community — the answer to Israel's forgetfulness is a covenant meal designed to keep Christ's saving act in perpetual, enacted memory.
106.8: A direct connection: "but he saved them for the sake of his own name, to demonstrate his mighty power" — God's salvation is for the honor of his name. John 17:6 ("I have revealed your name") and 12:28 ("Father, glorify your name!") show Christ understanding his mission in these terms. Rom 3:25-26 argues that God presented Christ as a propitiation "to demonstrate his righteousness." Salvation accomplishes both rescue and divine self-vindication.
106.9: A type: "he rebuked the Red Sea and it dried up; he led them through the depths as through a desert" — 1 Cor 10:1-2 interprets the sea-crossing as a baptismal type: "they were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea." Baptism into Christ (Rom 6:3-4) is the fulfillment: passing through death-waters to new life. The Red Sea is the precursor of the cross — the people pass through the waters of judgment because the road has been opened by divine power over chaos.
106.10: A type: "he saved them from the hand of the one who hated them and redeemed them from the power of the enemy" — the Exodus as redemption from enemy power. Col 1:13 uses the same language: "he has rescued us from the dominion of darkness." Heb 2:14-15 says Christ shared in flesh and blood "so that by his death he might break the power of him who holds the power of death — that is, the devil." The enemy is personalized and the rescue is through death itself.
106.11: A shadow: "the waters covered their enemies; not one of them remained" — the destruction of Pharaoh's army. Rev 19:11-21 draws on Exodus imagery for the final destruction of the beast and his armies. The eschatological "sea giving up its dead" (Rev 20:13) and the beast cast into the lake of fire complete the trajectory of this divine military victory. Christ as the cosmic warrior of Rev 19:11-16 is the fulfillment of this total divine victory.
106.12: A theme: "then they believed his words and sang his praise" — faith responding to salvation in song. This is the pattern of Gospel response. The Song of Moses (Exod 15) is the template for Revelation's "song of Moses and of the Lamb" (Rev 15:3-4) — the redeemed singing their salvation. Faith, praise, and song are the response of those saved by Christ, replicating the pattern established at the sea.
106.13: A revelation of God: "but soon they forgot what he had done and did not wait for his counsel" — forgetting as the root of covenant failure. The Eucharist is Christ's direct response to this pattern: "do this in remembrance of me" (1 Cor 11:24-25). The Last Supper creates a perpetual memory-practice for the covenant community — directly addressing the forgetfulness the psalm laments. Covenant memory is constitutive of covenant faithfulness.
106.14: A type: "they craved intensely in the wilderness and put God to the test in the desert" — the wilderness testing of Israel is what Jesus retraced and reversed. Matt 4:1-11 places Jesus in the wilderness where he is tested at precisely the points where Israel failed: bread, testing God, idolatry. Heb 4:15 confirms: "we have a high priest... tempted in every way, just as we are — yet was without sin." Israel failed the wilderness test; Christ passed it, accomplishing what Israel could not.
106.15: A revelation of God: "he gave them what they asked for but sent a wasting disease into their lives" — the tragic dynamic of getting what you demand apart from God's true provision.
