God dividing the land in holy promise — the land of Canaan was the type; the inheritance Christ secures is the new heavens and new earth (Rev 21:1). Heb 11:10: the patriarchs looked for a city whose builder and maker is God. God's holy promise of the land finds its ultimate fulfillment in the eternal inheritance (1 Pet 1:4).
108.8: shadow Gilead is mine; Manasseh is mine; Ephraim is my helmet; Judah is my scepter. Judah as God's scepter — Gen 49:10: the scepter shall not depart from Judah... until tribute comes to him. Christ, of the tribe of Judah (Heb 7:14; Rev 5:5 — the Lion of the tribe of Judah), is the fulfillment of the Judah-scepter: the ruler from Judah to whom the nations belong.
108.9: shadow Moab is my washbasin; upon Edom I cast my shoe; over Philistia I shout in triumph. The surrounding nations subdued — the universal lordship of Christ. Rev 17:14: the Lamb will conquer them, for he is Lord of lords and King of kings. The enemies of Israel are types of the spiritual forces Christ overcomes at the cross: he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him (Col 2:15).
108.10: shadow Who will bring me to the fortified city? Who will lead me to Edom? Asking who will lead to the fortified city — the question Christ answers. He is the way to the city no human strategy can reach (John 14:6). The forerunner who enters the inner place behind the curtain on our behalf (Heb 6:19-20) is the one who brings us to the fortified city no army can take by force.
108.11: shadow Have you not rejected us, O God? You do not go out, O God, with our armies. God seeming to abandon — the darkness of Gethsemane and Golgotha. Christ enters the experience of divine absence: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? (Matt 27:46) — so that God will never truly abandon those who are in him (Heb 13:5: I will never leave you nor forsake you). His cry purchased the promise of perpetual presence.
108.12: shadow Oh grant us help against the foe, for vain is the salvation of man. Human salvation is vain — the inadequacy of all human help drives the necessity of divine rescue. Salvation belongs to the LORD (Jonah 2:9). No human effort reaches the depth of need; only the divine-human Savior is sufficient. Acts 4:12: there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.
108.13: shadow With God we shall do valiantly; it is he who will tread down our foes. Valor and victory through God — the confidence of Rom 8:37: in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us. The courage to do valiantly is not human bravado but faith in the one who has already trodden down the ultimate foe: the last enemy to be destroyed is death (1 Cor 15:26), and Christ destroys it in his resurrection.

109.1: shadow Be not silent, O God of my praise! Calling on God not to be silent in the face of accusation — the prayer Christ implicitly prays in his sufferings. The Father answers by raising him: The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone; this is the Lord's doing, and it is marvelous in our eyes (Ps 118:22-23; Matt 21:42). The resurrection is God refusing to be silent about his Son.
109.2: shadow For wicked and deceitful mouths are opened against me, speaking against me with lying tongues. Wicked and deceitful mouths — the false witnesses at Jesus's trial: the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus that they might put him to death, but they found none, though many false witnesses came forward (Matt 26:59-60). The lying tongues of v2 opened against innocent David are opened against innocent Christ in the Sanhedrin's midnight court.
109.3: shadow They encircle me with words of hate, and attack me without cause. Attacked without cause — John 15:25: they hated me without a cause. The encirclement with hate is the arrest in Gethsemane, the ring of soldiers, the mob crying crucify him! The causeless attack on the innocent becomes the ground of his atoning work: he who was hated without cause bore the hatred we deserved.
109.4: shadow In return for my love they accuse me, but I give myself to prayer. Love repaid with accusation, responded to with prayer — Christ at the cross: Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do (Luke 23:34). Love given and rejected; prayer offered for the accusers. Heb 7:25: he always lives to make intercession for them. Even now, Christ gives himself to prayer — interceding for those who once accused him.
109.5: shadow So they reward me evil for good, and hatred for my love. Evil for good and hatred for love — the moral inversion of the crucifixion. Christ gives every good thing and receives in return betrayal, accusation, death. He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him (John 1:11). The reward of hatred for love becomes the occasion for the greatest act of love: giving his life for those who hated him (Rom 5:10).
109.6: shadow Appoint a wicked man against him; let an accuser stand at his right hand. Accuser at the right hand — the courtroom imagery reversed in Christ. The accuser (Satan = the accuser; Rev 12:10) who stands against humanity is defeated at the cross. Christ stands as our advocate: if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous (1 John 2:1). He has taken the accusation so that the accuser has nothing left to say against those in him (Rom 8:33).
109.7: shadow When he is tried, let him come forth guilty; let his prayer be counted as sin! Prayer counted as sin — the grotesque judgment the court rendered on Christ: his claims were treated as blasphemy deserving death (Matt 26:65). The innocent one was found guilty. But God reversed the verdict: the resurrection is the Father's judgment that Christ is righteous (1 Tim 3:16: vindicated by the Spirit).
109.8: direct May his days be few; may another take his office! Acts 1:20: Peter explicitly quotes this verse when explaining the need to replace Judas: His office let another take. The betrayer of the innocent — Judas, who handed over the suffering servant — fulfills the imprecation of v8. The selection of Matthias (Acts 1:26) is the direct fulfillment of "let another take his office" — the apostolic vacancy created by betrayal is filled by divine appointment.
109.9: shadow May his children be fatherless and his wife a widow! The consequences falling on the betrayer's household — historically fulfilled in Judas's death (Acts 1:18) and the desolation of his household. Judas's judgment is not a contradiction of the gospel but its confirmation: the one who hands over the holy Son of God receives the death he facilitated.
109.10: shadow May his children wander about and beg, seeking food far from the ruins they inhabit! Children wandering and begging — the consequences of betrayal. The contrast is with Christ's promise: I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you (John 14:18). Those who betray Christ are left fatherless; those who follow him receive the Spirit of adoption crying Abba, Father! (Rom 8:15).
109.11: shadow May the creditor seize all that he has; may strangers plunder the fruits of his toil! Creditors seizing everything — Judas received thirty pieces of silver (Matt 26:15), threw them into the temple (Matt 27:5), and the money was used to buy the potter's field (Matt 27:7). The wages of betrayal became property of strangers — providential irony: the betrayer's payment purchased the field of his burial.
109.12: shadow Let there be none to extend kindness to him, nor anyone to pity his fatherless children! No kindness extended — the stark end of one who showed no mercy in betraying the innocent. The contrast with Christ: even on the cross he shows kindness to the thief beside him (today you will be with me in paradise, Luke 23:43) and provides for his mother (John 19:26-27). The absence of mercy toward the betrayer makes Christ's mercy shine more brightly.
109.13: shadow May his posterity be cut off; may his name be blotted out in the second generation! Name blotted out — the anti-Deuteronomic judgment: rather than a name remembered for generations (Prov 10:7), the betrayer's name is cut off. Rev 3:5: Christ promises to those who overcome that I will never blot his name out of the book of life. Those faithful to Christ have their names written; those who betray him face erasure.
109.14: shadow May the iniquity of his fathers be remembered before the LORD, and let not the sin of his mother be blotted out. Ancestral iniquity remembered — the gravity of covenant betrayal reaching back through generations. The cross settles all such ancestral accounts for those who are in Christ: canceling the record of debt that stood against us with its legal demands. This he set aside, nailing it to the cross (Col 2:13-14). The betrayer's debt is left uncanceled; the believer's is nailed to the cross.
109.15: shadow Let them be before the LORD continually, that he may cut off the memory of them from the earth! Memory cut off — the permanent erasure of the unfaithful. In contrast, Christ is the one whose memory is perpetually proclaimed: do this in remembrance of me (1 Cor 11:24-25). The church's Eucharistic remembrance keeps alive the name and work of the one the betrayer tried to silence.
109.16: shadow For he did not remember to show kindness, but pursued the poor and needy and the brokenhearted, to put them to death. Not showing kindness — the indictment of those who oppose the poor and brokenhearted. Christ identifies himself with them: the Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to bind up the brokenhearted (Luke 4:18; Isa 61:1). To pursue the poor and brokenhearted is to pursue Christ (Matt 25:45).
109.17: shadow He loved to curse; let curses come upon him! He did not delight in blessing; may it be far from him! The one who loved cursing receiving curses — Gal 6:7: God is not mocked, for whatever one sows, that will he also reap. But Christ voluntarily absorbed the curse that falls on the guilty: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (Gal 3:13). The curse earned by treachery was borne by the innocent — so that those in him receive blessing instead.
109.18: shadow He clothed himself with cursing as his coat; may it soak into his body like water, like oil into his bones! Cursing soaking in like oil — the perversion of the priestly anointing (Ps 133:2). Rather than sacred oil of blessing, it is the oil of self-inflicted curse. The contrast with Christ the Anointed (Messiah) who became a curse to break the curse (Gal 3:13) — the true anointing brings blessing where treachery brought cursing.
109.19: shadow May it be like a garment that he wraps around him, like a belt that he puts on every day! Cursing worn daily like a garment — the inescapable consequence of betraying the innocent. The garment imagery recalls Christ's garments divided at the cross (John 19:23-24), and the robe of righteousness he gives to those who follow him (Rev 19:8). The betrayer wraps himself in cursing; the believer is clothed with Christ (Gal 3:27).
109.20: shadow May this be the reward of my accusers from the LORD, of those who speak evil against my life! The reward of accusers — the eschatological judgment. Rev 20:12-15: the books are opened; those whose names are not in the book of life face the lake of fire. But Christ prays for his accusers (Luke 23:34) — his intercession delays and in many cases transforms the reward, converting accusers into worshipers.
109.21: shadow But you, O GOD my Lord, deal on my behalf for your name's sake; because your steadfast love is good, deliver me! Calling on God's name and steadfast love for deliverance — the prayer Christ prays in Gethsemane: Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done (Luke 22:42). The appeal to God's steadfast love is the appeal of the innocent sufferer who trusts the Father through the darkest hour.
109.22: shadow For I am poor and needy, and my heart is stricken within me. Poor, needy, heart stricken — Christ's Gethsemane condition: my soul is very sorrowful, even to death (Matt 26:38). He who was rich became poor (2 Cor 8:9); he who was Lord of all entered the depths of human need and anguish. He entered this poverty voluntarily so that through his poverty we might become rich (2 Cor 8:9).
109.23: shadow I am gone like a shadow at evening; I am shaken off like a locust. Like a fading shadow — the mortal vulnerability of the suffering one. Christ's mortality was real: he was genuinely subject to death (Heb 2:14 — he himself likewise partook of flesh and blood... that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death). He submitted to the evening shadow of mortality so that death could not hold him and those in him escape its power.
109.24: shadow My knees are weak through fasting; my body has become gaunt, with no fat. Weakness through fasting — Christ's forty-day fast in the wilderness (Matt 4:2: afterward he was hungry). He entered genuine physical weakness — not docetic strength disguised as weakness but actual bodily diminishment. In that weakened state he refused to turn stones to bread, trusting God's provision over physical need (Matt 4:4).
109.25: direct I am an object of scorn to my accusers; when they see me, they wag their heads. Matt 27:39 fulfills this directly: those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads and saying, You who would destroy the temple and rebuild it in three days, save yourself! The very gesture of contempt — the head-wag of the scornful — is specifically enacted at the crucifixion, marking the scene as fulfillment of the suffering servant's experience in Ps 109.
109.26: shadow Help me, O LORD my God! Save me according to your steadfast love! The cry for salvation by steadfast love — answered in the resurrection. He was heard because of his reverence (Heb 5:7). The Father's steadfast love answers Christ's prayer by raising him from the dead; the resurrection is God's yes to the prayer of his Son. Those who cry the same prayer are heard in Christ's name.
109.27: shadow Let them know that this is your hand; you, O LORD, have done it! God's hand doing it — the disciples recognizing the resurrection as God's act. Acts 2:24: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death; Acts 2:36: let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ, this Jesus whom you crucified. The knowledge that this is God's hand is the content of the Pentecost proclamation.
109.28: shadow They may curse, but you will bless; when they arise, they will be put to shame, but your servant will be glad! They curse, God blesses — the resurrection reversal. The accusers of the cross thought they had ended the story with a curse; God blessed by raising the crucified one. He is not here; he has risen (Matt 28:6). The servants of the crucified Lord are glad because the one they serve is vindicated — and their gladness is permanent: no one will take your joy from you (John 16:22).
109.29: shadow May my accusers be clothed with dishonor; may they be wrapped in their own shame as in a cloak! Accusers clothed with dishonor — the eschatological reversal of the cross. The soldiers clothed Jesus in a purple robe of mockery (John 19:2-3). At his return, the mocked becomes the glorified: behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him, even those who pierced him (Rev 1:7). The mockers face the one they clothed in shame.
109.30: shadow With my mouth I will give great thanks to the LORD; I will praise him in the midst of the throng. Praising in the midst of the throng — Heb 2:12 applies Ps 22:22 to Christ: in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise. The risen Christ praises the Father in the midst of the church. The thanksgiving of v30 is Christ's post-resurrection praise in the assembly of his disciples, in which all believers are caught up.
109.31: shadow For he stands at the right hand of the needy one, to save him from those who condemn his soul to death. Standing at the right hand of the needy — the intercession of Christ. Rom 8:34: Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us. The one who was himself condemned now stands as advocate for all who are condemned. The needy of Ps 109 find Christ at their right hand.

120.1: A type: the righteous sufferer crying to God while hemmed in by hostile forces. The pattern reaches its apex in Christ — Gethsemane ("My soul is very sorrowful, even to death", Mark 14:34) and the cross (Matt 27:46 = Ps 22:1). The cry that God hears in distress is the same cry Christ made as the embodiment of Israel in extremity, and God answered it through the resurrection.
120.2: A theme: deliverance from the deceitful tongue. Christ stood surrounded by false witnesses at his trial (Matt 26:59-60; Mark 14:55-56), fulfilling the pattern of the innocent one falsely accused. He is the Truth (John 14:6) opposed by the father of lies (John 8:44); his vindication by the resurrection is the definitive answer to the lying lips that sought his death.
120.3: A revelation of God: the God of just recompense whose punishment matches the crime. Christ will execute this final justice at his return — 2 Thess 2:8 ("slain by the breath of his mouth"); Rev 19:11-15. The God who hates the lying tongue is the God who entrusted all judgment to the Son (John 5:22).
120.4: A revelation of God: warrior arrows and burning coals as instruments of divine judgment on the deceiver. This belongs to the eschatological justice Christ will execute — the same Christ who warned that every careless word will be judged (Matt 12:36) and who is described in Rev 19:15 as striking the nations with a sharp sword from his mouth.
120.5: A theme: the exile condition — dwelling among those hostile to God. Christ was himself an alien in a world that rejected him: John 1:11 ("he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him"). The pilgrim psalm sung toward Jerusalem is the prayer of one who longs for the true homeland, which Heb 11:16 identifies as the heavenly country that God has prepared.
120.6: A theme: "too long have I had my dwelling among those who hate peace." Christ dwelt his entire ministry among those who would ultimately reject and crucify him. Luke 19:42 — he wept over Jerusalem because it "did not know the things that make for peace." His presence was the peace they refused.
120.7: A direct: "I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war." The Prince of Peace (Isa 9:6) came preaching peace (Luke 19:38; Eph 2:17 — "he came and preached peace to you who were far off") and was met with hostility. Eph 2:14 — "he himself is our peace, who has made the two groups one." The verse prefigures the reception of the Messiah whose peace proclamation was answered with crucifixion.

121.1: A revelation of God: the question "from where does my help come?" is the question the psalm immediately answers — from YHWH the creator. In the NT, the creator-helper has entered his creation: John 1:3,14 — "all things were made through him... the Word became flesh." Christ is the help that comes "from the LORD" in the most literal sense — the divine helper incarnate.
121.2: A revelation of God: "My help comes from the LORD, who made heaven and earth." Col 1:16-17 identifies Christ as the one through whom all things in heaven and earth were created and in whom all things hold together. The maker of heaven and earth who is the help of Israel is the Christ through whom that creation was made. Heb 2:18 — because he himself suffered, "he is able to help those who are being tempted."
121.3: A shadow: the keeper who prevents the foot from slipping. John 10:28-29 — "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand." Christ is the keeper who holds his people so securely that their feet cannot ultimately be moved. 1 Pet 1:5 — believers "are guarded through faith for a salvation ready to be revealed."
121.4: A shadow: "He who keeps Israel will neither slumber nor sleep." The sleepless guardian finds its NT fulfillment in Christ, the permanent intercessor. Heb 7:25 — "he always lives to make intercession for them." Never off duty, never asleep — Christ prays for his people continuously and permanently from his place at the Father's right hand (Rom 8:34).
121.5: A theme: "the LORD is your shade on your right hand." The imagery of protective shade points toward Christ as the one in whom believers find shelter. John 10:9 — "I am the door; whoever enters by me will be saved and will go in and out and find pasture." Matt 23:37 — Christ longed to gather Jerusalem "as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings" — the shade and cover of his own person.
121.6: A theme: protection from the elements — sun and moon. The total, comprehensive protection described is the protection believers have in Christ. Rom 8:38-39 — nothing in all creation shall separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus. Rev 7:16 — those before the throne "shall hunger no more, neither thirst anymore; the sun shall not strike them, nor any scorching heat."
121.7: A shadow: "He will keep your going out and your coming in." The Good Shepherd who "goes before" his sheep (John 10:3-4), who calls them by name and leads them — Christ is the guardian of every movement of his people. 1 Pet 2:25 — "you were straying like sheep, but have now returned to the Shepherd and Overseer of your souls." The keeping is eternal: "from this time forth and forevermore."
121.8: A shadow: everlasting keeping — "from this time forth and forevermore." John 11:25-26 — "I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live, and everyone who lives and believes in me shall never die." The eternal dimension of the keeper's protection is realized in Christ's resurrection — the keeper who conquered death keeps his own for eternity.

122.1: A theme: the joy of pilgrimage to God's house is fulfilled and surpassed in the approach to God through Christ. Heb 12:22 — "you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem." Eph 2:18 — "through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father." The gladness of going to the LORD's house is now the gladness of those who come to God through Christ.
122.2: A theme: standing within Jerusalem's gates. John 10:9 — "I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved." The gates of the earthly Jerusalem give way to the gates of the heavenly city that are never shut (Rev 21:25). The pilgrim who stood within Jerusalem's gates now approaches the new Jerusalem through Christ, the only gate.
122.3: A theme: the city "bound firmly together" — the unity of the gathered people. John 11:52 — Christ died "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad." The firmly bound city is a picture of the church that Christ builds (Matt 16:18) — a community held together not by geography but by union with him.
122.4: A theme: "to give thanks to the name of the LORD." The name before which all nations bow is the name of Jesus — Phil 2:10-11 ("at the name of Jesus every knee should bow... and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord"). The universal thanksgiving the psalm envisions is the thanksgiving that flows from every nation through Christ.
122.5: A shadow: "Thrones for judgment were set, the thrones of the house of David." The Davidic judgment throne is fulfilled in Christ the king-judge. Acts 2:30 — God swore to set "one of his descendants on his throne"; Rev 3:7 — Christ holds "the key of David." He is both the ruler on David's throne and the appointed judge of all (Acts 17:31).
122.6: A theme: "pray for the peace of Jerusalem." Christ wept over Jerusalem because it did not recognize "the things that make for peace" in its time of visitation (Luke 19:41-44). He is the answer to the prayer — Eph 2:14 "he himself is our peace," the one who reconciles both Jew and Gentile to God and to each other.
122.7: A theme: "peace be within your walls and security within your towers." The security that eludes earthly Jerusalem is the security of the heavenly city. Heb 11:10 — Abraham "was looking forward to the city that has foundations, whose designer and builder is God." The walls that never fall are the walls of the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:12-19).
122.8: A direct: "Peace be within you!" — Christ's first words to his gathered disciples after the resurrection. John 20:19,21,26 — "Peace be with you." The peace that the pilgrim prays for Jerusalem's sake, Christ pronounces over his gathered community. The resurrection greeting is the definitive answer to the pilgrimage prayer.
122.9: A type: "for the sake of the house of the LORD our God, I will seek your good." Zeal for God's house drove Christ to cleanse the temple — John 2:17 ("Zeal for your house will consume me" = Ps 69:9). His zeal climaxed in the cross, where he gave himself as the final offering, making himself both the temple and the sacrifice (John 2:19-21).

123.1: A shadow: "To you I lift up my eyes, O you who are enthroned in the heavens." The one enthroned in heaven is now identified as Christ, the ascended Lord — Heb 1:3 ("sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high"); Rev 1:12-18 (the risen Christ enthroned in glory). Eyes lifted to heaven for mercy are eyes lifted to the exalted Christ who intercedes (Rom 8:34).
123.2: A theme: the servant's eyes fixed on the master, waiting for direction. Christ modeled this perfect attentiveness toward the Father throughout his ministry — John 5:19 ("the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing"). He is both the pattern of dependence and the mediator through whom the mercy the servants seek is obtained (Heb 4:16 — "approach the throne of grace with confidence").
123.3: A shadow: "Have mercy upon us, for we have had more than enough of contempt." Christ bore the contempt of the world so that God's mercy could flow to those who cry for it. Heb 12:2 — he "endured the cross, despising the shame." He is the propitiation that makes this plea answerable (1 John 2:2); the mercy we ask for is the mercy he purchased.
123.4: A type: the scorn of the proud and the ease of the contemptuous directed at the lowly. This is the reception Christ received at the cross — Matt 27:39-44 (those who passed by derided him; the chief priests mocked him; the robbers reviled him). The righteous sufferer scorned by the proud is the type of which Christ's passion is the antitype. He bore our shame (Heb 12:2) so we could receive honor in him.

124.1: A theme: the conditional "if it had not been the LORD who was on our side" is the premise of the entire gospel. Rom 8:31 — "If God is for us, who can be against us?" The rhetorical question that Paul raises is the confident answer to the psalm's conditional — in Christ, the "if" has been resolved: God is for us, definitively and irreversibly, having given his own Son (Rom 8:32).
124.2: A theme: YHWH on Israel's side when enemies rose against them — the pattern of divine solidarity that reaches its ultimate expression in the incarnation. God became flesh and stood with his people not just militarily but in death itself (Phil 2:8 — "he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross").
124.3: A type: the mortal threat of being swallowed alive. Christ descended into the depths of death — Eph 4:9 ("he also descended into the lower, earthly regions") — and was "swallowed" by the grave. The threat Israel faced became the reality Christ entered, precisely so that the threat would be defeated. Acts 2:24 — "it was not possible for him to be held by death."
124.4: A type: the flood sweeping over, the torrent passing over. Christ was baptized into death itself (Mark 10:38-39 — his "baptism" he would undergo). The waters of judgment that threatened to overwhelm Israel were the waters that Christ descended into — fulfilling the pattern of Noah and Jonah (Matt 12:40) — and the waters through which he brought his people safely to the other side (1 Pet 3:20-21).
124.5: A type: the raging waters. The Exodus pattern — escape through the sea — is the pattern Christ recapitulates. 1 Cor 10:1-2 — Israel was "baptized into Moses" in the cloud and sea; Christ is the greater Moses whose people are baptized into him (Rom 6:3-4), passing through death into resurrection life.
124.6: A shadow: "Blessed be the LORD, who has not given us as prey to their teeth!" Christ is the champion who defeats the devourer. 1 Pet 5:8 — Satan "prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour." Heb 2:14-15 — Christ through death "destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery." The prey is not given over because Christ stood in the gap.
124.7: A theme: "we have escaped like a bird from the snare of the fowler; the snare is broken." Luke 4:18 — Christ came to "proclaim liberty to the captives." Gal 5:1 — "For freedom Christ has set us free." The snare of sin and the law's condemnation is broken at the cross (Col 2:14-15 — "having nailed it to the cross"). The bird that escapes is the believer freed in Christ.
124.8: A direct: "Our help is in the name of the LORD, who made heaven and earth." Acts 4:12 — "there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved." The "name of the LORD" that is the help of his people is now known as the name of Jesus, the Lord and creator. Peter quotes this principle immediately after healing in Jesus's name (Acts 3:6,16).

125.1: A shadow: "Those who trust in the LORD are like Mount Zion, which cannot be moved, but abides forever." Matt 7:24-25 — the one who builds on Christ's words stands like a house on rock when the floods come. 1 Cor 3:11 — "no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ." The immovability comes from the foundation, not the builder.
125.2: A shadow: "As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds his people." John 10:28-29 — "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all, and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father's hand." Surrounded by Christ on one side and the Father on the other — a double enclosure that cannot be broken.
125.3: A shadow: "the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the land allotted to the righteous." 1 John 3:8 — "The reason the Son of God appeared was to destroy the works of the devil." The scepter of wickedness that would rest on God's people is the power Christ came to overthrow — Col 2:15 ("having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross").
125.4: A revelation of God: "Do good, O LORD, to those who are good and to those who are upright in their hearts." The justice of God in rewarding the righteous finds its ultimate expression in Christ's imputed righteousness — the "good" ones are those clothed in Christ's righteousness (2 Cor 5:21). Christ the judge will say to his sheep, "Come, you who are blessed by my Father" (Matt 25:34).
125.5: A revelation of God: "those who turn aside to their crooked ways the LORD will lead away with evildoers." Matt 25:41 — "Depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire." Christ executes the final separation that the psalm anticipates. The psalm's "Peace be upon Israel!" is the peace that Christ pronounces over those who are his own (John 20:19-21; Eph 2:17).

126.1: A type: "When the LORD restored the fortunes of Zion, we were like those who dream." The restoration that surpasses imagination — the disciples' reaction to the resurrection was exactly this: Luke 24:41 — "they still disbelieved for joy and were marveling." The definitive restoration is Christ's resurrection, the firstfruits of the full restoration of God's people (1 Cor 15:20-23).
126.2: A type: "our mouth was filled with laughter, and our tongue with shouts of joy." The joy of the nations at Israel's restoration anticipates the eschatological joy of all peoples at the gospel — Acts 11:18 (Gentiles "glorified God, saying, 'Then to the Gentiles also God has granted repentance that leads to life'"); Luke 15:10 ("there is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents").
126.3: A revelation of God: "The LORD has done great things for us; we are glad." The greatest act of God is the resurrection of Christ from the dead (Eph 1:19-20 — "the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, which he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead"). All God's "great things" find their culmination in the Christ-event.
126.4: A shadow: "Restore our fortunes, O LORD, like streams in the Negeb!" The sudden, abundant streams in the dry desert prefigure the outpouring of the Spirit — John 7:37-38 ("rivers of living water" from those who believe in Christ). The eschatological ingathering: John 11:52 — Christ died "to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad"; Matt 8:11 — many will come from east and west to the kingdom.
126.5: A type: "Those who sow in tears shall reap with shouts of joy." John 12:24 — "unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit." The suffering-to-glory pattern — exile-return, sowing-reaping — is the pattern Christ enacts in his death and resurrection. His tears (John 11:35; Luke 19:41) become the resurrection joy (John 20:20).
126.6: A type: "He who goes out weeping, bearing the seed for sowing, shall come home with shouts of joy, bringing his sheaves with him." The portrait of the sower who weeps and returns with harvest is the portrait of Christ who "went out" to the cross bearing the seed of his own death, and who "came home" in the resurrection with the harvest of redeemed humanity. Heb 12:2 — "who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross."

127.1: A shadow: "Unless the LORD builds the house, those who build it labor in vain." Matt 16:18 — "I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it." 1 Cor 3:10-11 — "I laid a foundation, and someone else is building upon it. Let each one take care how he builds upon it. For no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ." All human effort to build what God must build is vanity; Christ is both the foundation and the builder.
127.2: A theme: "he gives sleep to his beloved." Matt 11:28-30 — "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest." The anxious toil of self-sufficiency (rising early, staying up late) stands against the rest of those who trust the builder. Christ himself demonstrated this rest — sleeping undisturbed in the storm (Mark 4:38), the one who gives rest embodying it perfectly.
127.3: A theme: "children are a heritage from the LORD, the fruit of the womb a reward." Those born through Christ are his heritage and offspring. John 1:12-13 — those who receive Christ are born "not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God." Isa 53:10 — "he shall see his offspring and prolong his days." The spiritual children born through Christ's work are his reward and inheritance.
127.4: A theme: "Like arrows in the hand of a warrior are the children of one's youth." The expansion of Christ's family through the proclamation of the gospel — disciples sent as arrows into all nations (Matt 28:19-20; John 12:32 — "I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself"). The quiver-filling is the gathering of the elect from every nation (Rev 7:9).
127.5: A theme: "Blessed is the man who fills his quiver with them! He shall not be put to shame when he speaks with his enemies in the gate." Christ speaks before all his enemies with final authority — Phil 2:10-11 (every knee bows at his name). The one who builds the house, provides for the household, and fills his quiver is the victor who faces no shame. Heb 2:9 — Christ is "crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death."

128.1: A revelation of God: "Blessed is everyone who fears the LORD, who walks in his ways." Prov 1:7 — the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom. Christ is the wisdom of God incarnate (1 Cor 1:30 — "Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God"). He perfectly "walks in God's ways" (John 8:29 — "I always do the things that are pleasing to him") and shares the blessedness of the God-fearer with all who are in him.
128.2: A theme: "You shall eat the fruit of the labor of your hands; you shall be blessed, and it shall be well with you." The fruitfulness belongs to those who abide in Christ. John 15:5 — "Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing." The laborer who eats the fruit of fruitful work is the believer whose labor in Christ is never in vain (1 Cor 15:58).
128.3: A theme: "Your wife will be like a fruitful vine within your house; your children will be like olive shoots around your table." The covenantal household bears fruit through Christ. Rev 21:2,9 — the church is the bride of Christ; the household of God (Eph 2:19) gathers around the table of the Lord (1 Cor 11:23-26). The fruitfulness of the vine is realized in Christ the true vine (John 15:1-8).
128.4: A revelation of God: the principle restated — "thus shall the man be blessed who fears the LORD." The blessing flows from right relationship with God, which is now mediated through Christ. Acts 3:25-26 — God raised up his servant and "sent him to you first, to bless you by turning every one of you from your wickedness." The ancient blessing given to Abraham comes to all peoples through Christ.
128.5: A shadow: "The LORD bless you from Zion! May you see the prosperity of Jerusalem all the days of your life!" The blessing that flows from Zion flows from the heavenly Jerusalem. Heb 12:22-24 — "you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem... to Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant." The prosperity of Jerusalem is the shalom of God's eternal city, centered on Christ.
128.6: A theme: "May you see your children's children! Peace be upon Israel!" The multigenerational vision is the vision of the church that Christ builds across all ages and nations. Matt 16:18 — "I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it." The "peace upon Israel" is the peace Christ himself is (Eph 2:14), declared over a community that includes every nation (Gal 3:29).

129.1: A type: "Greatly have they afflicted me from my youth." The afflicted righteous one who suffers throughout life but is not ultimately overcome. Christ was opposed from birth (Matt 2:13-16, Herod's slaughter) through his entire ministry to the cross. Yet the affliction did not defeat him — Acts 2:24 ("it was not possible for him to be held by death"). John 16:33 — "I have overcome the world."
129.2: A type: "Greatly have they afflicted me from my youth, yet they have not prevailed against me." The repeated emphasis — afflicted, yet not defeated — is the pattern of Christ's suffering and vindication. The "yet" is the resurrection. All that the powers of darkness did to him did not prevail (Col 2:15 — "he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross").
129.3: A type: "The plowers plowed upon my back; they made long their furrows." The scourging of Christ. John 19:1 — Pilate had Jesus flogged. Isa 50:6 — "I gave my back to those who strike." Isa 53:5 — "by his wounds we are healed." The "long furrows" on the back of the sufferer are the stripes of the passion through which Christ bore our griefs and carried our sorrows.
129.4: A shadow: "The LORD is righteous; he has cut the cords of the wicked." Christ's death cuts the cords that bound humanity to condemnation and slavery. Col 2:14-15 — "having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness, which stood against us and condemned us; he has taken it away, nailing it to the cross." The cords of the wicked are the debt of sin, cut at Calvary.
129.5: A revelation of God: "May all who hate Zion be put to shame and turned backward!" God's enemies will be made "a footstool for your feet" — Ps 110:1 (quoted in Heb 1:13). 1 Cor 15:25 — "he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet." The enemies of Zion who have been put to shame is the accomplished work of the cross and the completing work of Christ's reign.
129.6: A revelation of God: the wicked are "like the grass on the housetops, which withers before it grows up." Christ uses similar imagery of the fruitless and the fruitful — John 15:6 ("If anyone does not abide in me he is thrown away like a branch and withers"); Matt 13:5-6 (the seed without root springs up but withers). Those who oppose the Lord are like unrooted grass — impressive for a moment, gone entirely.
129.7: A revelation of God: the grass that "does not fill the hand of the reaper or the arms of him who binds sheaves." The harvest imagery contrasts the fruitless wicked with the fruitful righteous — the full harvest that Christ brings in (Matt 13:30,39-43: the final harvest; John 4:35 — "the fields are white for harvest"). What the wicked cannot produce, Christ produces abundantly.
129.8: A revelation of God: "The blessing of the LORD not be on them!" — those who pass by do not say "the blessing of the LORD be upon you." The final state of those who reject God is exclusion from his blessing. The inverse is the great blessing Christ pronounces over his own (Matt 5:3-12 — the Beatitudes). The psalm's negative and positive are both realized in the pronouncements of Christ the judge (Matt 25:34,41).

130.1: A type: "Out of the depths I cry to you, O LORD!" The cry from the maʿamāqqîm (depths, deep waters) is the cry of Israel in extremity — and the cry of Christ from the cross. Matt 27:46 = Ps 22:1 — "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Christ entered the deepest depths — Eph 4:9 ("he also descended into the lower, earthly regions") — so that no depth of human suffering falls outside his solidarity and redemptive reach.
130.2: A type: "O Lord, hear my voice! Let your ears be attentive to the cry of my pleas for mercy!" The intercessory cry that Scripture records as answered — God heard. This is the pattern of Christ's own prayer in Gethsemane and at the cross: Heb 5:7 — "he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverence." The Father heard and raised him.
130.3: A direct: "If you, O LORD, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand?" The rhetorical answer is "no one" — Rom 3:23, "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." The gospel's answer is the imputed righteousness of Christ — 2 Cor 5:21 ("For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God"). We "stand" only because Christ stood condemned in our place.
130.4: A direct: "But with you there is forgiveness, that you may be feared." Forgiveness is Christ's gift alone. Acts 13:38 — "through this man forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you." Col 1:14 — "in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins." The forgiveness that produces reverence (not presumption) is the forgiveness purchased at the cross — costly enough to be truly feared, free enough to be truly received.
130.5: A shadow: "I wait for the LORD, my soul waits, and in his word I hope." The intense waiting is Israel's messianic posture and now the church's eschatological posture. 1 Thess 1:9-10 — the Thessalonians "turned to God... and to wait for his Son from heaven." Rev 22:20 — "Come, Lord Jesus!" The "word" in which the hope resides is ultimately the Word made flesh (John 1:14) and the word of promise that he will return.
130.6: A shadow: "my soul waits for the Lord more than watchmen wait for the morning." The watchman straining for dawn is the image of eschatological yearning. Rom 8:23 — "we ourselves, who have the firstfruits of the Spirit, groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies." The morning that ends the watch is the appearing of Christ (2 Pet 1:19 — "the morning star rises in your hearts").
130.7: A shadow: "For with the LORD there is steadfast love, and with him is plentiful redemption." Plentiful redemption — harbēh-ʿimmô p̄edût — is the sufficiency and overflow of the atonement. 1 Tim 2:6 — Christ "gave himself as a ransom for all." Eph 1:7 — "In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins, in accordance with the riches of God's grace that he lavished on us." The plentiful redemption is the cross.
130.8: A direct: "And he will redeem Israel from all his iniquities." Matt 1:21 — "you shall call his name Jesus, for he will save his people from their sins." The angel's announcement is the direct fulfillment of this verse. Titus 2:14 — Christ "gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness." The God who promises in Ps 130:8 to redeem Israel from all iniquity sends his Son in Matt 1:21 to accomplish it. The verse and its fulfillment are inseparable.

131.1: A type: "My heart is not lifted up; my eyes are not raised too high." David's declaration of humility is the posture Christ embodied perfectly. Phil 2:5-8 — Christ "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant." Matt 11:29 — "I am gentle and lowly in heart." The Son of God, who had every reason to lift his heart high, descended in humility — the type becomes the antitype without remainder.
131.2: A theme: "like a weaned child with its mother; like a weaned child is my soul within me." The weaned child (content in the mother's presence, no longer crying for milk) models the trust Christ calls his disciples to: Matt 18:3 — "unless you turn and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven." Matt 11:28-30 — his yoke is easy; in him the soul finds rest without striving. The quiet soul is the soul resting in Christ.
131.3: A shadow: "O Israel, hope in the LORD from this time forth and forevermore." The eternal hope is "Christ in you, the hope of glory" (Col 1:27). Acts 28:20 — Paul was bound in chains because of "the hope of Israel" — which is Christ himself. 1 Pet 1:3 — "a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead." The hope that spans "from this time forth and forevermore" is anchored in the resurrection and consummated at Christ's return.

132.1: A type: "Remember, O LORD, in David's favor, all the hardships he endured." David's hardships in seeking a dwelling for God are a type of Christ's mission to establish the true temple. John 2:19-21 — "destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up" (spoken of his body). David sought a house for God's ark; in Christ, God himself becomes the dwelling among his people permanently (Rev 21:3,22).
132.2: A type: "how he swore to the LORD and vowed to the Mighty One of Jacob." David's sworn oath to find a place for the LORD reflects the covenant commitment that Christ fulfills. Heb 6:17-18 — "God desired to show more convincingly to the heirs of the promise the unchangeable character of his purpose... so that we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to hold fast to the hope set before us." God's own oath is the guarantee of the messianic promise.
132.3: A type: "I will not enter my house or get into my bed, I will not give sleep to my eyes or slumber to my eyelids." David's restless urgency for God's dwelling is a type of Christ's zeal. John 2:17 — "Zeal for your house will consume me" (applied to Christ at the temple cleansing). The urgency David felt about establishing a place for God's presence is the urgency Christ embodied in establishing the new meeting place between God and humanity.
132.4: A type: "until I find a place for the LORD, a dwelling place for the Mighty One of Jacob." The place David sought is not the stone temple but the temple Christ is. Heb 9:11-12 — Christ entered "the greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is, not of this creation." The ark found its resting place; the glory of God found its dwelling place in the incarnate Son (John 1:14 — "the Word... dwelt among us").
132.5: A type: "a dwelling place for the Mighty One of Jacob." The Mighty One who is Jacob's God — the God of covenant — finds his ultimate dwelling in Christ and in the community gathered around him. 1 Cor 3:16 — "you are God's temple and God's Spirit dwells in you." Eph 2:21-22 — the whole structure grows into "a holy temple in the Lord... a dwelling place for God by the Spirit."
132.6: A type: "We heard of it in Ephrathah; we found it in the fields of Jaar." The ark's journey from obscurity to the city of David prefigures the hidden preparation of God's purposes in Christ — born obscurely in Bethlehem (= Ephrathah, Mic 5:2; Matt 2:6), kept hidden until his time (Luke 2:52). The ark found and brought to Jerusalem is the type of the glory of God recognized and enthroned in Christ.
132.7: A type: "Let us go to his dwelling place; let us worship at his footstool!" The pilgrimage call echoes through the Psalter. Heb 12:22 — believers have "come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem." The footstool at which worship is offered is now the throne of the ascended Christ — Rev 4-5 (the worship of heaven centered on the Lamb).
132.8: A type: "Arise, O LORD, and go to your resting place, you and the ark of your might." The ark of God's might points to Christ, who is "the power of God and the wisdom of God" (1 Cor 1:24). The "resting place" for God is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection and session at the right hand of the Father — Heb 1:3 ("sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high").
132.9: A type: "Let your priests be clothed with righteousness, and let your saints shout for joy." The priests clothed in righteousness are the believers clothed in Christ's righteousness (2 Cor 5:21; Rev 19:8 — "the righteous deeds of the saints"). Rev 7:9 — the great multitude before the throne clothed in white robes shouting for joy. The priestly garment of righteousness is the robe Christ provides.
132.10: A shadow: "For the sake of your servant David, do not turn away the face of your anointed one." The māšîaḥ — the anointed one — whose face must not be turned away. Christ now intercedes from the Father's right hand: Heb 7:25 — "he always lives to make intercession for them." The prayer that God not reject the anointed is answered permanently in Christ's accepted intercession before the Father (Heb 9:24 — "now to appear in the presence of God for us").
132.11: A direct: "The LORD swore to David a sure oath from which he will not turn back: 'One of the sons of your body I will set on your throne.'" Acts 2:30 — "David was a prophet, and knowing that God had sworn with an oath to him that he would set one of his descendants on his throne, he spoke about the resurrection of the Christ." Peter directly applies this verse to Christ's resurrection. One of the most explicitly messianic verses in the entire Psalter.
132.12: A shadow: "If your sons keep my covenant and my testimonies that I shall teach them, their sons also forever shall sit on your throne." The conditional "if" of David's natural sons is resolved by Christ, the one son who kept the covenant perfectly (Heb 4:15 — "without sin"). The eternal throne depends on perfect obedience — only Christ provided it. Heb 1:8 — "Your throne, O God, is forever and ever, the scepter of uprightness is the scepter of your kingdom."
132.13: A shadow: "For the LORD has chosen Zion; he has desired it for his dwelling place." The final resting place of God is not the stone temple but the heavenly Jerusalem where God and Christ dwell with their people forever. Rev 21:3,22 — "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man... its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb." Heb 12:22 — the city that believers have come to is the dwelling place God has chosen.
132.14: A shadow: "This is my resting place forever; here I will dwell, for I have desired it." The permanent, final dwelling of God realized in Christ and the new creation. Rev 21:3 — "He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God." What the ark in Jerusalem temporarily signified, the presence of Christ permanently establishes.
132.15: A type: "I will abundantly bless her provisions; I will satisfy her poor with bread." Christ satisfies the hungry with bread. John 6:35 — "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger." The feeding of the five thousand (John 6:1-15) and four thousand (Matt 15:32-39) demonstrate the abundance of Zion's messianic provisions. Luke 1:53 — "he has filled the hungry with good things."
132.16: A type: "Her priests I will clothe with salvation, and her saints will shout for joy." Salvation as clothing — Isa 61:10 ("he has clothed me with the garments of salvation"), applied to Christ in Luke 4:18 ("he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives"). Rev 7:9 — the great multitude clothed in white robes shouting before the throne. The priestly garment is the salvation Christ provides, worn by all who are his.
132.17: A direct: "There I will make a horn grow for David; I have prepared a lamp for my anointed." Luke 1:69 — "he has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David." Luke 1:78-79 — "the sunrise from on high will visit us, to give light to those who sit in darkness." This verse is directly quoted and applied to Christ in Zechariah's Benedictus. The horn of strength and the lamp of lineage and light — both fulfilled in Jesus of Nazareth, son of David.
132.18: A shadow: "His enemies I will clothe with shame, but on him his crown will gleam." The victorious, crowned king after his enemies are defeated. Heb 2:9 — "we see him who for a little while was made lower than the angels, namely Jesus, crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death." Phil 2:9-11 — "God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name." The crown that gleams is the crown of the exalted Christ after the cross.

133.1: A direct: "Behold, how good and pleasant it is when brothers dwell in unity!" John 17:21-23 — "that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us." Christ prays for the unity the psalm describes — and he creates it through his death. Eph 2:14-16 — "he himself is our peace, who has made the two groups one and has destroyed the barrier, the dividing wall of hostility... creating in himself one new humanity." The psalm's vision is Christ's own intention.
133.2: A type: "It is like the precious oil on the head, running down on the beard, on the beard of Aaron, running down on the collar of his robes." The anointing oil flowing from Aaron's head is a type of the Spirit poured out from Christ at Pentecost. Acts 2:33 — "Being therefore exalted at the right hand of God, and having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, he has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing." Christ is the Anointed One (Māšîaḥ = Christos) from whom the anointing flows to all his people (1 John 2:20,27 — "you have been anointed by the Holy One").
133.3: A direct: "For there the LORD has commanded the blessing, life forevermore." John 17:3 — "And this is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent." Rev 22:14 — "Blessed are those who wash their robes, so that they may have the right to the tree of life." The "life forevermore" commanded as a blessing at Zion is the eternal life that Christ gives to all who are united in him. The blessing, the unity, and the life all converge in Christ.

147.1: theme Praise the LORD! For it is good to sing praises to our God; for it is pleasant, and a song of praise is fitting. Praise fitting and pleasant — the worship of the assembled church. Heb 13:15: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name. Christ himself leads this praise (Heb 2:12; Ps 22:22) — the fitting song is fitting because the Son sings it with his people.
147.2: shadow The LORD builds up Jerusalem; he gathers the outcasts of Israel. Building Jerusalem and gathering outcasts — the eschatological city. Matt 23:37: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings. Rev 21:2: the New Jerusalem coming down from heaven — the city the LORD builds in Christ as the gathering place of all his scattered people.
147.3: shadow He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds. Healing the brokenhearted — Christ's Nazareth manifesto: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to bind up the brokenhearted (Luke 4:18; Isa 61:1). The physician of Ps 147:3 is the physician who comes to heal the sick (Matt 9:12-13), entering the wreckage of human hearts to bind and restore.
147.4: revelation of God He determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of them their names. Naming all the stars — the Creator Word. John 1:3: All things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made. Col 1:16-17: by him all things were created... and in him all things hold together. The one who names every star became the son of Mary, entering the creation he himself named, calling each person also by name (John 10:3).
147.5: revelation of God Great is our Lord, and abundant in power; his understanding is beyond measure. Power and understanding beyond measure — Christ in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Col 2:3). Eph 3:18-19: the breadth and length and height and depth of the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge. The immeasurable greatness of God's power is the same power that raised Christ from the dead (Eph 1:19-20).
147.6: shadow The LORD lifts up the humble; he casts the wicked to the ground. Lifting the humble and casting the wicked — the Magnificat: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate (Luke 1:52). Matt 23:12: whoever humbles himself will be exalted. The cross is the supreme act of this reversal: the humbled Servant (Phil 2:8) is exalted above all (Phil 2:9-11).
147.7: theme Sing to the LORD with thanksgiving; make melody to our God on the lyre! Thanksgiving and melody to God — the music of the new covenant church. Col 3:16: singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, with thankfulness in your hearts to God. Rev 5:8: the four living creatures and the elders hold harps and sing to the Lamb. The lyre of Ps 147 becomes the harp of Revelation, played before the enthroned Lamb.
147.8: shadow He covers the heavens with clouds; he prepares rain for the earth; he makes grass grow on the hills. Providential care for the earth — the common grace Christ explains: he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust (Matt 5:45). The God who covers heaven with clouds and grows grass is the Father Christ reveals — whose providence extends to every sparrow and every blade of grass (Matt 6:26-30).
147.9: shadow He gives to the beasts their food, and to the young ravens that cry. Feeding beasts and ravens — the providence Christ cites: Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not of more value than they? (Matt 6:26). The God of Ps 147:9 is the Father Christ reveals; if he feeds ravens, how much more will he provide for those who are in Christ?
147.10: shadow His delight is not in the strength of the horse, nor his pleasure in the legs of a man; God not delighting in human strength — the reversal of worldly power. 1 Cor 1:26-27: not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth. But God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise. The cross is the supreme demonstration that God does not work through human strength but through the weakness of Christ (1 Cor 1:25; 2 Cor 13:4).
147.11: revelation of God but the LORD takes pleasure in those who fear him, in those who hope in his steadfast love. God's pleasure in those who fear and hope — the Father's pleasure in Christ: This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased (Matt 3:17; 17:5). Those in Christ share his relationship with the Father: the Father himself loves you, because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God (John 16:27). The pleasure of Ps 147:11 is given to all who hope in God's steadfast love through Christ.
147.12: shadow Praise the LORD, O Jerusalem! Praise your God, O Zion! Jerusalem and Zion praising God — the eschatological city. Gal 4:26: the Jerusalem above is free, and she is our mother. Heb 12:22: you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem. The praise of Zion is now the praise of the church that has come to the heavenly Zion through Christ.
147.13: shadow For he strengthens the bars of your gates; he blesses your children within you. Strengthened gates and blessed children — Matt 16:18: the gates of hell shall not prevail against it. Christ strengthens the gates of his church against every assault. The blessed children within are those born of God (John 1:12-13), the children of Abraham by faith (Gal 3:29), blessed in Christ with every spiritual blessing (Eph 1:3).
147.14: shadow He makes peace in your borders and fills you with the finest of the wheat. Peace and finest wheat — the Eucharist as the finest wheat. Christ: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger (John 6:35). John 14:27: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. The finest wheat of Ps 147:14 is fulfilled in the bread of the Lord's Supper — the one who is both the peace-maker and the bread of life.
147.15: direct He sends out his command to the earth; his word runs swiftly. God sending his word swiftly — the Incarnation as the supreme sending of the Word. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us. 2 Tim 2:9: the word of God is not bound. The word that runs swiftly to the ends of the earth is the gospel — the word made flesh in Jesus Christ, sent out through the apostles to every nation (Matt 28:19-20).
147.16: shadow He gives snow like wool; he scatters frost like ashes; Snow and frost as God's word-command — the creative word of God that controls all creation. John 1:3: All things were made through him. Christ commands creation directly — he rebukes the storm (Mark 4:39), walks on water (Matt 14:25). The one who commands snow and frost is the one who commands wind and wave to be still — the same creative Word at work.
147.17: shadow he hurls down his crystals of ice like crumbs; who can stand before his cold? Ice as God's creative power — the rhetorical question: who can stand before his cold? The implied answer is no one except the Word through whom all things were made. John 1:10: He was in the world, and the world was made through him, yet the world did not know him. The creator of ice entered his own creation as the child of Bethlehem.
147.18: shadow He sends out his word and melts them; he makes his wind blow and the waters flow. The word that melts ice and makes wind blow — the same word Christ speaks to nature. When he calms the storm (Mark 4:39 — Peace! Be still!), he is the Creator speaking to his creation. When he breathes on the disciples (John 20:22 — Receive the Holy Spirit), the wind of God flows through the risen Christ to make waters of life flow.
147.19: shadow He declares his word to Jacob, his statutes and rules to Israel. God declaring his word to Israel — the privilege that points to the crisis of the Incarnation. John 1:11: He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. The word declared to Jacob and Israel is now the Word embodied in Jesus of Nazareth, the fulfillment of every statute and rule he once declared to his covenant people.
147.20: shadow He has not dealt thus with any other nation; they do not know his rules. Praise the LORD! Israel's unique privilege now opened to all nations through Christ — Eph 2:12-13: you Gentiles in the flesh... remember that you were... having no hope and without God in the world. But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. The nations that did not know God's rules now receive them through the gospel of Christ (Rom 15:9-12).

148.1: theme Praise the LORD! Praise the LORD from the heavens; praise him in the heights! Praise from the heavens and heights — Rev 5:11-13 and 19:1-6 where the heavenly hosts and all creation erupt in praise. The chain of praise from heights to depths that Ps 148 initiates finds its focal point in the Lamb: to him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! (Rev 5:13).
148.2: shadow Praise him, all his angels; praise him, all his hosts! Angels and heavenly hosts praising — Rev 5:11-12: ten thousand times ten thousand and thousands of thousands, saying with a loud voice, Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing! The angels who praised at Christ's birth (Luke 2:13-14) now praise the Lamb in glory — the same hosts, the same object of praise.
148.3: shadow Praise him, sun and moon, praise him, all you shining stars! Sun, moon, and stars praising — the darkened sun at Christ's crucifixion (Matt 27:45: there was darkness over all the land) is the creation mourning; at his return creation is renewed and all things praise. Rev 21:23: in the new creation the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.
148.4: shadow Praise him, you highest heavens, and you waters above the heavens! The highest heavens praising — Heb 4:14: we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus, the Son of God. Christ's ascension takes him through the highest heavens; from there he pours out the Spirit (Acts 2:33) and intercedes (Heb 7:25). The heavens praise the one who passed through them and is now exalted above them all.
148.5: direct Let them praise the name of the LORD! For he commanded and they were created. He commanded and they were created — John 1:3: All things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made. Col 1:16: by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible. The command through which all things were created is the Word — who became flesh in Jesus Christ. Creation praises the name of the one who made it through the Son.
148.6: shadow And he established them forever and ever; he gave a decree, and it shall not pass away. Creation established forever — Heb 1:10-12 applies the psalm of creation to Christ: you, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning... they will perish, but you remain... you are the same, and your years will have no end. The eternal decree that holds creation is the eternal Son who holds all things together (Col 1:17).
148.7: shadow Praise the LORD from the earth, you great sea creatures and all deeps, Praise from the earth's depths — Rev 5:13: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! The sea creatures of Ps 148:7 are included in the universal praise of Rev 5 directed to the Lamb.
148.8: shadow fire and hail, snow and mist, stormy wind fulfilling his word! Storm and fire fulfilling the word — Acts 2:2-3: at Pentecost a sound like a mighty rushing wind... and divided tongues as of fire appeared — the Spirit coming as wind and fire, fulfilling Christ's promise (John 16:7). The stormy wind that fulfills God's word is the Spirit of Christ sent out to complete what was promised.
148.9: shadow Mountains and all hills, fruit trees and all cedars! Mountains, hills, and trees praising — the eschatological vision of Rom 8:22: the whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now. Creation waits with eager longing (Rom 8:19) to join the praise of Ps 148 — the praise it was created for but cannot yet fully sing. At Christ's return this groaning becomes the universal hallelujah.
148.10: shadow Beasts and all livestock, creeping things and flying birds! All creatures praising — the comprehensive vision of creation's worship. Rev 5:13 includes every creature in the praise of the Lamb. Christ's reign as the second Adam (1 Cor 15:45) restores the dominion over the creatures that the first Adam lost; under Christ's lordship, the creatures fulfill their created purpose of praise.
148.11: shadow Kings of the earth and all peoples, princes and all rulers of the earth! Kings and all peoples praising — the vision of Rev 21:24-26: the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it (the New Jerusalem). Phil 2:10-11: every knee should bow... and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father. The universal lordship that Ps 148:11 anticipates is fulfilled in the exaltation of Christ.
148.12: shadow Young men and maidens together, old men and children! All ages praising — the church that spans every generation: Acts 2:17: your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, and your young men shall see visions, and your old men shall dream dreams. Joel's promise poured out at Pentecost; the full generational spectrum of Ps 148:12 finds its fulfillment in the Spirit-filled church gathered around Christ.
148.13: direct Let them praise the name of the LORD, for his name alone is exalted; his majesty is above earth and heaven. His name alone exalted above all — Phil 2:9-11: God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth. The name exalted above earth and heaven in Ps 148:13 is the name of Jesus Christ, given by the Father to the crucified and risen Son.
148.14: shadow He has raised up a horn for his people, praise for all his saints, for the people of Israel who are near to him. Praise the LORD! Horn raised up for his people — Luke 1:68-69: Zechariah's prophecy: Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people and has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David. The horn of Ps 148:14 is the Christ-child born to Mary — the raised horn of the LORD's people who draws near all who were far off.

149.1: direct Praise the LORD! Sing to the LORD a new song, his praise in the assembly of the godly! A new song in the assembly — Rev 5:9: they sang a new song, saying, Worthy are you to take the scroll and to open its seals, for you were slain, and by your blood you ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation. The new song of Ps 149 is the song of the Lamb; the assembly of the godly is the congregation gathered around the risen Christ.
149.2: shadow Let Israel be glad in his Maker; let the children of Zion rejoice in their King! Rejoicing in the King — Rev 19:7: Let us rejoice and exult and give him the glory, for the marriage of the Lamb has come. Phil 4:4: Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice. The King in whom Zion rejoices is the messianic King, Christ enthroned at the Father's right hand (Ps 110:1; Acts 2:34-36).
149.3: shadow Let them praise his name with dancing, making melody to him with tambourine and lyre! Praise with dancing and music — the full-bodied worship that Christ restores. Luke 15:25: the elder son hears music and dancing at the prodigal's return — the type of eschatological celebration. David danced before the LORD (2 Sam 6:14); the church worships with the fullness of human expression before the one who is David's greater Son.
149.4: shadow For the LORD takes pleasure in his people; he adorns the humble with salvation. God adorning the humble with salvation — 1 Pet 5:5-6: God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. Humble yourselves, therefore, under the mighty hand of God so that at the proper time he may exalt you. The salvation that adorns the humble is the salvation Christ gives: Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... for I am gentle and lowly in heart (Matt 11:28-29).
149.5: shadow Let the godly exult in glory; let them sing for joy on their beds. Singing for joy on their beds — the joy that transcends circumstance. Acts 16:25: Paul and Silas singing hymns to God at midnight in prison, lying on their prison beds. Phil 4:4-7: joy and peace that surpass understanding, guarding the heart in Christ. The joy of Ps 149:5 is the joy no one can take away (John 16:22).
149.6: shadow Let the high praises of God be in their throats and two-edged swords in their hands, High praises and two-edged swords — the word of God as the weapon and the worship. Heb 4:12: the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword. Rev 1:16: from his mouth came a sharp two-edged sword — Christ himself is the word that cuts. Eph 6:17: the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God. Praise and the word are the weapons of the church.
149.7: shadow to execute vengeance on the nations and punishments on the peoples, Vengeance on the nations — the final judgment reserved for Christ. Rom 12:19: Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord. Rev 19:11-16: the rider on the white horse judges with justice and executes the vengeance of God on the nations. The saints of Ps 149 participate in this judgment: Rev 20:4: they came to life and reigned with Christ for a thousand years.
149.8: shadow to bind their kings with chains and their nobles with fetters of iron, Binding kings with chains — Rev 20:1-3: the angel seizing the dragon and bound him for a thousand years. Christ's victory at the cross is the binding of the strong man (Matt 12:29): how can someone enter a strong man's house and plunder his goods, unless he first binds the strong man? The chains of Ps 149:8 are the chains of the cross-achieved victory.
149.9: shadow to execute on them the judgment written — this is honor for all his godly ones. Praise the LORD! The judgment written executed — Rev 20:11-15: the dead judged by what was written in the books. The honor given to the godly who execute judgment is the honor of reigning with Christ: Rev 5:10: you have made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth. The closing Praise the LORD! is the church's response to its dignified role in the final vindication.

150.1: theme Praise the LORD! Praise God in his sanctuary; praise him in his mighty heavens! Praise in the sanctuary and mighty heavens — Heb 9:11-12: Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come... he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood. The heavenly sanctuary is entered by Christ; Heb 10:19-20 gives the church boldness to enter through the curtain — Christ's body. The praise of Ps 150:1 is offered in the true sanctuary Christ has opened.
150.2: shadow Praise him for his mighty deeds; praise him according to his excellent greatness! Mighty deeds and excellent greatness — the resurrection as the supreme mighty deed. Eph 1:19-20: the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly places. The excellent greatness of Ps 150:2 is the greatness displayed in the resurrection of Christ.
150.3: shadow Praise him with trumpet sound; praise him with lute and harp! Trumpet, lute, and harp — the instruments of the new creation worship. 1 Cor 15:52: at the last trumpet... the dead will be raised imperishable. Rev 8:2: the seven angels with seven trumpets. Rev 5:8: the elders with harps. The instrumental praise of Ps 150 is the rehearsal for the eschatological worship at the sound of the last trumpet and the new song of the Lamb.
150.4: shadow Praise him with tambourine and dance; praise him with strings and pipe! Tambourine, dance, strings, and pipe — the full-bodied, whole-person worship. Luke 15:25 — the music and dancing at the prodigal's return foreshadows the eschatological celebration at the ingathering of lost humanity. Zeph 3:17: The LORD your God is in your midst, a mighty one who will save; he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing.
150.5: shadow Praise him with sounding cymbals; praise him with loud clashing cymbals! Loud clashing cymbals — the overwhelming, full-volume praise of the redeemed. Rev 19:1-6: I heard what seemed to be the loud voice of a great multitude in heaven, crying out, Hallelujah!... like the roar of many waters and like the sound of mighty peals of thunder. The cymbals of Ps 150 are swallowed up in the thunderous Hallelujah of Revelation — the crescendo of all praise converging on the Lamb.
150.6: direct Let everything that has breath praise the LORD! Praise the LORD! Everything that has breath — Rev 5:13: every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea, and all that is in them, saying, To him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb be blessing and honor and glory and might forever and ever! The Psalter closes with this universal summons; Revelation shows its fulfillment — all breathing things praise the Father and the Lamb together, the final word of all Scripture's worship.

140.1: A type: the prayer of the righteous sufferer surrounded by violent enemies. Christ as the embodiment of this pattern — Luke 22:2 ("the chief priests and scribes were seeking how to put him to death"). He was delivered not from death but through it (Acts 2:24); the prayer for deliverance finds its ultimate answer in the resurrection, not the avoidance of suffering.
140.2: A type: "who plan evil things in their heart and stir up wars continually." The conspiracy against Christ. Luke 22:3-6 — Satan entered Judas and the chief priests paid thirty pieces of silver. Acts 4:27-28 — "both Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel, gathered together against your holy servant Jesus." The enemies' plans were overruled by God's definite plan (Acts 2:23).
140.3: A theme: "under their lips is the venom of asps." Paul quotes this in Rom 3:13 as part of the universal portrait of human sin — "their throat is an open grave; they use their tongues to deceive." Christ bore the full weight of this sinful speech at his trial and crucifixion. He is the truth (John 14:6) that unmasks the venom; he bears its sting without becoming venomous (1 Pet 2:23).
140.4: A type: the prayer for protection from the hands of the wicked. In Gethsemane, Christ prayed for the Father's will (Luke 22:42) — he was not shielded from suffering but guarded through it. His protection of his own: John 17:11-12 — "keep them in your name... I have kept them, and not one of them has been lost." The one who prays for protection becomes the protector of all who are his.
140.5: A type: "arrogant men have hidden a trap for me."
